title: Phase 3 AXIOM-LC Trial Shows Tocilizumab Subcutaneous Injection Reduces Long COVID Joint Pain and Inflammation by 43% in Patients With Elevated IL-6
excerpt: The AXIOM-LC trial demonstrated that weekly subcutaneous tocilizumab significantly reduced joint symptoms and systemic inflammation in long COVID patients with persistently elevated interleukin-6 levels over 24 weeks.
body:
Results from the Phase 3 AXIOM-LC trial, published this week in The Lancet Rheumatology, show that weekly subcutaneous tocilizumab reduced joint pain and inflammatory symptoms by 43% in long COVID patients with elevated IL-6, compared to 11% in the placebo arm. The multicenter, double-blind trial enrolled 684 patients across 52 sites in North America and Europe who had persistent arthralgia, morning stiffness, and IL-6 levels above 5 pg/mL for at least six months following acute SARS-CoV-2 infection.

Participants received either 162 mg subcutaneous tocilizumab weekly or matched placebo for 24 weeks. The primary composite endpoint combined patient-reported joint pain (measured by a modified DAS-28 adapted for post-viral syndromes), C-reactive protein normalization, and functional capacity assessed by the HAQ-DI. Secondary endpoints included fatigue, sleep quality, and post-exertional symptom burden.

At week 24, 61% of tocilizumab recipients achieved the primary composite response versus 18% on placebo. CRP normalized in 74% of the treatment group. Notably, patients also reported meaningful improvements in fatigue scores, suggesting IL-6 blockade addresses inflammatory drivers beyond joint disease. Adverse events mirrored the established tocilizumab safety profile, with elevated liver enzymes in 8% and neutropenia in 4%; two serious infections occurred in the treatment arm versus one in placebo.

Biomarker-Guided Enrollment Proved Decisive

Lead investigator Dr. Rachel Ostrow of Brigham and Women's Hospital emphasized that the trial's success hinged on biomarker-based patient selection. Earlier attempts to repurpose IL-6 inhibitors for undifferentiated long COVID populations had shown inconsistent results. By restricting enrollment to patients with laboratory-confirmed IL-6 elevation, AXIOM-LC identified a responder phenotype that has proven elusive in broader post-acute sequelae research.

Roche has submitted a supplemental Biologics License Application to the FDA, with priority review expected given the drug's existing safety database from rheumatoid arthritis and cytokine release syndrome indications. If approved, tocilizumab would become the fifth immunomodulatory therapy authorized for a specific long COVID subtype within the past 18 months, joining daratumumab, isatuximab, rituximab, and efgartigimod in an expanding phenotype-directed armamentarium.

Implications for Precision Rheumatology in Post-Viral Disease

The AXIOM-LC results provide the strongest evidence to date that a subset of long COVID represents a durable IL-6-driven inflammatory state amenable to targeted cytokine blockade. Investigators are now planning a follow-on trial combining tocilizumab with low-dose methotrexate in patients showing incomplete response, modeled on rheumatoid arthritis treat-to-target strategies.

Why this matters: AXIOM-LC establishes IL-6 as a validated therapeutic target in a defined long COVID subpopulation and reinforces the shift toward biomarker-stratified treatment that is transforming long COVID from a syndrome of exclusion into a set of mechanistically distinct, treatable diseases.

title: First Circuit Rules Universities Must Provide Asynchronous Learning Options for Graduate Students With Long COVID Under Section 504
excerpt: The First Circuit held that federally funded universities must offer asynchronous learning accommodations to graduate students with long COVID, rejecting arguments that synchronous seminar attendance is an essential academic requirement.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the First Circuit ruled 2-1 yesterday in Chen v. Trustees of Northeastern University that graduate institutions receiving federal funds must provide asynchronous participation options to students with long COVID whose cognitive and autonomic symptoms preclude reliable synchronous attendance. The decision reverses a district court ruling that had characterized live seminar participation as a non-negotiable essential function of doctoral education.

Plaintiff Wei Chen, a fourth-year PhD candidate in molecular biology, developed long COVID in 2023 following a workplace exposure. Her symptoms include post-exertional cognitive crashes, orthostatic intolerance, and unpredictable fatigue that made scheduled afternoon seminars impossible to attend reliably. After the university denied her request for recorded lectures with asynchronous discussion board participation, citing the pedagogical importance of real-time dialogue, Chen filed suit under Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act.

Court Rejects 'Essential Function' Defense

Writing for the majority, Judge Kayatta held that universities bear a substantial burden to demonstrate that synchronous participation is truly essential rather than merely traditional. The court found that Northeastern had already offered asynchronous options during the pandemic and to students with scheduling conflicts related to teaching assistantships, undercutting its claim that live attendance was pedagogically indispensable.

The opinion draws heavily on the Supreme Court's recent Henderson decision recognizing long COVID brain fog as a cognitive disability, and extends its reasoning into the academic accommodation context. Judge Kayatta wrote that 'the Rehabilitation Act does not permit institutions to reserve their most rigorous programs for students whose disabilities happen to be compatible with pre-pandemic instructional formats.'

The dissent, authored by Judge Barron, warned that the ruling could compel universities to fundamentally restructure programs like clinical training and laboratory rotations where physical presence is genuinely required. The majority responded that its holding is limited to didactic components and does not require accommodations that would eliminate genuinely essential experiential requirements.

Broader Impact on Higher Education

The ruling applies to universities in Maine, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, Puerto Rico, and Rhode Island, but is expected to influence pending cases in the Second, Third, and Ninth Circuits. The Department of Education's Office for Civil Rights has already cited the decision in guidance to institutions handling long COVID accommodation requests, and disability rights advocates estimate that between 180,000 and 240,000 U.S. graduate students currently meet criteria for long COVID.

Northeastern has not announced whether it will seek en banc review or Supreme Court certiorari. The university now faces remand proceedings to determine damages and to implement asynchronous options for Chen's remaining coursework and qualifying examinations.

Why this matters: The decision establishes that academic tradition cannot serve as a categorical shield against disability accommodation, opening advanced degree programs to a generation of scholars whose long COVID would otherwise force them to abandon their education.

title: FDA Approves Nebulized Deupirfenidone for Long COVID Patients With Bronchiolar Fibrosis Documented on Hyperpolarized Xenon MRI
excerpt: The FDA has approved nebulized deupirfenidone as the first inhaled antifibrotic therapy for long COVID patients with small airway fibrosis visualized on hyperpolarized xenon-129 MRI, offering targeted lung delivery with reduced systemic toxicity.
body:
The Food and Drug Administration yesterday approved nebulized deupirfenidone (marketed as Pulmavex) for adults with long COVID whose small airway fibrosis has been confirmed by hyperpolarized xenon-129 MRI. The approval is the first for an inhaled antifibrotic in any post-viral lung disease indication and represents a significant refinement of the systemic pirfenidone strategy validated in last year's AURORA-LC trial.

Deupirfenidone incorporates deuterium substitutions that slow hepatic metabolism, allowing effective lung concentrations at doses roughly one-tenth those required for oral pirfenidone. Delivered via a proprietary vibrating mesh nebulizer twice daily, the drug achieved primary and secondary endpoints in the Phase 3 BREATHE-LC trial, which enrolled 512 patients with imaging-confirmed bronchiolar fibrosis and forced expiratory volume decline of at least 8% from pre-infection baseline.

Trial Data and Imaging Integration

Over 48 weeks, patients receiving nebulized deupirfenidone experienced a 58% reduction in FEV1 decline compared to placebo, along with meaningful improvements in six-minute walk distance and King's Brief Interstitial Lung Disease scores. Hyperpolarized xenon MRI, which measures gas exchange and ventilation at the alveolar level, showed stabilization or improvement in ventilation defect percentage in 71% of treated patients versus 22% on placebo.

Gastrointestinal side effects and photosensitivity, which limit oral pirfenidone tolerability, occurred in less than 5% of the nebulized cohort. The most common adverse events were cough during administration and transient dysgeusia. Two cases of nebulizer-associated bronchospasm led to discontinuation.

The FDA label specifically requires hyperpolarized xenon MRI or equivalent functional imaging for treatment initiation, cementing the emerging role of advanced pulmonary imaging in long COVID diagnostic workups. Approximately 140 U.S. centers now offer xenon-129 MRI, and CMS issued a preliminary coverage determination extending Medicare reimbursement for the imaging when used for deupirfenidone eligibility assessment.

Access and Distribution Challenges

Manufacturer Genentech announced a patient assistance program capping monthly out-of-pocket costs at $50 for commercially insured patients and providing free drug to uninsured individuals below 500% of the federal poverty line. List price is $8,400 per month, comparable to oral pirfenidone for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.

Pulmonologists have raised concerns about the geographic concentration of xenon MRI capability, which is largely limited to academic centers. The American Thoracic Society is working with the FDA to establish a validation pathway for alternative diagnostic approaches, including oscillometry combined with high-resolution CT, that might expand access in community settings.

Why this matters: Pulmavex introduces organ-targeted antifibrotic therapy for a distinct long COVID pulmonary phenotype and further establishes advanced functional imaging as the gateway to precision treatment of post-COVID lung disease.

title: Phase 3 AURORA-LC Trial Shows Pirfenidone Reduces Pulmonary Fibrosis Progression in Long COVID Patients by 51% Over 52 Weeks
excerpt: The antifibrotic agent pirfenidone significantly slowed the progression of post-COVID pulmonary fibrosis in patients with persistent radiographic and physiologic abnormalities, according to results published this week in The Lancet Respiratory Medicine.
body:
Pirfenidone, an antifibrotic medication currently approved for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, reduced the progression of post-COVID interstitial lung disease by 51% compared to placebo in the Phase 3 AURORA-LC trial, according to results published Monday in The Lancet Respiratory Medicine. The findings represent the first large-scale positive trial for a targeted therapy addressing the fibrotic phenotype of long COVID.

The multinational trial enrolled 842 patients across 74 sites in North America, Europe, and Japan who had persistent dyspnea, reduced diffusing capacity (DLCO below 70% predicted), and evidence of reticulation or ground-glass opacities on high-resolution CT at least six months after acute SARS-CoV-2 infection. Participants were randomized to receive pirfenidone 2,403 mg daily or matched placebo for 52 weeks, with the primary endpoint being change in forced vital capacity (FVC).

Key Findings

Patients receiving pirfenidone experienced a mean FVC decline of 42 mL over 52 weeks compared to 156 mL in the placebo group, a 51% relative reduction in disease progression. Secondary endpoints also favored the treatment arm: six-minute walk distance improved by an average of 34 meters in the pirfenidone group versus a decline of 12 meters in placebo recipients, and the King's Brief Interstitial Lung Disease questionnaire showed clinically meaningful improvement in 47% of treated patients versus 21% of controls.

Radiographic analysis using automated quantitative CT scoring showed a 28% reduction in fibrotic burden progression, with the most pronounced benefit seen in patients whose baseline scans demonstrated a usual interstitial pneumonia-like pattern. Serum biomarkers including KL-6 and surfactant protein D declined significantly in the treatment arm, suggesting reduced alveolar epithelial injury.

Safety and Tolerability

Adverse events were consistent with pirfenidone's known safety profile. Gastrointestinal side effects occurred in 38% of treated patients, and photosensitivity reactions in 14%, leading to discontinuation in 11% of the pirfenidone arm compared to 4% of placebo recipients. No new safety signals emerged, and hepatic transaminase elevations were manageable with dose adjustments.

Principal investigator Dr. Ayodele Okafor of the University of Toronto emphasized that the trial identifies a treatable subgroup within the heterogeneous long COVID population. "Roughly 8 to 12% of patients with persistent respiratory long COVID show evidence of established or progressive fibrosis on imaging," Okafor said. "For these patients, we now have a therapy that alters the disease trajectory rather than merely managing symptoms."

The manufacturer has submitted a supplemental New Drug Application to the FDA seeking an expanded indication, with a decision expected in the first quarter of 2027. Several major payers have already signaled willingness to cover off-label use for patients meeting the trial's radiographic and physiologic criteria.

Why this matters: AURORA-LC provides the first proven disease-modifying therapy for the fibrotic subtype of long COVID, addressing an irreversible complication that has left tens of thousands of patients on supplemental oxygen with no prior treatment options.

title: First Circuit Rules Municipal Employers Must Provide Air Filtration and Masking Accommodations to Long COVID Workers Under ADA Title II
excerpt: In a landmark decision, the First Circuit held that public employers must implement environmental infection control measures as reasonable accommodations for employees with long COVID at heightened risk from reinfection.
body:
The First Circuit Court of Appeals ruled unanimously Tuesday in Delacroix v. City of Providence that municipal employers must provide HEPA air filtration, upgraded ventilation, and mask-wearing requirements for coworkers as reasonable accommodations under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act when requested by employees with long COVID whose conditions are exacerbated by SARS-CoV-2 reinfection.

The case was brought by Marie Delacroix, a Providence public library archivist who developed severe long COVID in 2022 and experienced substantial symptom relapses following two subsequent SARS-CoV-2 infections contracted at work. After her physician documented that reinfection posed a substantial risk of permanent deterioration, Delacroix requested that her department install portable HEPA units, upgrade to MERV-13 filtration in HVAC systems serving her workspace, and require masking during periods of elevated community transmission. The city denied all three requests as "unreasonable" and "beyond the scope" of individualized accommodation.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for a unanimous three-judge panel, Judge Patricia Reyes rejected the city's argument that infection control measures affecting multiple employees fall outside the ADA's individualized accommodation framework. "The statute requires reasonable modifications to policies, practices, and procedures," Reyes wrote. "An employer's decision about air handling and communicable disease protocols is precisely such a policy, and modifying it to protect a qualified individual with a disability is not categorically different from installing a wheelchair ramp or restructuring a work schedule."

The panel emphasized that long COVID's recognized status as a disability under HHS and EEOC guidance, combined with substantial scientific evidence that reinfection worsens symptoms in a significant fraction of patients, made the requested accommodations both medically necessary and administratively feasible. The court noted that the CDC and ASHRAE have published clear technical standards for the requested measures.

Scope and Limitations

The court remanded the case for a determination of whether specific accommodations would impose undue hardship on the city, but established that cost alone cannot defeat requests supported by industry-standard engineering guidance. Importantly, the ruling recognized that masking requirements for coworkers may be reasonable when limited to defined periods, defined spaces, or defined activities, rejecting the argument that any burden on third parties automatically renders an accommodation unreasonable.

Disability rights advocates called the decision the most significant workplace long COVID ruling since the EEOC's 2025 presumptive disability guidance. The Job Accommodation Network estimates that approximately 340,000 U.S. workers with long COVID have documented heightened vulnerability to reinfection-driven relapse.

The city has not indicated whether it will seek en banc review or petition for certiorari. Similar cases are pending in the Fourth and Ninth Circuits.

Why this matters: The ruling establishes that infection control accommodations are legally required for vulnerable workers, potentially transforming workplace air quality standards for millions of Americans with long COVID and other immune-compromising conditions.

title: FDA Approves Sipavibart Long-Acting Monoclonal Antibody for Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis in Long COVID Patients With Reinfection-Triggered Relapse
excerpt: The FDA has approved AstraZeneca's sipavibart for pre-exposure prophylaxis specifically in long COVID patients with documented history of symptom exacerbation following reinfection, marking the first prophylactic authorization targeting this population.
body:
The Food and Drug Administration on Wednesday approved sipavibart (marketed as Kavigale) for pre-exposure prophylaxis of SARS-CoV-2 infection in adults with long COVID who have experienced clinically significant symptom worsening following one or more prior reinfections. The approval represents the first regulatory authorization of a preventive antibody therapy specifically indicated for the long COVID population.

Sipavibart is a long-acting monoclonal antibody engineered with an extended half-life and preserved neutralizing activity against currently circulating Omicron-descendant variants, including KP.3.1.1 and XEC lineages. The FDA action follows updated in vitro neutralization data submitted this spring showing retained potency against variants representing more than 92% of currently sequenced U.S. isolates.

Trial Evidence

Approval was based on the SUPERNOVA-LC extension study, which enrolled 1,247 long COVID patients with a documented history of symptom relapse following prior SARS-CoV-2 reinfection. Participants received either sipavibart 300 mg intramuscularly every six months or placebo for 18 months. The primary endpoint of symptomatic laboratory-confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection occurred in 4.1% of sipavibart recipients versus 18.7% of placebo recipients, a relative risk reduction of 78%.

Critically for the target population, the trial also demonstrated a 64% reduction in long COVID symptom exacerbation events, defined as sustained increase of at least 20 points on the Post-COVID Functional Status Scale lasting more than four weeks. Patient-reported outcomes on fatigue, cognitive function, and post-exertional malaise favored the treatment arm across all prespecified subgroups.

Access and Implementation

AstraZeneca has priced sipavibart at $3,200 per dose, with twice-yearly administration recommended. CMS issued a preliminary coverage decision Wednesday afternoon indicating that Medicare Part B will cover the drug under existing preventive services authority when prescribed by clinicians affiliated with designated long COVID centers of excellence. Major commercial insurers including UnitedHealthcare, Elevance, and CVS Health/Aetna have announced coverage effective within 30 days, though prior authorization requirements will apply.

The label specifies that eligible patients must have (1) a clinical diagnosis of long COVID meeting WHO or NASEM criteria, (2) documented symptom relapse following at least one prior reinfection, and (3) contraindication or inadequate response to vaccination. The FDA estimates approximately 2.1 million U.S. adults with long COVID meet these criteria.

Dr. Katherine Ozawa of Johns Hopkins, who chaired the FDA advisory committee that unanimously recommended approval in May, said the drug addresses a critical unmet need. "For patients whose long COVID has worsened with each reinfection, prophylaxis is not optional — it is the difference between stability and progressive decline," Ozawa said.

Why this matters: Sipavibart provides the first proven protective option for millions of long COVID patients whose condition deteriorates with every SARS-CoV-2 exposure, potentially preventing hundreds of thousands of relapses annually.

title: Phase 3 MOSAIC-LC Trial Shows Daratumumab Reduces Long COVID Symptoms by 47% in Patients With Persistent Plasmablast Expansion
excerpt: The MOSAIC-LC trial published in The Lancet demonstrated that the anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody daratumumab produced substantial symptom reduction in long COVID patients with expanded plasmablast populations, offering a new B-cell-directed treatment pathway distinct from rituximab.
body:
The Phase 3 MOSAIC-LC trial published this week in The Lancet demonstrated that subcutaneous daratumumab reduced composite long COVID symptom scores by 47% over 24 weeks in patients with persistent plasmablast expansion, meeting all primary and secondary endpoints. The multicenter international trial enrolled 612 patients across 48 sites in North America, Europe, and Australia who had documented long COVID symptoms for at least six months and elevated circulating plasmablasts above 2% of total B cells.

Daratumumab, an anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody currently approved for multiple myeloma, depletes plasmablasts and plasma cells that produce pathogenic antibodies, including the anti-GPCR autoantibodies implicated in dysautonomia and microvascular dysfunction. Unlike rituximab, which spares CD20-negative plasmablasts, daratumumab targets the antibody-producing compartment directly. Patients received weekly subcutaneous injections for eight weeks followed by monthly maintenance dosing.

Trial Results and Biomarker Findings

The primary endpoint, a composite score encompassing fatigue, post-exertional malaise, orthostatic intolerance, and cognitive dysfunction, improved by 47% in the daratumumab arm versus 12% with placebo. Secondary endpoints showed a 52% reduction in anti-beta-2 adrenergic receptor autoantibody titers, 44% improvement in flow-mediated dilation, and 39% improvement in six-minute walk distance. Notably, 34% of treated patients achieved sustained symptom remission at 48-week follow-up, defined as maintaining at least 50% symptom reduction six months after final dosing.

Adverse events were manageable, with injection site reactions occurring in 28% of patients and grade 3 or higher infections in 6%. Investigators recommended pneumococcal and shingles vaccination prior to initiation and monitoring for hypogammaglobulinemia during maintenance therapy. Three patients required treatment interruption for pneumonia; all recovered without sequelae.

Positioning Within Emerging Immunotherapy Landscape

MOSAIC-LC principal investigator Dr. Akiko Tanaka of Johns Hopkins noted that the trial specifically enrolled patients with a plasmablast-driven phenotype identified through peripheral blood immunophenotyping, representing approximately 22% of long COVID patients screened. This aligns with growing evidence that long COVID comprises distinct immunological endotypes requiring targeted rather than universal treatment approaches.

The FDA granted daratumumab breakthrough therapy designation for this indication in April 2026, and Janssen has filed a supplemental biologics license application with expected action by first quarter 2027. Insurance coverage remains uncertain given the drug's estimated $85,000 annual cost, though patient advocacy groups are pressing CMS to include daratumumab in its emerging long COVID coverage framework.

Why this matters: MOSAIC-LC provides the strongest evidence to date that plasmablast-directed therapy can produce durable remission in a defined subset of long COVID patients, reinforcing the shift toward biomarker-guided precision immunotherapy.

title: Supreme Court Rules 6-3 in Whitfield v. Prudential That Insurers Must Consider Composite Functional Evidence When Denying Long COVID Disability Claims
excerpt: In a landmark decision issued this week, the Supreme Court held that ERISA disability insurers cannot rely on isolated normal test results to deny long COVID claims when composite functional evidence documents impairment.
body:
The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 in Whitfield v. Prudential Insurance Company of America that ERISA-governed disability insurers must consider the totality of functional evidence, including patient-reported outcomes, wearable device data, and multi-day activity monitoring, when adjudicating long COVID disability claims. The decision, authored by Justice Barrett with Chief Justice Roberts and Justices Sotomayor, Kagan, Kavanaugh, and Jackson joining, reverses a Third Circuit ruling that had upheld Prudential's denial based on a single normal cardiac stress test.

The case involved Marcus Whitfield, a former commercial airline pilot who developed long COVID after a 2023 infection and was denied long-term disability benefits despite documented post-exertional malaise, orthostatic intolerance, and cognitive dysfunction. Prudential relied on a single-day cardiopulmonary exercise test showing preserved peak VO2 to conclude Whitfield could perform sedentary work, dismissing wearable heart rate variability data, two-day CPET results, and neuropsychological findings.

The Court's Analysis

Writing for the majority, Justice Barrett emphasized that long COVID's episodic and exertion-triggered nature renders single-point testing inherently unreliable as evidence of functional capacity. "An insurer that ignores composite functional evidence in favor of a snapshot measurement that cannot capture the condition's defining features acts arbitrarily and capriciously as a matter of law under ERISA," Barrett wrote. The opinion drew on the Court's prior Henderson v. Acme Logistics reasoning while extending it from ADA to ERISA contexts.

The decision requires insurers to articulate specific reasons for discounting patient-reported symptoms, wearable-derived physiological data, and multi-day functional assessments. Insurers may still deny claims, but must engage substantively with the full evidentiary record. The ruling also endorses the two-day CPET methodology previously accepted by the D.C. Circuit for Social Security claims.

Dissent and Industry Response

Justice Alito dissented, joined by Justices Thomas and Gorsuch, arguing the majority "transforms ERISA's deferential standard into a plaintiff-friendly rule that will invite protracted litigation." The dissent expressed concern about insurer reliance on subjective symptom reporting.

The American Council of Life Insurers estimated the ruling could affect adjudication of approximately 340,000 pending and future long COVID disability claims. Prudential, MetLife, Unum, and Cigna have already issued internal guidance directing claims adjusters to document engagement with all submitted functional evidence. The Department of Labor announced it will issue conforming regulatory guidance under ERISA Section 503 within 90 days.

Plaintiff attorneys anticipate reopening of thousands of previously denied claims where insurers relied on isolated normal testing, though the retroactive reach remains subject to plan-specific limitations provisions.

Why this matters: Whitfield establishes a nationwide evidentiary standard that finally aligns long COVID disability adjudication with the clinical reality of the condition, potentially unlocking benefits for hundreds of thousands of disabled workers.

title: HHS Launches $1.8 Billion National Long COVID Biobank and Multi-Omics Data Commons at 24 Academic Medical Centers
excerpt: The Department of Health and Human Services announced a five-year initiative establishing the first federally coordinated long COVID biobank and multi-omics data commons, aiming to accelerate biomarker validation and endotype-targeted therapeutics.
body:
The Department of Health and Human Services announced a $1.8 billion, five-year initiative this week establishing the National Long COVID Biobank and Multi-Omics Data Commons at 24 academic medical centers, creating the first federally coordinated infrastructure for standardized biospecimen collection and integrated multi-omics analysis in long COVID research. The initiative will collect samples from 60,000 patients over five years, with matched controls from acute COVID recoverers and uninfected individuals.

The biobank will store whole blood, serum, plasma, PBMCs, stool, urine, cerebrospinal fluid subsets, and skin biopsies for small fiber neuropathy assessment. Each patient will undergo deep phenotyping including standardized symptom assessment, autonomic testing, cognitive evaluation, cardiopulmonary exercise testing, and continuous wearable monitoring. The data commons will integrate genomic, transcriptomic, proteomic, metabolomic, lipidomic, and microbiome data with clinical phenotypes and longitudinal outcomes.

Consortium Structure and Access Framework

The 24 participating centers include Mount Sinai, Johns Hopkins, Stanford, UCSF, Mayo Clinic, Duke, University of Washington, Vanderbilt, Yale, and 15 others distributed across all HHS regions. Coordination will be managed jointly by NIH's National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences and a newly created HHS Office of Long COVID Research Coordination. The Broad Institute will serve as the central multi-omics processing hub, while Vanderbilt will host the informatics platform.

Data will be released to qualified investigators through a tiered access system modeled on the All of Us Research Program. Researchers from academic, government, and industry sectors will be eligible following institutional certification and data use agreements. Aggregate summary data will be publicly accessible; individual-level data will require controlled access approval. The initiative mandates return of clinically actionable findings to participants and requires 40% enrollment from historically underrepresented populations.

Scientific and Policy Rationale

HHS Assistant Secretary for Health Dr. Rachel Levine described the initiative as filling a critical gap left by fragmented research efforts. "Despite significant investment through RECOVER and other programs, we have lacked a unified biospecimen and data resource of sufficient scale to definitively resolve long COVID endotypes and validate biomarkers for regulatory decision-making," Levine said at the announcement.

The FDA has committed to accepting biobank-derived biomarker data for drug development qualification submissions, potentially accelerating approvals for endotype-targeted therapies. Pharmaceutical companies including Pfizer, AstraZeneca, Regeneron, and Vertex have signed pre-competitive collaboration agreements to contribute proprietary assay methods in exchange for early access to de-identified data.

Patient advocacy organizations praised the initiative but emphasized the need for meaningful patient governance representation. The steering committee will include six patient representatives with voting authority on research priorities and data access policies.

Why this matters: A unified national biobank at this scale finally provides the statistical power and standardization required to definitively map long COVID endotypes and validate the biomarkers necessary for precision therapeutics to reach patients.

title: Phase 3 BEACON-LC Trial Shows Nirmatrelvir-Ritonavir 15-Day Extended Course Reduces Long COVID Symptoms by 36% in Patients With Persistent Nucleocapsid Antigenemia
excerpt: An extended 15-day course of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir produced significant symptom reduction in long COVID patients with detectable circulating SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid antigen, according to results from the Phase 3 BEACON-LC trial published this week.
body:
An extended 15-day course of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir produced a 36% reduction in long COVID symptom burden in patients with detectable circulating SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid antigen, according to the Phase 3 BEACON-LC trial published in The New England Journal of Medicine this week. The results provide the strongest randomized evidence to date that a persistent viral reservoir drives symptoms in a defined subset of patients and that antigen testing can identify who will respond to antiviral therapy.

BEACON-LC enrolled 1,148 adults with long COVID symptoms lasting at least six months and detectable plasma nucleocapsid antigen on the Quanterix Simoa assay. Participants were randomized to 15 days of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir 300 mg/100 mg twice daily or matched placebo, with a primary endpoint of change in the PROMIS-29 physical function score at week 12. Investigators at 62 sites across North America and Europe stratified enrollment by antigen concentration and duration of illness.

Results and Antigen Clearance

Treated patients experienced a 36% reduction in composite symptom burden compared with a 9% reduction in the placebo arm (p<0.001). Fatigue, post-exertional malaise, and cognitive symptoms all improved, with the largest effect sizes seen in patients whose baseline antigen levels exceeded 10 pg/mL. Fifty-eight percent of treated patients achieved undetectable antigen at week 12, versus 11% of controls, and antigen clearance strongly correlated with symptom improvement. A prespecified subgroup analysis showed no benefit in patients without detectable baseline antigen, reinforcing the biomarker-driven approach.

Safety was consistent with prior nirmatrelvir-ritonavir experience. Dysgeusia occurred in 31% of treated patients, and 4% discontinued for adverse events. No serious drug-drug interactions were reported, though 12% of participants required temporary adjustment of concomitant statins or calcium channel blockers.

Regulatory and Clinical Implications

Pfizer confirmed it will submit a supplemental new drug application to the FDA within 60 days seeking a long COVID indication limited to antigen-positive patients. Several long COVID clinics have already begun offering off-label extended courses; BEACON-LC investigators cautioned that antigen testing should precede treatment, both to identify likely responders and to conserve a supply-constrained medication.

The trial builds on smaller pilot studies including STOP-PASC and PAX-LC, which showed inconsistent results in unselected populations. BEACON-LC's biomarker enrichment strategy appears to explain the divergence, and editorialists called the design a template for future long COVID antiviral trials.

Why this matters: BEACON-LC establishes viral persistence as a treatable driver of long COVID in a biomarker-defined subgroup, opening a path to targeted antiviral therapy for potentially millions of patients with detectable antigenemia.

title: Supreme Court Grants Certiorari in Ramirez v. Aetna to Decide Whether ERISA Preempts State Laws Mandating Coverage of Long COVID Rehabilitation Services
excerpt: The Supreme Court has agreed to hear Ramirez v. Aetna, a case that will determine whether ERISA preempts a growing patchwork of state laws requiring self-funded employer health plans to cover multidisciplinary long COVID rehabilitation.
body:
The Supreme Court granted certiorari on June 29 in Ramirez v. Aetna Life Insurance Co., agreeing to decide whether the Employee Retirement Income Security Act preempts state laws that mandate coverage of long COVID rehabilitation services by self-funded employer health plans. The case reaches the Court after a split among the circuits and carries implications for coverage of an estimated 14 million Americans with long COVID enrolled in self-funded plans.

At issue is California's Long COVID Care Access Act of 2025, which requires all health plans doing business in the state to cover cardiopulmonary rehabilitation, cognitive rehabilitation, autonomic rehabilitation, and coordinated multidisciplinary care for patients meeting WHO ICD-11 diagnostic criteria. Twelve other states have enacted similar statutes. Aetna, administering a self-funded plan for a large logistics employer, denied coverage for a 24-week rehabilitation program prescribed for plaintiff Elena Ramirez, arguing the California mandate was preempted under ERISA §514.

Circuit Split

The Ninth Circuit ruled 2-1 in October 2025 that the California law survived preemption as an insurance regulation saved under ERISA's savings clause, even as applied indirectly to self-funded plans through their stop-loss carriers. The Fifth and Eighth Circuits reached the opposite conclusion in cases involving Texas and Missouri laws, holding that mandated benefit statutes are classically preempted when applied to self-funded arrangements. The Third Circuit produced a middle position, allowing state enforcement against third-party administrators but not against plans themselves.

The Solicitor General filed a brief urging the Court to take the case and siding with the Ninth Circuit's approach, arguing that long COVID rehabilitation constitutes essential health care and that ERISA's saving clause should be read to permit state coverage floors for post-viral conditions.

Stakes for Patients and Employers

Amicus briefs have poured in from patient advocacy groups, state attorneys general, the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, and the ERISA Industry Committee. Patient groups estimate that a ruling against the state laws would strip coverage from between 8 and 11 million long COVID patients enrolled in self-funded plans, which represent roughly 65% of the employer-sponsored market. Employer groups counter that state-by-state mandates create unworkable compliance burdens and undermine ERISA's uniformity principle.

The Court is expected to hear argument in the October 2026 term, with a decision by June 2027. Justices Sotomayor and Jackson have previously signaled skepticism of broad ERISA preemption in health coverage contexts, while the Chief Justice authored the Court's most recent restrictive preemption opinion.

Why this matters: The Court's decision will determine whether states can effectively require long COVID care coverage for the majority of American workers, or whether ERISA preemption will leave that coverage to the discretion of individual employers.

title: FDA Approves Ensifentrine Inhalation Solution for Long COVID Patients With Persistent Small Airway Dysfunction Documented by Oscillometry
excerpt: The FDA has approved ensifentrine inhalation solution as the first therapy specifically indicated for long COVID patients with small airway dysfunction, based on Phase 3 data showing significant improvements in respiratory symptoms and airway resistance.
body:
The FDA approved ensifentrine inhalation solution on June 25 as the first therapy specifically indicated for long COVID patients with small airway dysfunction confirmed by impulse oscillometry. The approval is based on the Phase 3 BREATHE-LC trial, which demonstrated significant improvements in respiratory symptoms, small airway function, and exercise capacity over 24 weeks in a population with normal spirometry but abnormal oscillometry findings.

Ensifentrine, a dual phosphodiesterase 3 and 4 inhibitor previously approved for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, targets both bronchodilation and anti-inflammatory pathways relevant to the small airway inflammation increasingly recognized in long COVID. The BREATHE-LC trial enrolled 892 patients with persistent dyspnea for at least six months, normal spirometry (FEV1/FVC >0.70), and elevated R5-R20 on oscillometry indicating peripheral airway obstruction.

Trial Results

Patients receiving ensifentrine 3 mg twice daily via nebulizer experienced a 42% reduction in dyspnea as measured by the modified Medical Research Council scale, compared with 14% in the placebo arm. R5-R20 values, a measure of small airway resistance, improved by 38% in treated patients. Six-minute walk distance increased by an average of 74 meters, and patient-reported cough frequency declined by 51%.

Subgroup analyses showed the largest benefits in patients with baseline R5-R20 above 0.10 kPa·L⁻¹·s and in those with post-acute imaging findings suggestive of small airway disease on expiratory CT. Adverse events were mild, with headache and nausea occurring more frequently in treated patients but no significant cardiovascular signals. Only 3% of patients discontinued treatment for adverse events.

Diagnostic Pathway and Access

The FDA label requires oscillometry documentation of small airway dysfunction prior to prescription, reflecting growing recognition that many long COVID patients with respiratory complaints have normal spirometry but abnormal peripheral airway function. CMS issued a companion coverage determination on June 30 covering both the medication and the oscillometry testing needed to qualify patients for treatment. Verona Pharma, the manufacturer, priced the therapy at $2,400 per month and committed to a patient assistance program capping out-of-pocket costs at $50 per month for insured patients earning under $150,000.

The approval expands the small but growing set of long COVID indications with specific diagnostic biomarkers, following a pattern established by recent approvals of larazotide acetate for spike antigenemia-positive patients and ampligen for those with impaired natural killer cell function. Pulmonologists specializing in post-COVID care welcomed the approval, noting that small airway disease has been an underrecognized cause of persistent dyspnea in patients previously told their tests were normal.

Why this matters: Ensifentrine's approval validates small airway dysfunction as a distinct treatable phenotype of long COVID and provides the first targeted therapy for the substantial subset of patients whose breathlessness has been dismissed because their spirometry appears normal.

title: Phase 3 SENTINEL-LC Trial Shows Maraviroc Monotherapy Reduces Long COVID Neuroinflammation by 39% in Patients With Elevated CCR5 Expression on CD8 T Cells
excerpt: The Phase 3 SENTINEL-LC trial published in The Lancet found that the CCR5 antagonist maraviroc reduced neuroinflammatory symptoms in long COVID patients with elevated CCR5+ CD8 T cell populations by 39% over 20 weeks. The findings represent the first large-scale validation of chemokine receptor blockade as monotherapy for a defined immunological subtype of long COVID.
body:
The Phase 3 SENTINEL-LC trial, published this week in The Lancet, found that the CCR5 antagonist maraviroc reduced neuroinflammatory symptoms in long COVID patients with elevated CCR5+ CD8 T cell populations by 39% over 20 weeks compared with placebo. The trial enrolled 612 patients across 44 sites in North America and Europe and represents the first Phase 3 validation of chemokine receptor blockade as monotherapy for an immunologically defined long COVID subtype.

Participants were selected based on flow cytometry showing CCR5 expression on more than 35% of circulating CD8 T cells, a phenotype previously associated with tissue-infiltrating cytotoxic lymphocytes in autopsy and biopsy studies of long COVID patients with neurological symptoms. Patients received either 300 mg oral maraviroc twice daily or matched placebo for 20 weeks, with the primary endpoint being change in a composite neuroinflammation symptom score capturing headache, cognitive slowing, sensory hypersensitivity, and sleep disturbance.

The maraviroc arm showed a 39% mean reduction on the composite score, compared with 11% in the placebo arm (p<0.001). Secondary endpoints included cerebrospinal fluid neopterin and CXCL10 levels in a subset of 148 patients; both declined significantly in treated participants, with neopterin dropping by a mean of 31% and CXCL10 by 28%. Functional neurocognitive testing showed corresponding improvements in processing speed and sustained attention. Adverse events were consistent with the drug's established HIV safety profile, with transaminase elevations in 4.1% of treated patients, all reversible.

Building on Antiretroviral Repurposing

Maraviroc's rationale in long COVID emerged from observations that SARS-CoV-2 upregulates CCR5 signaling and that persistent CCR5-driven trafficking of activated T cells into the central nervous system may drive chronic neuroinflammation independent of viral replication. Unlike the earlier PAXLC-2 combination study, which targeted persistent viral reservoirs using tenofovir-based regimens plus maraviroc, SENTINEL-LC tested maraviroc alone and specifically in patients without detectable spike antigenemia, suggesting the benefit derives from immune modulation rather than antiviral activity.

The principal investigator, Dr. Elena Vasquez of UCSF, noted that the effect size in biomarker-selected patients exceeded that of most other immunomodulatory agents tested in long COVID to date. An FDA supplemental new drug application is expected within six months, with the sponsor pursuing a companion diagnostic to identify eligible patients through standardized flow cytometry panels.

Ongoing extension studies will assess durability of response after treatment cessation and whether earlier intervention within 12 months of symptom onset yields greater benefit.

Why this matters: SENTINEL-LC provides the strongest evidence yet that a specific chemokine-driven immune subtype of long COVID can be identified and effectively treated with an existing, well-characterized oral therapy.

title: Eleventh Circuit Rules Long COVID Patients May Pursue Class Action Claims Against Disability Insurers Under RICO for Coordinated Denial Schemes
excerpt: The Eleventh Circuit held that long COVID patients alleging coordinated claim denial practices by disability insurers may pursue civil RICO claims, reversing dismissal of a putative class action against three major carriers. The decision opens a significant new avenue of accountability for systemic denials.
body:
The Eleventh Circuit Court of Appeals ruled Monday in Torres v. Meridian Life & Disability that long COVID claimants alleging coordinated denial practices among disability insurers may pursue civil claims under the Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations Act, reversing a district court dismissal of a putative class action against three major carriers. The decision creates the first appellate authority permitting RICO theories against insurers for systemic long COVID claim handling.

The underlying complaint, brought on behalf of an estimated 14,000 policyholders, alleges that Meridian Life & Disability, Consolidated Assurance, and Northbridge Indemnity coordinated with a common medical review vendor, Axiom Clinical Consultants, to apply undisclosed internal guidelines classifying long COVID as a "self-reported" or "subjective" condition subject to 24-month benefit caps regardless of policy language. Plaintiffs allege the carriers exchanged claim adjudication protocols, shared blacklists of independent medical examiners viewed as "claimant-friendly," and jointly funded ghostwritten medical literature disputing the objective basis of long COVID.

Court Finds Sufficient Predicate Acts

Writing for a unanimous panel, Judge Marisela Rodriguez held that plaintiffs adequately pleaded predicate acts of mail and wire fraud through allegations that denial letters misrepresented internal medical review conclusions and omitted material information about the coordinated guidelines. The court rejected the insurers' argument that ERISA preempts RICO claims where the underlying conduct extends beyond individual plan administration to industry-wide coordination.

"The distinct injury alleged here is not merely improper denial of a single benefit claim," the panel wrote, "but participation in an association-in-fact enterprise designed to systematically deprive a class of insureds of contractual rights through fraudulent misrepresentation of medical consensus." The court also found the pattern requirement satisfied by allegations spanning multiple states and continuing over three years.

The decision distinguishes prior circuit precedent limiting RICO's application to insurance disputes by emphasizing the alleged coordination among ostensibly competing carriers and the use of a shared vendor to launder discretionary determinations through a veneer of independent medical review. It remands for class certification proceedings, where plaintiffs will seek to represent all long COVID claimants denied benefits by the three carriers since January 2022.

Industry analysts anticipate significant implications for disability insurance litigation. Treble damages under RICO, combined with attorneys' fees, dramatically alter settlement calculus compared to conventional ERISA benefit claims, which are limited to recovery of the benefit itself. Several plaintiffs' firms have announced parallel investigations of other carriers based on similar allegations.

The insurers have indicated they will seek en banc review or Supreme Court certiorari.

Why this matters: The ruling transforms the legal landscape for long COVID disability disputes by allowing coordinated denial practices to be prosecuted as organized fraud rather than isolated administrative errors, potentially exposing insurers to damages far exceeding traditional benefit recovery.

title: CMS Finalizes Payment Model Providing Bundled Monthly Reimbursement for Long COVID Multidisciplinary Care Beginning January 2027
excerpt: CMS finalized a new alternative payment model providing bundled monthly per-patient reimbursement of $487 to qualifying multidisciplinary long COVID clinics, launching January 2027. The model addresses longstanding financial barriers that have caused specialized clinics to close.
body:
The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services finalized a new alternative payment model on Tuesday that will provide bundled monthly per-patient reimbursement of $487 to qualifying multidisciplinary long COVID clinics beginning January 1, 2027. The Long COVID Comprehensive Care Model (LC-CCM) directly addresses the financial fragility that has caused more than 40 specialized clinics to close since 2023 despite growing patient demand.

Under the model, participating clinics receive a case-mix-adjusted monthly payment covering physician oversight, nurse care coordination, integrated behavioral health, physical and occupational therapy consultation, and remote symptom monitoring. Payments continue for up to 24 months per patient, with extensions available for those meeting persistent severity criteria. The bundle replaces fragmented fee-for-service billing that has historically failed to cover the time-intensive care coordination required for complex long COVID patients.

Structural Reform After Years of Clinic Closures

The rule follows two years of pilot testing in 22 clinics across nine states, where participating sites reported 34% improvements in patient-reported functional outcomes and 41% reductions in emergency department utilization compared with matched controls. Notably, pilot clinics achieved these results while maintaining financial viability, contrasting sharply with the closure of prominent programs at academic medical centers that could not sustain operations under conventional billing.

To qualify for the model, clinics must demonstrate integrated staffing across at least five specialties, use standardized diagnostic protocols aligned with the recently adopted WHO ICD-11 subtype framework, participate in the CDC National Long COVID Surveillance System, and report quarterly outcome data on validated instruments including the PROMIS-29 and DePaul Symptom Questionnaire. Clinics must also accept all Medicare beneficiaries referred without cherry-picking based on complexity.

The final rule expanded eligibility beyond the proposed version to include federally qualified health centers and rural health clinics that partner with academic hubs through telehealth consultation arrangements. This provision responds to comments raising concerns about geographic inequity in access to specialized care, with rural patients previously required to travel hundreds of miles to reach qualifying clinics.

Payment methodology incorporates a risk-adjustment formula accounting for post-exertional malaise severity, dysautonomia, cognitive impairment, and comorbidity burden. High-complexity patients generate supplemental payments up to $312 monthly above the base rate. CMS estimates total federal outlays of $3.8 billion over five years, offset by projected reductions in emergency care and disability program utilization.

Private insurers frequently follow Medicare payment innovations, and industry observers expect commercial adoption of similar bundled models within 18 months. The Long COVID Alliance called the finalization the most consequential payment reform for the condition to date.

Why this matters: By replacing broken fee-for-service billing with sustainable bundled payments, the model may finally allow multidisciplinary long COVID clinics to survive financially and expand access to comprehensive care nationwide.

title: Phase 3 SUMMIT-LC Trial Shows Baricitinib Reduces Long COVID Vascular Inflammation Markers by 44% in Patients With Elevated Soluble P-Selectin
excerpt: The 780-patient SUMMIT-LC trial found that 24 weeks of low-dose baricitinib significantly reduced endothelial inflammation biomarkers and improved functional capacity in Long COVID patients stratified by vascular activation signatures.
body:
The Phase 3 SUMMIT-LC trial, published this week in The Lancet Rheumatology, found that 24 weeks of low-dose baricitinib reduced soluble P-selectin, VCAM-1, and von Willebrand factor levels by an average of 44% in Long COVID patients with documented vascular inflammation, while improving six-minute walk distance by 68 meters compared to placebo.

The multicenter trial enrolled 780 adults across 62 sites in the United States, Germany, and Australia, all of whom met WHO Long COVID criteria and had persistent symptoms for at least nine months. Participants were required to demonstrate elevated baseline levels of at least two endothelial activation markers, a stratification strategy that investigators say was critical to the trial's success after earlier unstratified JAK inhibitor studies produced mixed results.

Trial Design and Primary Outcomes

Patients received either 2 mg baricitinib daily or matched placebo. The primary composite endpoint combined biomarker normalization with a minimum 15% improvement in the PROMIS-29 physical function score. At week 24, 51% of the baricitinib arm met the composite endpoint compared to 18% of placebo recipients. Secondary endpoints showed statistically significant improvements in orthostatic tolerance, cognitive processing speed on NIH Toolbox testing, and patient-reported post-exertional malaise frequency.

Notably, the trial incorporated repeat sublingual microcirculation imaging, which showed measurable improvements in capillary density and red blood cell velocity in treated patients. Investigators believe this provides mechanistic support for the biomarker findings and distinguishes baricitinib's effects from purely symptomatic treatments.

Safety Profile and Regulatory Path

Adverse events were consistent with baricitinib's established safety profile in rheumatoid arthritis and COVID-19 acute care indications. Herpes zoster reactivation occurred in 2.3% of treated patients versus 0.5% on placebo, and modest LDL elevations were observed. No thromboembolic events exceeded background rates, a finding of particular importance given the vascular focus of the trial.

Eli Lilly, which supplied study drug, has announced plans to submit a supplemental New Drug Application to the FDA before the end of 2026, seeking a Long COVID indication restricted to biomarker-positive patients. The company said it would pursue a companion diagnostic pathway to enable payer coverage tied to laboratory stratification.

Independent commentators emphasized that SUMMIT-LC is the first large trial to demonstrate that endothelial dysfunction, previously characterized in observational studies including the RECOVER-VASCULAR cohort, is pharmacologically reversible. Several editorialists cautioned that long-term safety data will be essential given the chronic nature of Long COVID and the potential for extended dosing.

Why this matters: SUMMIT-LC establishes vascular inflammation as a treatable driver of Long COVID and provides the first mechanistically targeted therapy addressing the endothelial pathology that affects the majority of long-haul patients.

title: Eleventh Circuit Rules Private Schools Receiving Federal Voucher Funds Must Accommodate Students With Long COVID Under Section 504
excerpt: In a unanimous decision, the Eleventh Circuit held that private schools accepting state voucher or education savings account funds derived from federal sources qualify as recipients of federal financial assistance and must provide reasonable accommodations to students with Long COVID.
body:
The Eleventh Circuit Court of Appeals ruled unanimously this week in Delacruz v. Sacred Heart Academy that private schools accepting state education voucher or education savings account funds traceable to federal sources qualify as recipients of federal financial assistance and are therefore bound by Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, including its accommodation requirements for students with Long COVID.

The case arose from a Florida family whose twelve-year-old daughter, disabled by post-exertional malaise, dysautonomia, and cognitive symptoms following a 2023 SARS-CoV-2 infection, was denied requests for shortened school days, rest breaks, and remote instruction options. The academy argued that as a private religious institution it was exempt from Section 504, even though it received tuition payments through Florida's Family Empowerment Scholarship program.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for the panel, Judge Britt Grant held that the pass-through of federal pandemic-era education funds and ongoing federal Title I supplementation of state voucher programs created sufficient nexus to trigger Section 504 obligations. The court rejected the school's argument that voucher funds become purely private upon receipt, applying the Supreme Court's reasoning in Grove City College v. Bell and its progeny.

The opinion specifically addressed Long COVID, citing CDC surveillance data and the RECOVER-PEDIATRIC study findings that 4.3% of U.S. children meet Long COVID criteria. Judge Grant wrote that the condition's episodic and exertion-triggered nature requires flexible accommodations that many private schools have historically refused to provide, and that Congress's decision to condition federal funding on nondiscrimination cannot be circumvented through voucher intermediation.

Implications for School Choice Programs

The ruling has immediate implications across the Eleventh Circuit's jurisdiction of Florida, Georgia, and Alabama, which together enroll more than 400,000 students in publicly funded voucher and scholarship programs. Education law analysts expect similar litigation in states with expansive education savings account programs, including Arizona, Iowa, and Ohio.

The decision creates a circuit split with dicta from the Fifth Circuit suggesting narrower application of Section 504 to voucher recipients, making Supreme Court review possible. Advocacy organizations including the Council of Parent Attorneys and Advocates and the Long COVID Kids Coalition filed amicus briefs supporting the plaintiffs.

Florida Attorney General Ashley Moody indicated the state may seek en banc review, arguing the decision undermines legislative intent to shield participating schools from federal regulation. The Sacred Heart Academy has not indicated whether it will petition for rehearing.

The court remanded for calculation of compensatory education services and attorneys' fees, and ordered the school to develop a Section 504 plan for the plaintiff student pending further proceedings.

Why this matters: The ruling closes a major accommodation gap for the growing population of children with Long COVID whose families use voucher programs, and could reshape federal civil rights obligations across the fast-expanding private school choice landscape.

title: FDA Approves Ampligen as First Immunomodulator for Long COVID Patients With Post-Exertional Malaise and Impaired Natural Killer Cell Function
excerpt: The FDA granted full approval to Ampligen (rintatolimod) for treatment of Long COVID patients with documented post-exertional malaise and reduced NK cell cytotoxicity, marking the first immunomodulator specifically indicated for the condition.
body:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration granted full approval today to Ampligen (rintatolimod), a TLR3 agonist manufactured by AIM ImmunoTech, for the treatment of Long COVID patients with documented post-exertional malaise and laboratory evidence of impaired natural killer cell cytotoxicity. It is the first immunomodulator approved with a Long COVID-specific label and the first therapy indicated for post-exertional malaise, the cardinal disabling symptom of the condition.

The approval rests on data from the Phase 3 AMPLIFY-PEM trial, which enrolled 620 patients across 48 U.S. sites and randomized them to twice-weekly intravenous rintatolimod or placebo for 40 weeks. The trial met its primary endpoint, with 47% of treated patients achieving at least a 30% reduction in post-exertional malaise frequency measured by the validated DePaul Symptom Questionnaire-PEM subscale, compared with 19% of placebo recipients.

Mechanism and Patient Selection

Rintatolimod is a mismatched double-stranded RNA that selectively activates Toll-like receptor 3, driving a type I interferon response distinct from the sustained interferon signaling implicated in some Long COVID pathology. Investigators believe the pulsed nature of TLR3 activation restores natural killer cell function without perpetuating chronic inflammation. Patients enrolled in the pivotal trial were required to demonstrate NK cell cytotoxicity below the lower limit of normal on a standardized 51-chromium release assay, a stratification approach that mirrors the drug's decades-long research history in myalgic encephalomyelitis.

The approved label restricts use to patients meeting these criteria and specifies that treatment should be initiated only after at least six months of persistent PEM. The FDA also required a post-marketing registry to monitor long-term safety, cardiovascular outcomes, and durability of response.

Access, Pricing, and Historical Context

AIM ImmunoTech set the U.S. list price at $34,500 per year, substantially below several recently approved Long COVID biologics. The company said it would offer patient assistance programs and has begun discussions with major insurers regarding coverage pathways under the recent HHS-Department of Labor joint guidance on Long COVID diagnostics and treatments.

The approval represents a striking reversal for a drug that had been rejected by the FDA multiple times over three decades for its previous chronic fatigue syndrome indication. Regulators cited the substantially larger and more rigorous AMPLIFY-PEM trial, improved manufacturing controls, and the availability of a validated stratification biomarker as reasons for approval.

Patient advocacy organizations including Body Politic and the Solve ME/CFS Initiative welcomed the decision but urged rapid expansion of infusion capacity, warning that the twice-weekly IV administration schedule could create access bottlenecks similar to those experienced with early monoclonal antibody rollouts.

Why this matters: Ampligen becomes the first FDA-approved therapy targeting post-exertional malaise, the symptom that renders millions of Long COVID patients unable to work or function, and its approval validates decades of research linking immune dysfunction to post-viral disability.

title: Phase 3 HORIZON-LC Trial Shows CD38 Inhibitor Isatuximab Reduces Long COVID Symptoms by 45% in Patients With Elevated NAD+ Depletion Markers
excerpt: The Phase 3 HORIZON-LC trial, published in The Lancet last week, found that monthly infusions of the anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody isatuximab reduced composite Long COVID symptom scores by 45% in patients with laboratory evidence of NAD+ depletion. The findings validate a metabolic-immune mechanism that has been debated since 2022.
body:
The Phase 3 HORIZON-LC trial, published in The Lancet on June 24, found that monthly infusions of the anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody isatuximab reduced composite Long COVID symptom scores by 45% over 24 weeks in patients with laboratory evidence of NAD+ depletion. The trial enrolled 612 adults across 47 sites in the United States, Germany, and Japan, and represents the first successful Phase 3 evaluation of a metabolic-immune targeted therapy in the condition.

CD38 is a surface enzyme expressed on activated immune cells that consumes NAD+, a coenzyme essential for mitochondrial energy production. Investigators had previously observed that Long COVID patients with persistent fatigue and post-exertional malaise showed elevated CD38 expression on CD8+ T cells and correspondingly depressed intracellular NAD+ concentrations. The HORIZON-LC trial prospectively selected patients on the basis of these biomarkers, randomizing them to isatuximab or placebo infusions every four weeks.

Trial Design and Primary Outcomes

The primary endpoint, a composite of fatigue, cognitive function, and post-exertional symptom scores measured by the PROMIS-29 and DePaul Symptom Questionnaire, improved by 45% in the treatment arm compared to 11% in the placebo arm. Secondary endpoints including six-minute walk distance and hand grip strength also showed statistically significant improvements. Serum NAD+ levels rose by an average of 62% in responders, and CD8+ T cell activation markers normalized in 71% of treated patients by week 20.

Adverse events were consistent with the drug's oncology safety profile. Infusion reactions occurred in 18% of patients but were manageable with premedication. Three cases of serious infection occurred in the treatment arm compared to one in the placebo arm, and investigators noted transient neutropenia in 22% of patients.

Clinical Deployment Questions

Isatuximab is currently approved for multiple myeloma at substantially higher doses than those used in HORIZON-LC. Sanofi has announced plans to file a supplemental Biologics License Application by the end of 2026, and the FDA has indicated the submission will receive priority review given the size of the affected population. Analysts project that if approved, list pricing at the reduced Long COVID dose would fall between $48,000 and $62,000 per year, raising immediate questions about payer coverage frameworks and prior authorization criteria.

Investigators cautioned that the biomarker-selected design means the results cannot be generalized to Long COVID patients without documented CD38 elevation and NAD+ depletion, a subset estimated at 28 to 34% of the diagnosed population.

Why this matters: HORIZON-LC provides the strongest evidence to date that a targeted metabolic-immune intervention can produce durable clinical improvement in a biologically defined Long COVID subgroup, moving the field decisively toward precision medicine.

title: D.C. Circuit Rules OPM Must Recognize Long COVID as Qualifying Condition for Federal Disability Retirement Under FERS
excerpt: The D.C. Circuit ruled on June 18 that the Office of Personnel Management may not require federal employees seeking FERS disability retirement to demonstrate Long COVID through objective imaging or serologic findings where functional impairment is otherwise documented. The decision affects an estimated 340,000 federal workers with post-acute COVID symptoms.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit ruled on June 18 in Chen v. Office of Personnel Management that OPM may not require federal employees seeking Federal Employees Retirement System disability retirement to demonstrate Long COVID through objective imaging or serologic findings where functional impairment is otherwise documented through treating physician records and standardized functional assessments. The unanimous three-judge panel reversed a decision by the Merit Systems Protection Board that had denied benefits to a former Commerce Department economist.

Background of the Dispute

Petitioner Wei Chen, a GS-14 economist, applied for FERS disability retirement in 2024 after being unable to sustain a 40-hour workweek due to cognitive fatigue and orthostatic intolerance following a 2022 SARS-CoV-2 infection. Her physician documented meeting criteria for both myalgic encephalomyelitis and postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome, and her employing agency had exhausted reasonable accommodations. OPM denied the application on the grounds that Chen had not produced objective diagnostic evidence such as tilt-table findings, brain imaging abnormalities, or serologic markers meeting OPM's internal Long COVID adjudication guidance.

The MSPB affirmed, citing OPM's authority to establish evidentiary standards. Chen appealed, arguing that the standard imposed a heightened burden not applied to other conditions such as fibromyalgia or chronic pain, and that it conflicted with the FERS statute's focus on functional inability to perform position duties.

The Court's Analysis

Writing for the panel, Judge Millett held that OPM's Long COVID guidance represented an improper substantive rule adopted without notice and comment, and that it conflicted with 5 U.S.C. § 8451, which defines disability by reference to service and position rather than diagnostic method. The court noted that OPM accepts treating physician documentation as sufficient for numerous other chronic conditions and cannot single out Long COVID for a heightened evidentiary standard without statutory authorization or rulemaking.

The panel further held that OPM's practice violated the Rehabilitation Act by imposing conditions that disproportionately excluded a disability category. The court remanded Chen's application for reconsideration under standards applicable to comparable conditions.

Implications for Federal Workforce

OPM data released last year indicated that approximately 340,000 federal employees have reported Long COVID symptoms, with roughly 47,000 having applied for FERS disability retirement since 2022. Denial rates for Long COVID applications have exceeded 68%, compared to approximately 31% for other conditions. Federal employee unions estimated that the ruling could trigger reconsideration of tens of thousands of denied applications.

Why this matters: The decision removes one of the largest institutional barriers preventing disabled federal workers with Long COVID from accessing retirement benefits and signals that agencies cannot quietly adopt diagnostic double standards for post-viral conditions.

title: HHS Announces $2.4 Billion Long COVID Clinical Care Network Establishing 180 Regional Centers of Excellence With Guaranteed Insurance Parity
excerpt: Health and Human Services Secretary announced on June 30 a $2.4 billion, five-year initiative to establish 180 regional Long COVID Centers of Excellence, with statutory insurance parity provisions requiring commercial payers to reimburse network care at Medicare rates. The program addresses years of documented access gaps in specialized care.
body:
Health and Human Services announced on June 30 a $2.4 billion, five-year initiative to establish 180 regional Long COVID Centers of Excellence across all fifty states and U.S. territories, with statutory insurance parity provisions requiring commercial payers to reimburse in-network care at rates no lower than Medicare fee schedules. The Long COVID Clinical Care Network was authorized under the FY2026 appropriations act signed in December and represents the largest federal investment in dedicated Long COVID clinical infrastructure to date.

Network Structure

The network is organized around a hub-and-spoke model. Twenty academic medical center hubs will provide advanced diagnostics, complex case management, and clinical research infrastructure, while 160 community-based spoke clinics will deliver primary specialty care within a 90-minute drive of 94% of the U.S. population. Each site must meet certification standards developed by the Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality, including staffing benchmarks for physical therapy, autonomic testing, cognitive rehabilitation, and mental health integration.

Sites will be required to accept all insurance including Medicaid and to maintain sliding-scale fees for uninsured patients. Wait times for initial evaluation are capped at 45 days under the certification standards, and sites failing to meet the benchmark for two consecutive quarters lose federal designation and associated funding.

Insurance Parity Provisions

The most consequential element of the program is the statutory insurance parity requirement embedded in the authorizing legislation. Commercial group health plans and individual market plans must reimburse network care at rates equal to or exceeding 100% of Medicare, may not impose utilization management more restrictive than Medicare, and cannot apply mental health carve-outs to services delivered within the network. The provisions preempt contrary state law and are enforced jointly by CMS and the Department of Labor.

Payer trade associations have signaled likely litigation, arguing the parity mandate exceeds the federal government's authority under ERISA and the McCarran-Ferguson Act. Legal analysts note the provisions were drafted with attention to prior circuit court decisions on mental health parity and are likely to survive facial challenge.

Rollout Timeline

The first 40 sites, drawn from existing RECOVER clinical hubs and academic Long COVID clinics, will receive certification and funding by October 2026. Full network deployment is scheduled for the end of 2028. HHS estimated that the network will serve approximately 3.2 million Long COVID patients annually at full capacity, representing roughly 22% of the U.S. adult Long COVID population.

Why this matters: The combination of guaranteed geographic access, certified clinical standards, and enforceable insurance parity addresses the three most persistent structural failures in Long COVID care and creates a durable delivery infrastructure that will outlast the current administration.

title: Phase 3 CATALYST-LC Trial Shows Sirolimus Reduces Long COVID Post-Exertional Malaise by 37% in Patients With mTOR Pathway Activation
excerpt: The multicenter CATALYST-LC trial reported that low-dose sirolimus reduced post-exertional malaise episodes by 37% over 24 weeks in Long COVID patients with elevated mTOR signaling on peripheral blood mononuclear cell assays.
body:
The Phase 3 CATALYST-LC trial, published this week in The Lancet, found that low-dose sirolimus reduced post-exertional malaise (PEM) episodes by 37% over 24 weeks in Long COVID patients with laboratory evidence of mTOR pathway hyperactivation. The 612-patient randomized trial, conducted across 38 U.S. and European sites, marks the first successful large-scale test of an mTOR inhibitor in the condition.

Participants received either 2 mg oral sirolimus daily or matched placebo, with dose adjustments based on trough serum concentrations. Enrollment required both a documented history of PEM meeting IOM criteria and elevated phosphorylated S6 kinase levels in PBMCs, a marker of mTOR pathway activation identified in prior mechanistic studies as characteristic of a subset of Long COVID patients with mitochondrial dysfunction and impaired autophagy.

The primary endpoint, a 50% reduction in weekly PEM episodes captured through validated symptom diaries and wearable activity monitors, was met by 44% of sirolimus recipients compared with 19% of placebo recipients. Secondary endpoints showed improvements in the DePaul Symptom Questionnaire PEM subscale, hand-grip strength recovery after exertion challenge, and cognitive performance on the NIH Toolbox. Ceiling effects on cardiopulmonary exercise testing were not observed, but VO2 at ventilatory threshold improved by a mean of 2.1 mL/kg/min in the treatment arm.

Safety and Mechanism

Adverse events were consistent with the known sirolimus profile. Mouth ulcers occurred in 18% of treated patients, mild hyperlipidemia in 22%, and transient cytopenias in 7%. Three patients discontinued for infections; no cases of interstitial lung disease were reported. Investigators noted that the doses used were substantially lower than those employed in transplant medicine, and trough levels were maintained between 3 and 8 ng/mL.

The trial builds on translational work showing that persistent mTOR activation in Long COVID drives impaired mitophagy, accumulation of dysfunctional mitochondria, and abnormal T-cell metabolism. Lead investigator Dr. Amelia Kwon of Johns Hopkins said the results validate targeting cellular quality control pathways rather than downstream inflammation alone. A companion biomarker paper published in Cell Reports Medicine showed that responders had greater reductions in mitochondrial-derived cell-free DNA and normalization of CD8+ T-cell metabolic profiles.

Regulatory Path

The sponsor, Rapamycin Holdings, plans to submit a supplemental new drug application by the fourth quarter of 2026, seeking a Long COVID indication limited to patients meeting the trial's biomarker criteria. The FDA granted Fast Track designation in 2025, and Breakthrough Therapy designation is expected to follow the completed trial submission.

Why this matters: CATALYST-LC provides the first Phase 3 evidence that a mechanistically targeted intervention can meaningfully reduce post-exertional malaise, the disabling core symptom that has resisted most therapeutic approaches to date.

title: EEOC Issues Final Enforcement Guidance Classifying Long COVID as Presumptive Disability Under ADA for Workers With Documented Symptoms Beyond 12 Weeks
