excerpt: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission finalized enforcement guidance treating Long COVID as a presumptive disability under the ADA when symptoms persist beyond 12 weeks and substantially limit at least one major life activity, shifting the burden to employers to rebut the presumption.
body:
The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission on Monday issued final enforcement guidance establishing that Long COVID will be treated as a presumptive disability under Title I of the Americans with Disabilities Act when medical documentation shows symptoms persisting beyond 12 weeks that substantially limit one or more major life activities. The guidance, effective September 1, 2026, replaces the agency's 2021 technical assistance document and codifies positions the EEOC has taken in recent litigation.

Under the new framework, an employee who presents documentation from a treating clinician establishing (1) a prior SARS-CoV-2 infection or reasonable clinical inference of one, (2) symptom persistence beyond 12 weeks, and (3) impairment of a major life activity such as concentrating, working, sleeping, or exertion tolerance, is entitled to a presumption of ADA coverage. Employers may rebut the presumption only with contrary medical evidence, not by pointing to the absence of a positive PCR test or hospital admission during the acute illness.

Interactive Process Requirements

The guidance also imposes new specificity on the ADA's interactive process. Employers must respond in writing to accommodation requests within 15 business days, must consider telework, schedule modification, rest breaks, and cognitive supports as presumptively reasonable accommodations for Long COVID, and must document any claim of undue hardship with financial or operational specifics. Blanket policies denying open-ended intermittent leave for fluctuating conditions are identified as facially problematic.

The document incorporates the Supreme Court's 2025 Henderson v. Acme Logistics decision, which held that Long COVID brain fog qualifies as a cognitive disability without requiring formal neuropsychological testing. It also aligns with recent circuit court rulings requiring employers to accept two-day cardiopulmonary exercise testing and specialist clinical evaluations as sufficient objective evidence.

Business and Advocacy Response

The U.S. Chamber of Commerce criticized the guidance as effectively creating a new protected class without congressional authorization and signaled likely legal challenges under the Loper Bright framework governing agency interpretations. Disability rights organizations, including the American Association of People with Disabilities and the Long COVID Alliance, praised the document as long overdue given data showing that 46% of Long COVID workers report adverse employment actions.

EEOC Chair Charlotte Burrows said in a statement that the guidance responds to a documented pattern of employers demanding levels of proof that Long COVID patients cannot practically obtain, particularly given the absence until recently of validated diagnostic tests. The guidance notes that the FDA's 2025 approval of a multi-analyte immune signature blood test may support but is not required for coverage.

Why this matters: Presumptive disability status shifts the evidentiary burden away from ill workers who have struggled to document a condition still lacking universal diagnostic criteria, potentially reducing the wave of denied accommodation claims that has defined Long COVID employment litigation.

title: CMS Announces National Coverage Determination Extending Medicare Reimbursement to Extracorporeal Apheresis for Long COVID Patients With Microclot and Autoantibody Burden
excerpt: The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services issued a final national coverage determination extending Medicare reimbursement to double-filtration plasmapheresis and immunoadsorption apheresis for Long COVID patients meeting specific microclot and autoantibody criteria.
body:
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services on Thursday issued a final National Coverage Determination extending Medicare reimbursement to extracorporeal apheresis procedures for Long COVID patients who meet defined laboratory criteria for fibrinaloid microclot burden or G-protein-coupled receptor autoantibody elevation. The NCD, effective October 1, 2026, ends a five-year period in which patients often paid $8,000 to $25,000 per treatment course out of pocket at overseas or cash-based clinics.

Coverage Criteria

The determination covers two apheresis modalities: double-filtration plasmapheresis (DFPP) for patients with documented microclot burden using standardized fluorescence imaging assays validated under the FDA's 2025 diagnostic pathway, and immunoadsorption apheresis for patients with elevated titers of specific autoantibodies against beta-adrenergic and muscarinic receptors, angiotensin II type 1 receptors, or endothelin-1 receptors.

Coverage requires diagnosis of Long COVID under WHO ICD-11 codes, symptom duration of at least six months, failure of at least three months of guideline-directed medical therapy, and treatment at CMS-designated Long COVID specialty centers meeting personnel and quality reporting requirements. Initial coverage authorizes up to eight sessions over 12 weeks, with continuation contingent on documented functional improvement measured by the PROMIS-29 and a validated activity monitor.

The NCD was informed by evidence from three randomized trials published between 2024 and 2026, including a German multicenter study showing 41% symptom reduction with immunoadsorption in autoantibody-positive patients and a South African/UK collaborative trial demonstrating microclot clearance correlated with improvements in exercise capacity and cognitive function.

Payment and Access

CMS set the payment rate at $2,340 per session under the hospital outpatient prospective payment system, with a physician work component of approximately $340. Analysts at Avalere estimate the coverage expansion could reach 180,000 Medicare beneficiaries in the first year at a projected cost of $2.1 billion, offset partially by reductions in emergency department utilization and inpatient admissions for autonomic and cardiovascular events.

Private insurers historically follow Medicare coverage determinations within 12 to 24 months, and several major carriers, including UnitedHealthcare and Aetna, indicated they would review their coverage policies in light of the NCD. State Medicaid programs remain subject to separate coverage decisions, though the Eleventh Circuit's 2025 ruling requiring state Medicaid programs to cover Long COVID specialty clinic services may create pressure for parallel coverage.

The American Society for Apheresis warned that current U.S. capacity for these procedures is inadequate to meet projected demand and called for CMS to fund training programs and mobile apheresis units serving rural regions.

Why this matters: Medicare coverage of biomarker-guided apheresis transforms an experimental last-resort treatment into a standard-of-care option for the sickest Long COVID patients, while capacity constraints will determine whether access matches the newly recognized coverage.

title: Phase 3 KINDLE-LC Trial Shows Metformin Extended-Release Reduces Long COVID Incidence by 41% When Started Within Seven Days of Acute Infection
excerpt: A large multicenter Phase 3 trial published in The Lancet Infectious Diseases confirms that early metformin treatment during acute SARS-CoV-2 infection cuts the incidence of Long COVID at six months by 41%. The finding transforms metformin into the first widely available prophylactic agent against Long COVID.
body:
Extended-release metformin initiated within seven days of a positive SARS-CoV-2 test reduced the incidence of Long COVID at six months by 41% compared with placebo, according to results from the Phase 3 KINDLE-LC trial published this week in The Lancet Infectious Diseases. The finding establishes the inexpensive, off-patent diabetes drug as the first widely available agent shown in a confirmatory trial to prevent the development of Long COVID.

The trial enrolled 6,842 non-hospitalized adults aged 30 to 75 across 94 sites in the United States, United Kingdom, and Brazil between 2024 and 2026. Participants were randomized within 168 hours of symptom onset to receive either 1,500 mg of extended-release metformin daily for 14 days or matching placebo. The primary endpoint was a physician-adjudicated diagnosis of Long COVID at 180 days using the WHO consensus definition.

At six months, 7.4% of the metformin group met criteria for Long COVID compared with 12.6% of the placebo group, corresponding to a relative risk reduction of 41.3% (95% CI, 33.2 to 48.7). Benefits were consistent across age, sex, vaccination status, and body mass index strata, though the absolute effect was largest in participants with a BMI above 30 and in those unvaccinated within the preceding 12 months. Secondary endpoints showed reductions in post-exertional malaise (44%), cognitive symptoms (38%), and cardiovascular events at one year (29%).

Mechanism and Prior Signals

The KINDLE-LC investigators attribute the effect to metformin's inhibition of mTOR-mediated viral replication, dampening of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, and modulation of the gut microbiome. Prior signals from the COVID-OUT trial and multiple observational cohorts had suggested a protective effect, but KINDLE-LC is the first adequately powered, dedicated Long COVID prevention trial to confirm the finding under regulatory-grade conditions.

Adverse events were consistent with metformin's established safety profile. Gastrointestinal symptoms occurred in 18% of metformin recipients versus 9% of placebo recipients, and no cases of lactic acidosis were reported.

Regulatory and Practice Implications

The FDA has indicated it will convene an advisory committee in September to consider a supplemental indication for metformin in acute COVID-19 to prevent Long COVID. The Infectious Diseases Society of America issued interim guidance recommending metformin be offered to adults with acute SARS-CoV-2 infection who have at least one risk factor for Long COVID, pending formal labeling. Because the drug costs less than $4 per treatment course at generic pricing, global health authorities are already modeling implementation in low- and middle-income settings.

Why this matters: For the first time, clinicians have a cheap, generic, orally administered intervention with confirmatory Phase 3 evidence to prevent Long COVID at the point of acute infection.

title: D.C. Circuit Rules Social Security Administration Must Accept Two-Day Cardiopulmonary Exercise Testing as Objective Evidence of Disability in Long COVID Claims
excerpt: The D.C. Circuit held that the Social Security Administration acted arbitrarily when it disregarded two-day cardiopulmonary exercise testing showing post-exertional malaise in a Long COVID disability claimant. The ruling requires SSA to treat 2-day CPET results as objective medical evidence in Long COVID cases nationwide.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit ruled Monday that the Social Security Administration must accept two-day cardiopulmonary exercise testing (2-day CPET) as objective medical evidence of disability in Long COVID claims, reversing a policy that had dismissed such testing as insufficiently standardized. The decision in Whitaker v. O'Malley is binding on federal claims processed through the SSA's national Appeals Council and is likely to influence adjudication in every circuit.

The plaintiff, Denise Whitaker, a 46-year-old former air traffic controller, developed Long COVID after a 2022 infection and applied for Social Security Disability Insurance in 2023. Her application included results from a 2-day CPET at Ithaca's Workwell Foundation showing a 24% drop in VO2 at the ventilatory threshold on day two, a physiologic pattern consistent with the post-exertional malaise that defines ME/CFS and is a core feature of Long COVID. The administrative law judge disregarded the testing on the grounds that SSA policy did not recognize 2-day CPET as an accepted objective measure, and denied benefits.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for a unanimous three-judge panel, Judge Cornelia Pillard held that SSA's categorical exclusion of 2-day CPET was arbitrary and capricious under the Administrative Procedure Act. The court noted that the CDC, NIH RECOVER program, and the American Academy of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation all recognize 2-day CPET as the most objective available measure of post-exertional malaise, and that SSA had failed to articulate any medical basis for excluding it. "An agency cannot ignore the settled scientific consensus of the very federal research enterprise charged with characterizing the disease at issue," the opinion states.

The court further held that SSA's Ruling 24-1p, which acknowledges Long COVID as a medically determinable impairment, cannot be given effect if adjudicators are permitted to reject the primary objective test capable of documenting its hallmark symptom.

Practical Consequences

The ruling requires SSA to remand Whitaker's claim and to issue guidance within 120 days instructing adjudicators to consider 2-day CPET findings as objective evidence when properly documented. Advocacy organizations estimate that roughly 34,000 pending Long COVID disability claims nationwide involve 2-day CPET results that were previously discounted. The National Organization of Social Security Claimants' Representatives called the decision "the most significant Long COVID disability ruling since the ADA amendments era."

SSA declined to say whether it will seek en banc review or Supreme Court certiorari, but noted that Commissioner Martin O'Malley has previously expressed support for updating the agency's evidentiary framework for post-viral illness.

Why this matters: The decision forces the nation's largest disability program to align its evidentiary standards with the physiological reality of post-exertional malaise, opening a path to benefits for tens of thousands of Long COVID claimants.

title: FDA Approves Larazotide Acetate as First Targeted Therapy for Long COVID Patients With Intestinal Permeability and Persistent Spike Antigenemia
excerpt: The FDA has approved larazotide acetate for adults with Long COVID who have documented intestinal hyperpermeability and detectable circulating spike antigen, marking the first approval of a targeted therapy for the condition. The decision follows the Phase 3 SEAL-LC trial showing a 39% reduction in symptom burden.
body:
The Food and Drug Administration on Tuesday approved larazotide acetate (marketed as Zovaxa) for adults with Long COVID who have laboratory-confirmed intestinal hyperpermeability and persistent circulating SARS-CoV-2 spike antigen, making it the first drug ever approved with a Long COVID-specific indication rather than a symptomatic one. The approval was based on the Phase 3 SEAL-LC trial, which showed a 39% reduction in composite symptom burden over 24 weeks.

Larazotide is a synthetic octapeptide that tightens intestinal epithelial junctions by antagonizing zonulin, the endogenous regulator of paracellular permeability. Investigators hypothesized that the drug would reduce the ongoing translocation of gut-derived spike antigen into systemic circulation, a mechanism increasingly implicated in the pathophysiology of a distinct Long COVID endotype.

The SEAL-LC Trial

SEAL-LC enrolled 918 adults across 62 U.S. and European sites who met three inclusion criteria: WHO-defined Long COVID persisting more than 12 months, elevated lactulose-to-mannitol ratio indicating intestinal hyperpermeability, and detectable full-length spike antigen on the Simoa ultrasensitive assay. Participants were randomized to larazotide 0.5 mg orally three times daily or placebo for 24 weeks.

At 24 weeks, the larazotide group showed a 39.4% reduction in the composite Long COVID Symptom Index versus 11.2% in the placebo group (p<0.001). Circulating spike antigen became undetectable in 58% of treated patients versus 12% of controls. Secondary endpoints showed significant improvements in fatigue (NIH PROMIS-Fatigue, -8.7 points), cognitive function (Cognitron composite, +0.42 SD), and gastrointestinal symptom scores. Effects were most pronounced in patients whose baseline spike antigen exceeded 0.5 pg/mL.

Adverse events were mild and consistent with the drug's celiac disease development program. Headache (14%) and abdominal discomfort (11%) were the most common; no serious drug-related events were reported.

Labeling and Access

The FDA-approved label restricts use to patients who have both a validated intestinal permeability test and a positive spike antigen assay from an FDA-cleared platform. The agency also required a post-marketing commitment to enroll 4,000 patients in a real-world registry to characterize long-term outcomes and identify predictors of response. Manufacturer 9 Meters Biopharma announced a list price of $2,340 per month with a patient assistance program capping out-of-pocket costs at $50 for insured patients. CMS indicated it will issue a national coverage analysis within 90 days, and several large commercial insurers, including Aetna and Cigna, have already published interim coverage policies.

Approximately 18% of the estimated 17 million U.S. adults with Long COVID are expected to meet the biomarker criteria for treatment eligibility.

Why this matters: The approval represents the first time a therapy has been sanctioned by regulators specifically for Long COVID and establishes a precedent for biomarker-defined endotypes as the path forward in drug development for the condition.

title: Phase 3 RENEW-LC Trial Shows JAK1 Inhibitor Upadacitinib Reduces Long COVID Inflammatory Symptoms by 42% in Patients With Elevated Interferon Signatures
excerpt: The 640-patient RENEW-LC trial found that upadacitinib produced significant reductions in fatigue, joint pain, and post-exertional malaise in Long COVID patients with elevated type I interferon gene signatures. Results were published this week in The Lancet.
body:
The Phase 3 RENEW-LC trial, published Monday in The Lancet, found that the JAK1-selective inhibitor upadacitinib reduced overall symptom burden by 42% over 20 weeks in Long COVID patients with documented elevated type I interferon gene signatures. The multicenter trial randomized 640 patients across 38 sites in the United States, United Kingdom, and Germany.

Patients enrolled in the study had persistent Long COVID symptoms for at least 12 months and demonstrated interferon-stimulated gene expression scores at least two standard deviations above healthy controls. Participants received either 15 mg oral upadacitinib daily or matched placebo. The primary endpoint was change in the Composite Long COVID Symptom Score at week 20.

Key Findings

Beyond the primary endpoint, upadacitinib recipients showed a 47% reduction in patient-reported joint pain, a 39% reduction in fatigue as measured by the FACIT-Fatigue scale, and a 36% improvement in cognitive symptoms on the Cogstate battery. Post-exertional malaise frequency, measured by the DePaul Symptom Questionnaire, declined by 44% compared with 11% in the placebo arm.

Biomarker analyses showed normalization of interferon-stimulated gene expression in 68% of treated patients by week 12, and reductions in serum CXCL10 and IFN-α protein levels correlated with symptom improvement. Investigators reported that treatment response was strongest in patients with the highest baseline interferon signatures, supporting a biomarker-stratified treatment approach.

Safety Profile

Serious adverse events occurred in 4.1% of the upadacitinib group compared with 2.8% of the placebo group. Herpes zoster reactivation was reported in 2.2% of treated patients, consistent with the drug's established safety profile in rheumatoid arthritis and atopic dermatitis. No cases of major adverse cardiovascular events or venous thromboembolism were reported during the trial period. Investigators emphasized that longer-term safety monitoring would be required before considering broad clinical use.

AbbVie, which manufactures upadacitinib under the brand name Rinvoq, announced that it will submit a supplemental new drug application to the FDA seeking a Long COVID indication for the interferon-high subgroup by the end of the third quarter. Analysts estimate that between 18% and 24% of Long COVID patients meet the interferon-high phenotype based on registry data from the NIH RECOVER program.

Independent commentators noted that RENEW-LC represents the first Phase 3 trial to demonstrate efficacy of a targeted immunomodulator in a biomarker-defined Long COVID subgroup and could accelerate the movement toward precision medicine approaches for the condition.

Why this matters: Successful biomarker-stratified trials like RENEW-LC signal a shift from one-size-fits-all Long COVID care to targeted immunologic therapy for defined patient subgroups.

title: Eighth Circuit Rules Long COVID Patients Denied Workers' Compensation for Occupational SARS-CoV-2 Exposure May Bring Section 1983 Due Process Claims
excerpt: In a 2-1 decision, the Eighth Circuit held that state workers' compensation boards that categorically deny Long COVID claims without individualized hearings violate procedural due process. The ruling revives claims from healthcare and meatpacking workers across four states.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit ruled Tuesday that state workers' compensation boards that categorically deny Long COVID claims without providing individualized hearings on causation violate the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause, opening the door for injured workers to bring Section 1983 claims in federal court. The decision in Ramirez v. Missouri Division of Workers' Compensation revives lawsuits filed by nurses, respiratory therapists, and meatpacking workers across Missouri, Iowa, Nebraska, and Arkansas.

The lead plaintiff, respiratory therapist Elena Ramirez, contracted COVID-19 in 2022 while caring for ventilated patients at a St. Louis hospital and developed persistent dysautonomia, cognitive impairment, and fatigue that left her unable to return to work. Missouri's workers' compensation division denied her claim under a 2023 administrative policy stating that Long COVID does not qualify as a compensable occupational disease absent proof of a specific transmission event.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for the majority, Judge Jane Kelly held that the categorical denial policy deprived workers of a constitutionally protected property interest in workers' compensation benefits without the individualized process required under Mathews v. Eldridge. The court emphasized that occupational disease claims have historically been evaluated through medical causation evidence rather than proof of a single exposure event, and that applying a stricter standard to Long COVID amounted to arbitrary disparate treatment.

The court also noted that categorical denials effectively shifted the risk of a novel occupational hazard onto the workers most exposed to it, contrary to the remedial purpose of workers' compensation statutes. The panel declined to reach the plaintiffs' equal protection claims but noted that the same reasoning would likely support such theories.

Practical Implications

The dissent, authored by Judge Steven Grasz, argued that workers' compensation is a matter of state statutory grace and that federal courts should not micromanage state administrative procedures. The dissent warned that the ruling could open the floodgates to federal litigation over routine workers' compensation denials.

Labor advocates estimate that between 30,000 and 50,000 healthcare and food processing workers in the Eighth Circuit's jurisdiction have had Long COVID workers' compensation claims denied since 2022. The Missouri Attorney General's office indicated it is considering seeking rehearing en banc or petitioning the Supreme Court for review. The ruling creates a circuit split with the Fifth Circuit, which upheld a similar Louisiana policy in 2024, increasing the likelihood of Supreme Court review.

Legal analysts said the decision could pressure state legislatures to enact presumption statutes covering essential workers, similar to those already adopted in California, New York, and Illinois.

Why this matters: The ruling gives essential workers a federal forum to challenge blanket denials of Long COVID workers' compensation claims and may force states to overhaul how they evaluate pandemic-related occupational disease.

title: FDA Grants Breakthrough Device Designation to Implantable Baroreflex Activation System for Long COVID Patients With Refractory Dysautonomia
excerpt: The FDA has granted Breakthrough Device Designation to CVRx's Barostim Neo system for use in Long COVID patients with severe autonomic dysfunction unresponsive to pharmacologic therapy. The designation follows early data showing significant reductions in orthostatic symptoms.
body:
The Food and Drug Administration announced last week that it has granted Breakthrough Device Designation to an implantable baroreflex activation therapy system developed by CVRx for use in Long COVID patients with severe, treatment-refractory autonomic dysfunction. The designation, which accelerates FDA review and provides enhanced interaction with agency reviewers, is based on preliminary data from an investigator-initiated trial at Vanderbilt University showing substantial reductions in orthostatic symptoms in patients who had failed multiple pharmacologic therapies.

The device, already approved for heart failure and resistant hypertension under the brand name Barostim Neo, delivers electrical stimulation to baroreceptors in the carotid sinus, modulating autonomic nervous system output. In the 42-patient Vanderbilt pilot study, patients with severe Long COVID-associated postural tachycardia syndrome or orthostatic hypotension who had failed at least three prior therapies experienced a 51% reduction in orthostatic symptom scores and a 44% improvement in upright tolerance time at 24 weeks.

Patient Population and Trial Design

Eligible patients had documented autonomic dysfunction on tilt-table testing, symptoms persisting for at least 18 months, and had failed treatment with at least three of the following: beta-blockers, ivabradine, midodrine, droxidopa, fludrocortisone, or pyridostigmine. Approximately 8% to 12% of Long COVID patients with dysautonomia are estimated to have treatment-refractory symptoms that might qualify for device therapy.

The device is implanted subcutaneously in the chest with a lead placed at the carotid sinus. The procedure typically takes 90 minutes under general anesthesia and requires an overnight hospital stay. In the Vanderbilt cohort, procedural complications occurred in 7% of patients, including one hematoma requiring evacuation and two cases of transient hoarseness that resolved within eight weeks.

Pivotal Trial Enrollment

CVRx announced that a Phase 3 pivotal trial, RESTORE-AUTONOMIC, will enroll 340 patients across 22 U.S. sites beginning in September, with primary endpoints including change in Composite Autonomic Symptom Score at 12 months and improvement in the 10-minute stand test. If the trial succeeds, the company projects a potential FDA approval decision by late 2028.

Autonomic specialists emphasized that the therapy is not intended to replace conservative treatment but to provide an option for the small subset of patients whose lives have been rendered unmanageable by refractory dysautonomia. Insurance coverage remains a significant question, though Breakthrough Device Designation typically expedites subsequent Medicare coverage determinations under the Transitional Coverage for Emerging Technologies pathway.

Patient advocacy organizations welcomed the designation but urged that access not be limited to patients with substantial private insurance or geographic proximity to academic centers.

Why this matters: Device-based neuromodulation represents an emerging therapeutic frontier for the most severely disabled Long COVID patients who have exhausted pharmacologic options.

title: Phase 3 CLARITY-LC Trial Shows IVIG Reduces Small Fiber Neuropathy Symptoms in Long COVID by 45% Over 24 Weeks
excerpt: Intravenous immunoglobulin therapy produced significant improvements in pain, autonomic function, and skin biopsy nerve fiber density in Long COVID patients with biopsy-confirmed small fiber neuropathy, according to results from the Phase 3 CLARITY-LC trial published this week.
body:
Intravenous immunoglobulin therapy produced significant improvements in pain, autonomic function, and skin biopsy nerve fiber density in Long COVID patients with biopsy-confirmed small fiber neuropathy, according to results from the Phase 3 CLARITY-LC trial published in The Lancet Neurology. The multicenter study randomized 412 patients with punch biopsy-confirmed small fiber neuropathy and Long COVID lasting at least 12 months to receive either monthly IVIG at 2 g/kg or placebo infusions over 24 weeks.

The primary endpoint, a composite score combining the Small Fiber Neuropathy Symptoms Inventory Questionnaire and COMPASS-31 autonomic symptom scale, improved by 45% in the IVIG arm compared to 11% in the placebo arm. Repeat skin biopsies at week 24 showed a mean increase of 3.2 nerve fibers per millimeter in the treatment group, compared to no significant change in controls. Patients receiving IVIG also demonstrated measurable improvements in heart rate variability, sudomotor function on QSART testing, and orthostatic tolerance during tilt table evaluation.

Patient Selection and Biomarker Stratification

The trial enrolled only patients with objectively documented small fiber neuropathy, a subset estimated to represent between 40% and 60% of Long COVID patients with prominent pain, burning sensations, or autonomic symptoms. Investigators used standardized 3-millimeter punch biopsies from the distal leg with intraepidermal nerve fiber density counting performed at two blinded reference laboratories. Patients with alternative causes of neuropathy, including diabetes and B12 deficiency, were excluded.

Subgroup analysis showed the largest treatment effects in patients with elevated trisulfated heparan sulfate antibodies and those with fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 autoantibodies, both markers previously associated with autoimmune small fiber neuropathy. This finding supports growing evidence that a substantial fraction of Long COVID neuropathic symptoms reflect an antibody-mediated process amenable to immunomodulation.

Safety and Access Considerations

Adverse events were consistent with the known IVIG safety profile, with headache reported in 34% of treated patients, transient hypertension in 12%, and one case of aseptic meningitis that resolved without sequelae. No thromboembolic events occurred in the study population. However, the trial highlights significant access challenges: IVIG costs approximately $10,000 per monthly infusion, and current insurance coverage for Long COVID indications remains inconsistent despite the recent HHS diagnostic coverage guidance.

The manufacturer, Grifols, announced it will submit a supplemental biologics license application seeking specific FDA labeling for Long COVID-associated small fiber neuropathy by the fourth quarter of 2026. Several major payers, including UnitedHealthcare and Aetna, indicated they are reviewing coverage policies pending FDA action.

Why this matters: CLARITY-LC provides the strongest evidence to date that a well-defined immunologic subtype of Long COVID responds to established immunomodulatory therapy, potentially opening insurance-covered treatment pathways for hundreds of thousands of patients with debilitating neuropathic and autonomic symptoms.

title: Fifth Circuit Rules Long-Term Care Insurers Cannot Categorically Exclude Long COVID From Cognitive Impairment Benefit Triggers
excerpt: The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals held that long-term care insurance policies cannot categorically exclude Long COVID-related cognitive impairment from triggering benefit eligibility, ruling that such exclusions violate state unfair claims practices statutes when applied to policyholders meeting standard cognitive impairment criteria.
body:
The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals held on Tuesday that long-term care insurance policies cannot categorically exclude Long COVID-related cognitive impairment from triggering benefit eligibility, ruling in Whitfield v. Genworth Life Insurance that such exclusions violate Texas and Louisiana unfair claims practices statutes when applied to policyholders who otherwise meet standard cognitive impairment criteria. The decision resolves a growing conflict over insurer efforts to deny benefits to aging Long COVID patients seeking assisted living and in-home care coverage.

Plaintiff Margaret Whitfield, a 71-year-old former accountant, purchased her long-term care policy in 2008 and developed severe Long COVID after a 2023 infection. Neuropsychological testing documented impairment across memory, executive function, and processing speed sufficient to meet the policy's cognitive impairment trigger, which required deficits in short-term or long-term memory, orientation, or deductive or abstract reasoning. Genworth denied her claim under a 2024 policy amendment excluding conditions attributable to post-viral syndromes.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for a unanimous panel, Judge Catharina Haynes held that the exclusion was an impermissible post-hoc modification that discriminated based on etiology rather than functional status. The court emphasized that long-term care policies exist precisely to cover functional decline regardless of cause, and that carving out specific disease etiologies contradicts the fundamental purpose of the coverage. The panel further found that Genworth had not provided adequate notice or actuarial justification for the amendment as required under state insurance regulations.

The ruling explicitly rejected the insurer's argument that Long COVID cognitive impairment is inherently different from Alzheimer's disease or other covered conditions. Citing the Supreme Court's recent Henderson v. Acme Logistics decision, the panel noted that Long COVID cognitive dysfunction has been recognized as a legitimate cognitive disability supported by objective imaging findings, autonomic testing, and standardized neuropsychological assessment.

Industry-Wide Implications

The decision affects an estimated 7.5 million long-term care policyholders across the Fifth Circuit and creates persuasive authority for challenges in other jurisdictions. At least four other major long-term care insurers—including John Hancock, Mutual of Omaha, and Northwestern Long-Term Care—have adopted similar post-viral exclusion language in recent policy amendments. Industry analysts at AM Best estimate that expanded Long COVID cognitive claims could add between $2 billion and $4 billion in annual long-term care claim payouts nationally.

The National Association of Insurance Commissioners announced it will convene an emergency working group to develop model regulations addressing post-viral cognitive impairment claims. Consumer advocates, including the National Council on Aging, called on state insurance commissioners to conduct market conduct examinations of insurers that have denied Long COVID cognitive impairment claims since 2023.

Why this matters: With Long COVID producing measurable cognitive impairment in millions of older adults, the ruling ensures that long-standing insurance protections cannot be quietly stripped away through etiology-based exclusions targeting a single disabling condition.

title: CDC Establishes National Long COVID Surveillance System With Mandatory Reporting From 4,200 Sentinel Clinical Sites
excerpt: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention launched the National Long COVID Surveillance System this week, requiring mandatory case reporting from 4,200 sentinel clinical sites and establishing standardized case definitions, functional outcome measures, and demographic tracking across all 50 states.
body:
The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention launched the National Long COVID Surveillance System this week, requiring mandatory case reporting from 4,200 sentinel clinical sites and establishing standardized case definitions, functional outcome measures, and demographic tracking across all 50 states, U.S. territories, and tribal health systems. The system replaces the patchwork of voluntary surveys and administrative claims analyses that have produced widely divergent Long COVID prevalence estimates over the past four years.

The sentinel network includes all Long COVID specialty clinics receiving federal funding, academic medical centers with more than 500 beds, Federally Qualified Health Centers meeting specified caseload thresholds, and Veterans Health Administration facilities. Participating sites must report all patients meeting the WHO's updated ICD-11 Long COVID criteria within 30 days of diagnosis, including subtype classification under the 14-code framework adopted in 2025, functional status using the PROMIS-29 instrument, and treatment interventions initiated.

Standardized Data Elements and Quality Assurance

The system requires reporting of 47 core data elements, including symptom onset relative to acute infection, vaccination and reinfection history, employment status changes, and diagnostic testing results. Sites must submit deidentified longitudinal follow-up data at 6-month intervals for at least three years. The CDC has awarded contracts to five regional coordinating centers to provide training, data validation, and quality assurance across participating sites.

To address longstanding concerns about undercounting in marginalized populations, the system requires oversampling from FQHCs serving predominantly Medicaid and uninsured populations and includes specific data elements on language preference, immigration status barriers to care, and prior denial of Long COVID diagnoses. The NIH RECOVER pediatric findings from earlier this year highlighted significant demographic disparities in diagnosis rates that the new surveillance system is designed to detect and quantify.

Integration With Public Health Response

Data from the surveillance system will feed weekly public dashboards showing case counts, subtype distributions, and functional outcomes by state and demographic group. The CDC will use aggregated data to identify emerging clinical patterns, evaluate treatment effectiveness in real-world settings, and inform Medicare, Medicaid, and CDC Foundation funding allocations. State health departments will receive granular data to support local response planning, including workforce accommodations and educational supports.

Congressional appropriators allocated $340 million for the surveillance system through fiscal year 2028 as part of the pandemic preparedness supplement enacted in April. The American Medical Association endorsed the initiative but raised concerns about administrative burden on clinicians, prompting the CDC to develop electronic health record integration tools with the major EHR vendors including Epic, Cerner, and athenahealth.

Why this matters: After years of unreliable estimates hampering policy, research, and resource allocation, the U.S. finally has an infrastructure capable of producing authoritative data on how many Americans have Long COVID, what subtypes they experience, and whether interventions are actually improving their lives.

title: Phase 3 ILLUMINATE-LC Trial Shows Near-Infrared Photobiomodulation Helmet Reduces Long COVID Cognitive Symptoms by 38% Over 16 Weeks
excerpt: A transcranial near-infrared photobiomodulation device met its primary endpoint in a 612-patient Phase 3 trial, reducing cognitive symptom burden by 38% compared to sham treatment. The results, published in Lancet Neurology, support a noninvasive approach to long COVID brain fog.
body:
A transcranial near-infrared photobiomodulation helmet reduced long COVID cognitive symptom burden by 38% over 16 weeks compared to sham treatment in the Phase 3 ILLUMINATE-LC trial, according to results published this week in Lancet Neurology. The 612-patient multicenter study, conducted across 34 sites in the United States, Canada, and the Netherlands, is the largest randomized trial of photobiomodulation in any post-viral condition.

Participants wore the helmet, which delivers 1064-nm near-infrared light to cortical regions implicated in long COVID-related hypometabolism, for 20 minutes daily at home. The primary endpoint was change in the Cognitive Failures Questionnaire score at week 16. Active treatment produced a 38% reduction in cognitive symptom burden versus 11% in the sham arm, with statistically significant separation emerging by week 6. Secondary endpoints, including processing speed on the Symbol Digit Modalities Test and working memory on the n-back task, also favored active treatment.

Mechanism and Imaging Findings

A prespecified imaging substudy of 148 participants used FDG-PET to assess cortical glucose metabolism before and after treatment. Active-arm patients showed a 14% increase in frontal and parietal metabolic activity, regions previously identified as hypometabolic in long COVID cognitive dysfunction. Investigators attribute the effect to enhanced mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase activity, the proposed primary chromophore for near-infrared light at this wavelength.

Adverse events were limited to mild scalp warmth and transient headache, occurring in 8% of active-arm participants. There were no serious device-related adverse events, and adherence exceeded 91% across the 16-week treatment period.

Subgroup Analyses

Patients with documented choroid plexus inflammation on baseline 7T MRI and those with elevated neurofilament light chain showed the largest treatment effects, with cognitive symptom reductions of 47% and 44% respectively. Patients without inflammatory markers still showed meaningful benefit, suggesting the device may have effects independent of neuroinflammatory status. The trial enrolled patients with symptoms lasting at least six months; treatment effect did not vary significantly by symptom duration up to four years.

The device manufacturer has submitted a De Novo classification request to the FDA, with a decision expected by the fourth quarter of 2026. The American Academy of Neurology issued a provisional practice advisory noting that photobiomodulation may be considered for long COVID cognitive dysfunction in patients who have not responded to pharmacologic interventions, pending regulatory clearance.

Why this matters: A noninvasive, home-administered cognitive treatment with a favorable safety profile could expand options for the millions of long COVID patients whose brain fog remains refractory to pharmacologic therapy.

title: Third Circuit Rules Long COVID Patients Can Sue Under Section 1557 for Algorithmic Discrimination in Hospital Triage Systems
excerpt: The Third Circuit held that long COVID patients have a private right of action under Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act to challenge clinical decision support algorithms that systematically deprioritize their care. The ruling revives a class action against a major hospital system in Pennsylvania.
body:
The Third Circuit Court of Appeals ruled this week that long COVID patients can bring private lawsuits under Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act against hospital systems whose clinical decision support algorithms systematically deprioritize their care. The decision in Ramirez v. Keystone Health Network revives a putative class action that had been dismissed at the district court level and creates the first appellate precedent on algorithmic discrimination claims involving post-viral illness.

The plaintiffs alleged that Keystone's emergency department triage algorithm, which incorporated machine learning models trained on pre-pandemic acuity data, assigned long COVID patients presenting with chest pain, syncope, and dyspnea systematically lower priority scores than patients with identical vital signs but different documented diagnoses. Internal data produced in discovery showed long COVID patients waited an average of 4.7 hours longer for evaluation and were 38% more likely to be discharged without cardiac workup despite presenting with symptoms warranting evaluation under standard guidelines.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for a unanimous panel, Judge Cheryl Krause held that Section 1557's prohibition on disability discrimination extends to facially neutral algorithms that produce disparate impacts on protected populations, citing the Supreme Court's reasoning in Henderson v. Acme Logistics regarding long COVID's status as a cognitive and physical disability under federal antidiscrimination law. The court rejected Keystone's argument that the algorithm's outputs reflected clinical judgment immune from civil rights scrutiny, finding that the deployment of automated decision tools by federally funded healthcare entities falls squarely within the statute's coverage.

The court also held that plaintiffs need not prove discriminatory intent to state a claim, adopting the disparate impact framework that HHS reaffirmed in its 2024 Section 1557 final rule. Plaintiffs must show that the algorithm produces statistically significant differential treatment of long COVID patients and that the defendant cannot demonstrate the disparity is justified by legitimate clinical necessity that could not be achieved through less discriminatory means.

Broader Implications

The decision is the first federal appellate ruling to apply algorithmic accountability principles to post-viral illness and is expected to influence pending litigation in at least seven other circuits involving similar claims against insurers, hospital systems, and disability determination contractors. HHS Office for Civil Rights filed an amicus brief supporting the plaintiffs and is reportedly preparing guidance specifically addressing algorithmic discrimination against patients with chronic post-infectious conditions.

The American Hospital Association expressed concern that the ruling will require extensive auditing of clinical decision support systems, while patient advocacy groups called it a watershed moment for accountability in healthcare AI.

Why this matters: As hospitals increasingly rely on algorithmic tools for triage, resource allocation, and treatment recommendations, this ruling establishes that long COVID patients have legal recourse when those systems encode biases that compromise their care.

title: Department of Education Issues Final Rule Requiring K-12 Schools to Provide Individualized Education Programs for Students With Long COVID Under IDEA
excerpt: The Department of Education finalized a rule clarifying that long COVID qualifies as an 'other health impairment' under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act, requiring schools to provide IEPs and related services. The rule takes effect for the 2026-2027 school year.
body:
The U.S. Department of Education issued a final rule today clarifying that long COVID qualifies as an 'other health impairment' under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), requiring public schools to evaluate affected students for special education services and provide individualized education programs (IEPs) when eligibility criteria are met. The rule takes effect for the 2026-2027 school year and applies to all public school districts receiving federal funding.

The rule responds to widespread reports that school districts have inconsistently classified long COVID, with some treating it under Section 504 accommodation plans only, others requiring extensive documentation before any services are provided, and many refusing to consider IDEA eligibility absent a specific medical diagnosis from a pediatric subspecialist. The NIH RECOVER-PEDIATRIC findings released earlier this year, documenting that 4.3% of U.S. children meet criteria for long COVID, accelerated regulatory action.

Key Requirements

Under the final rule, school districts must initiate special education evaluations within 30 days of a parent or physician request when a student has documented long COVID symptoms affecting school performance, attendance, or participation. Evaluations must specifically assess fatigue, post-exertional symptom exacerbation, cognitive function, autonomic symptoms, and pain, using instruments validated for pediatric post-viral illness rather than relying solely on traditional learning disability batteries.

IEPs for students with long COVID must address energy management, including modified schedules and rest periods; cognitive accommodations such as extended time and reduced cognitive load; physical education modifications that account for post-exertional malaise; and home or hospital instruction provisions for students unable to attend school in person. The rule specifically prohibits attendance policies that penalize absences related to long COVID symptom exacerbation when documented by a healthcare provider.

Implementation Support

The Department of Education is providing $340 million in implementation grants to state education agencies, with priority for districts serving high-poverty communities and those with documented gaps in pediatric long COVID services. The rule also requires state education agencies to collect and report data on long COVID identification, services provided, and educational outcomes, with the first reports due in November 2027.

The rule was developed in consultation with the Department of Health and Human Services and incorporates clinical criteria aligned with the WHO's updated ICD-11 framework adopted earlier this year. Disability rights organizations praised the rule for ending what they described as a patchwork of inconsistent district practices, while some school administrator associations warned of capacity constraints and called for additional federal funding beyond the announced grants.

Why this matters: Hundreds of thousands of children with long COVID have struggled to access appropriate educational services, and this rule establishes a uniform federal floor that should reduce the burden on families to fight district by district for accommodations their children need.

title: Phase 3 LIBERATE-LC Trial Shows Vagus Nerve Stimulation Device Reduces Long COVID Symptom Burden by 39% Over 24 Weeks
excerpt: A transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation device met primary and secondary endpoints in the 612-patient LIBERATE-LC trial, with the manufacturer filing for FDA De Novo authorization in May 2026.
body:
A non-invasive vagus nerve stimulation device reduced overall Long COVID symptom burden by 39% compared with sham stimulation in the Phase 3 LIBERATE-LC trial, with results published in The Lancet Neurology last week. The 612-patient multicenter trial randomized adults with Long COVID and documented autonomic dysfunction to either 30 minutes of daily transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation (taVNS) or an identical sham device for 24 weeks.

Participants in the active treatment arm showed statistically significant improvements across fatigue, post-exertional malaise, cognitive function, and gastrointestinal symptom domains as measured by the validated PASC Symptom Severity Index. Heart rate variability, a marker of parasympathetic tone, improved by an average of 28% in the treatment arm versus 4% in sham. Notably, 47% of treated participants achieved a 50% or greater reduction in PEM episodes, compared with 19% of controls.

Mechanism and Patient Selection

Investigators selected participants based on reduced heart rate variability and elevated inflammatory cytokines at baseline, hypothesizing that vagal afferent stimulation would dampen systemic inflammation through the cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway. Post-treatment blood samples confirmed significant reductions in IL-6, TNF-alpha, and CRP among responders. The trial also documented reduced sympathetic overactivity on tilt-table testing in 58% of treated participants.

Lead investigator Dr. Helena Vasquez of Mount Sinai noted that responders tended to share a specific autonomic phenotype characterized by low vagal tone and elevated catecholamines. Non-responders were more likely to have predominantly immunological or microvascular disease features, suggesting taVNS may be most effective as part of a phenotype-guided treatment strategy rather than as monotherapy for all Long COVID patients.

Safety and Regulatory Path

Adverse events were mild and limited to transient ear discomfort and skin irritation at the electrode site. No serious device-related events occurred. The manufacturer, Parasym Health, submitted a De Novo classification request to the FDA in May 2026, seeking authorization for Long COVID with autonomic dysfunction as a specific indication. Industry analysts expect a decision by early 2027.

The trial results have prompted several large academic medical centers, including Cleveland Clinic and Stanford, to incorporate taVNS into existing Long COVID treatment protocols on a research-use basis. Insurance coverage remains limited pending FDA authorization, with most patients currently paying $600 to $900 out of pocket for the device.

Why this matters: If authorized, taVNS would become the first FDA-cleared device specifically indicated for Long COVID treatment, offering a non-pharmacological, home-based option for the substantial subset of patients whose illness is driven by autonomic dysfunction.

title: NIH RECOVER-GUT Trial Finds Fecal Microbiota Transplantation Reduces Long COVID Gastrointestinal and Fatigue Symptoms by 42% in Patients With Dysbiosis
excerpt: Encapsulated fecal microbiota transplantation produced significant symptom reduction in a 340-patient NIH-funded trial of Long COVID patients with documented gut dysbiosis, with benefits sustained at 24 weeks.
body:
Encapsulated fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) reduced gastrointestinal symptoms and fatigue by 42% in Long COVID patients with documented gut dysbiosis, according to results from the NIH-funded RECOVER-GUT trial published in Cell Host & Microbe this week. The 340-patient randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial enrolled adults with Long COVID lasting at least 12 months and evidence of reduced microbial diversity on stool metagenomic sequencing.

Participants received either oral FMT capsules from screened healthy donors or placebo capsules, administered as an initial loading dose followed by weekly maintenance doses for 12 weeks. Symptoms were assessed using the Gastrointestinal Symptom Rating Scale and the Chalder Fatigue Scale at baseline, 12 weeks, and 24 weeks.

Restoration of Microbial Diversity

Stool sequencing showed that treated participants experienced substantial restoration of bacterial species commonly depleted in Long COVID, including Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Bifidobacterium adolescentis, and Roseburia intestinalis. These short-chain fatty acid-producing bacteria are known to support intestinal barrier function and modulate systemic inflammation. Levels of circulating lipopolysaccharide, a marker of intestinal permeability, dropped by an average of 38% in the FMT arm.

Investigators also documented improvements in serum tryptophan and kynurenine pathway metabolites, which have been implicated in neuroinflammation and fatigue. Lead author Dr. Siew Ng of the Chinese University of Hong Kong, who collaborated with the NIH RECOVER consortium, noted that the dual improvement in gut and systemic symptoms supports the gut-brain axis hypothesis of Long COVID pathogenesis.

Subgroup Analysis and Safety

The most pronounced benefits were observed in participants with the lowest baseline microbial diversity scores and highest pre-treatment intestinal permeability markers. Among this severe dysbiosis subgroup, symptom reduction exceeded 50% and was sustained through 24 weeks of follow-up. Patients with predominantly autonomic or cardiovascular phenotypes showed minimal benefit, reinforcing the importance of stratifying Long COVID by underlying mechanism.

Safety outcomes were favorable. Mild abdominal bloating occurred in 18% of FMT recipients and resolved within two weeks. No serious adverse events were attributed to FMT, and rigorous donor screening prevented any transmission of infectious agents.

Regulatory and Access Considerations

The FDA currently regulates FMT under enforcement discretion for Clostridioides difficile infection and has approved two oral microbiome products for that indication. The RECOVER-GUT investigators have requested a Type B meeting with FDA to discuss a regulatory pathway for Long COVID indication. Several stool banks, including OpenBiome, have indicated readiness to scale production if approved.

Why this matters: The trial provides the strongest evidence to date that gut microbiome restoration can directly improve Long COVID symptoms, opening a new therapeutic avenue for the substantial subset of patients whose illness involves gut dysbiosis and intestinal barrier dysfunction.

title: Phase 3 REVIVE-LC Trial Shows Inspiratory Muscle Training Device Improves Dyspnea and Exercise Capacity in Long COVID by 36%
excerpt: A multicenter Phase 3 trial published in The Lancet Respiratory Medicine found that a prescription-grade inspiratory muscle training device improved dyspnea scores and six-minute walk distance by 36% in long COVID patients with persistent breathlessness.
body:
A multicenter Phase 3 trial published in The Lancet Respiratory Medicine found that a prescription-grade inspiratory muscle training (IMT) device improved dyspnea scores and six-minute walk distance by 36% in long COVID patients with persistent breathlessness. The REVIVE-LC trial enrolled 612 patients across 28 sites in the United States, United Kingdom, and Germany who had documented exertional dyspnea persisting more than 12 months after acute SARS-CoV-2 infection but no evidence of structural pulmonary disease on imaging.

Participants were randomized to either daily home use of the POWERbreathe K5 device at 50–60% of maximal inspiratory pressure or a sham device delivering minimal resistance. After 16 weeks, the active group showed a mean improvement of 38 meters on the six-minute walk test compared to 11 meters in the sham group, along with a 2.3-point reduction on the Modified Medical Research Council dyspnea scale. Post-exertional malaise frequency, measured by the DSQ-PEM questionnaire, also declined by 28% in the treatment arm, a finding that surprised investigators who had not anticipated benefits beyond pulmonary mechanics.

Mechanistic Findings

Secondary analyses suggested that IMT may improve autonomic balance in addition to strengthening the diaphragm. Heart rate variability metrics improved significantly in the active group, and a subset of 120 patients who underwent cardiopulmonary exercise testing showed increased oxygen pulse and reduced ventilatory inefficiency (VE/VCO2 slope). Lead investigator Dr. Hannah Reiser of the University of Munich noted that diaphragmatic dysfunction had been documented in roughly half of long COVID patients with breathlessness in prior imaging studies, and that strengthening inspiratory muscles likely reduces the work of breathing and downstream sympathetic activation.

Adverse events were minimal, with the most common complaint being transient throat irritation. No serious adverse events were attributed to the device.

Clinical Implementation

The American Thoracic Society issued an updated clinical practice statement within weeks of publication, recommending IMT as a first-line non-pharmacological intervention for long COVID patients with persistent dyspnea after structural disease has been excluded. CMS announced that it is reviewing coverage determinations for prescription IMT devices, which currently cost between $300 and $500 and are not consistently reimbursed. Several major insurers, including Aetna and Cigna, have already added IMT to covered durable medical equipment lists for long COVID patients with documented exertional limitation.

The trial protocol called for 30 minutes of daily training divided into two sessions, and adherence exceeded 85% throughout the study period, suggesting the intervention is feasible for patients who often struggle with energy-intensive rehabilitation programs.

Why this matters: A low-cost, home-based device with strong Phase 3 evidence offers long COVID patients with breathlessness a scalable treatment option that can be deployed immediately without specialty referral.

title: Tenth Circuit Rules State Unemployment Agencies Cannot Deny Benefits to Long COVID Workers Who Quit Due to Failure to Accommodate
excerpt: The Tenth Circuit ruled that state unemployment insurance agencies must treat resignation due to an employer's failure to accommodate long COVID as 'good cause attributable to the employer,' preserving eligibility for benefits.
body:
The Tenth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled on May 18, 2026 that state unemployment insurance agencies must treat a long COVID worker's resignation due to an employer's failure to accommodate as 'good cause attributable to the employer,' preserving eligibility for benefits. The decision in Calderón v. Colorado Department of Labor and Employment resolves a circuit-level question that has produced inconsistent outcomes for tens of thousands of workers forced out of jobs when employers refused to modify schedules, workstations, or remote work arrangements.

The plaintiff, Maria Calderón, a hospital billing specialist diagnosed with long COVID in late 2023, requested a hybrid telework schedule after developing severe orthostatic intolerance and post-exertional malaise. Her employer denied the request, citing department-wide return-to-office policy, and Calderón resigned after attempting onsite work caused multiple syncopal episodes. The Colorado unemployment division denied her claim, finding she had voluntarily quit without good cause, a decision affirmed by state administrative review.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for a unanimous panel, Judge Carolyn McHugh held that an employer's failure to provide a reasonable accommodation under the ADA constitutes a substantial change in working conditions that gives rise to constructive discharge for unemployment purposes. The court rejected the state's argument that unemployment eligibility should be evaluated independently of ADA obligations, finding that 'a worker cannot be deemed to have voluntarily abandoned employment when the employer has unlawfully refused to maintain the conditions necessary for that worker to remain.'

The opinion explicitly addressed long COVID, noting that the Supreme Court's decision in Henderson v. Acme Logistics established cognitive and autonomic manifestations as protected disabilities, and that workers should not be forced to choose between deteriorating health and forfeiture of safety-net benefits. The court remanded with instructions to award Calderón retroactive benefits with interest.

Broader Impact

The ruling binds Colorado, Kansas, New Mexico, Oklahoma, Utah, and Wyoming, and is expected to be cited in pending litigation in the Fifth and Eighth Circuits, where similar denials have been challenged. The National Employment Law Project estimates that approximately 47,000 long COVID workers nationally have been denied unemployment benefits after resigning under analogous circumstances since 2022.

State unemployment administrators in the affected states have begun reviewing pending claims and issuing revised guidance to adjudicators. The Department of Labor's Employment and Training Administration signaled in a June 1 advisory that it is considering federal guidance that would harmonize the standard nationally.

Employer trade groups, including the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, criticized the decision as expanding state benefit obligations without legislative authorization and have indicated possible support for a petition for rehearing en banc.

Why this matters: The decision closes a gap that has left long COVID workers without income when employers refused to accommodate their conditions, aligning unemployment law with disability protections.

title: WHO Adopts Updated ICD-11 Coding Framework With 14 Distinct Long COVID Subtype Codes for Clinical and Epidemiological Use
excerpt: The World Health Organization adopted an updated ICD-11 coding framework that replaces the single 'post COVID-19 condition' code with 14 distinct subtype codes capturing cardiovascular, neurological, immunological, and other phenotypes.
body:
The World Health Organization adopted an updated ICD-11 coding framework on May 22, 2026 that replaces the single 'post COVID-19 condition' code (RA02) with 14 distinct subtype codes capturing cardiovascular, neurological, immunological, respiratory, gastrointestinal, and other recognized phenotypes of long COVID. The revision, effective January 1, 2027, follows three years of consultation with the WHO's Post COVID-19 Condition Clinical Case Definition Working Group and is the first major update to long COVID coding since the original code was introduced in 2021.

The New Code Structure

The expanded framework retains RA02 as the parent code but adds specific child codes including RA02.1 (post-COVID dysautonomia and POTS), RA02.2 (post-COVID cognitive dysfunction), RA02.3 (post-COVID post-exertional malaise/ME-CFS phenotype), RA02.4 (post-COVID small fiber neuropathy), RA02.5 (post-COVID microvascular dysfunction), RA02.6 (post-COVID interstitial lung disease), RA02.7 (post-COVID mast cell activation syndrome), RA02.8 (post-COVID immune dysregulation with autoantibody involvement), RA02.9 (post-COVID gastrointestinal dysmotility), RA02.A (post-COVID anosmia/parosmia), RA02.B (post-COVID cardiac inflammation), RA02.C (post-COVID renal dysfunction), RA02.D (post-COVID endocrine dysfunction), and RA02.E (pediatric post-COVID condition).

Each code includes diagnostic criteria, required documentation, and exclusion criteria intended to harmonize clinical practice across jurisdictions. The WHO also published a companion clinical guide running over 200 pages that provides phenotype definitions, recommended workup, and references to relevant biomarker studies.

U.S. Implementation

The CDC's National Center for Health Statistics confirmed that the United States will incorporate the new codes into ICD-10-CM through corresponding U-series codes effective October 1, 2026, three months ahead of the WHO timeline. CMS has indicated that the new codes will be incorporated into Medicare and Medicaid billing systems simultaneously and that quality measure reporting under the recently established National Long COVID Quality Measure Set will transition to subtype-specific stratification beginning in 2027.

Clinicians and advocacy organizations have largely welcomed the change. The Long COVID Alliance called it 'a long-overdue recognition that long COVID is not a single illness but a constellation of distinct, mechanistically different conditions requiring tailored care.' Researchers anticipate that more granular coding will dramatically improve epidemiological surveillance, enable more precise inclusion criteria for clinical trials, and reduce the diagnostic ambiguity that has plagued insurance authorization disputes.

Some health systems have raised concerns about administrative burden, particularly for primary care clinicians who may struggle to determine appropriate subtype assignment without specialist input. The WHO guide includes simplified decision trees and notes that multiple subtypes can be coded concurrently when patients meet criteria for more than one phenotype.

Why this matters: Subtype-specific coding will sharpen clinical care, research, and reimbursement by treating long COVID as the heterogeneous group of conditions it actually is rather than a single diagnosis.

title: Phase 3 CORAL-LC Trial Shows Temelimab Reduces Long COVID Symptoms by 40% in Patients With Elevated HERV-W Envelope Protein
excerpt: Results from the international Phase 3 CORAL-LC trial demonstrate that the monoclonal antibody temelimab, which neutralizes the human endogenous retrovirus-W envelope protein, reduced Long COVID symptom burden by 40% over 24 weeks. The findings validate a novel mechanistic pathway involving retroviral reactivation in post-acute SARS-CoV-2 sequelae.
body:
Results from the Phase 3 CORAL-LC trial, published this week in The Lancet, show that temelimab — a humanized monoclonal antibody targeting the human endogenous retrovirus-W (HERV-W) envelope protein — reduced Long COVID symptom burden by 40% over 24 weeks in patients with elevated baseline HERV-W antigen levels. The trial randomized 612 patients across 47 sites in Europe and North America and represents the first large-scale validation of retroviral reactivation as a therapeutic target in post-acute SARS-CoV-2 sequelae.

Investigators stratified patients by serum HERV-W envelope protein concentration at enrollment, with all participants demonstrating levels at least two standard deviations above healthy controls. Patients received monthly intravenous infusions of temelimab at 36 mg/kg or placebo. The primary endpoint — change in the Composite Long COVID Symptom Score — favored temelimab with a between-group difference of 18.4 points (p<0.001). Secondary endpoints showed significant improvements in fatigue measured by the Chalder scale, cognitive function on the Cogstate battery, and post-exertional malaise frequency.

Mechanistic Findings

HERV-W envelope protein, also called pHERV-W ENV, has been implicated in neuroinflammatory disease through TLR4-mediated microglial activation. Translational substudies in CORAL-LC found that responders showed a 62% reduction in serum neurofilament light chain and a 48% decrease in soluble CD14, suggesting attenuation of innate immune activation. PET imaging in a 78-patient substudy demonstrated reduced TSPO binding in the thalamus and brainstem of treated patients, consistent with decreased neuroinflammation.

Lead investigator Dr. Hervé Perron of the University of Lyon stated that the findings confirm a long-hypothesized link between coronavirus infection and reactivation of dormant endogenous retroviral elements. Approximately 31% of screened Long COVID patients met the HERV-W elevation threshold for enrollment, suggesting the mechanism applies to a substantial subpopulation rather than all patients.

Safety and Regulatory Path

Adverse events were comparable between arms, with infusion reactions occurring in 7.2% of temelimab recipients versus 5.1% on placebo. No opportunistic infections or malignancies were reported during the 24-week period. The manufacturer, GeNeuro, announced it will submit a Biologics License Application to the FDA in the third quarter of 2026, with European Medicines Agency filing to follow. The FDA previously granted Fast Track designation in late 2025 based on Phase 2 data.

Clinicians cautioned that HERV-W antigen testing is not yet widely available outside research settings, and routine implementation will require companion diagnostic development. Several reference laboratories have announced plans to validate clinical assays in anticipation of approval.

Why this matters: Temelimab offers the first targeted therapy aimed at retroviral reactivation in Long COVID, opening a mechanistically distinct treatment pathway for a defined patient subpopulation that has not responded to immunomodulators or antivirals.

title: Seventh Circuit Rules Employers Cannot Require In-Person Functional Capacity Evaluations as Condition of Long COVID Disability Benefits
excerpt: The Seventh Circuit held that requiring Long COVID claimants to undergo in-person functional capacity evaluations that trigger post-exertional malaise violates the ADA and constitutes an unreasonable claims practice under ERISA. The decision creates binding precedent across Illinois, Indiana, and Wisconsin.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit ruled yesterday in Reynolds v. Continental Casualty Co. that disability insurers and self-funded employer plans cannot require Long COVID claimants to undergo standard in-person functional capacity evaluations (FCEs) as a condition of receiving or continuing long-term disability benefits when such testing predictably triggers post-exertional malaise. The decision creates binding precedent across Illinois, Indiana, and Wisconsin and aligns the Seventh Circuit with growing federal jurisprudence treating exertional testing in post-exertional malaise conditions as inherently unreliable and discriminatory.

The case arose when plaintiff Megan Reynolds, a former emergency department nurse with documented Long COVID and post-exertional malaise, was required to complete a six-hour FCE involving lifting, treadmill walking, and sustained standing as a condition of continued benefits. Reynolds experienced a severe symptom crash lasting nine weeks and submitted physician documentation showing measurable worsening of cognitive and cardiovascular function. The insurer terminated benefits citing FCE results showing she could perform sedentary work.

Court's Reasoning

Writing for a unanimous three-judge panel, Judge Diane Wood held that the FCE protocol violated both the Americans with Disabilities Act and ERISA's fiduciary duty requirements. The court found that standard FCE methodology measures only acute single-day capacity and fails to capture the delayed, multi-day symptom exacerbation that defines post-exertional malaise. Relying on the WHO's 2026 global guideline against graded exercise testing in post-exertional symptom conditions, the panel concluded that single-session FCE results in this population are not substantial evidence supporting benefit denial.

The court further held that requiring a claimant to undergo testing that the insurer knew or should have known would cause harm violates the ADA's prohibition on disability-related inquiries that are not job-related and consistent with business necessity. The opinion specifically endorsed two-day cardiopulmonary exercise testing protocols as the medically appropriate alternative when objective functional measurement is required, while emphasizing that physician attestation and validated symptom inventories may suffice in many cases.

Implications for Claims Administration

Disability insurance industry analysts predict the ruling will prompt national changes in FCE protocols, as carriers typically maintain uniform procedures across jurisdictions. The American Council of Life Insurers issued a statement noting the decision will require substantial revision to claims handling guidelines. Plaintiff-side ERISA attorneys are already citing Reynolds in pending cases across other circuits, and a similar case is currently before the Second Circuit.

The Department of Labor's Employee Benefits Security Administration indicated it is reviewing the decision and may issue updated guidance on reasonable claims practices for post-exertional malaise conditions later this year.

Why this matters: The ruling closes a major loophole insurers have used to terminate Long COVID disability claims and establishes that test methodology must be appropriate to the underlying condition rather than a one-size-fits-all standard.

title: NIH RECOVER-VASCULAR Trial Finds Endothelial Dysfunction Persists in 71% of Long COVID Patients and Predicts Cardiovascular Events Within Three Years
excerpt: The NIH RECOVER-VASCULAR observational cohort, published in Circulation, found that 71% of Long COVID patients show measurable endothelial dysfunction at two years post-infection, and that abnormal flow-mediated dilation independently predicts a 2.8-fold increased risk of major adverse cardiovascular events. The findings reshape cardiovascular risk stratification for the post-acute COVID population.
body:
The NIH RECOVER-VASCULAR study, published this week in Circulation, found that 71% of Long COVID patients exhibit measurable endothelial dysfunction at two years post-infection and that abnormal flow-mediated dilation (FMD) independently predicts a 2.8-fold increased risk of major adverse cardiovascular events within three years. The prospective cohort enrolled 3,247 Long COVID patients and 1,108 recovered controls across 38 RECOVER sites, with serial vascular assessments beginning at six months post-infection.

Investigators measured brachial artery FMD, peripheral arterial tonometry, and circulating endothelial biomarkers including soluble VCAM-1, ICAM-1, syndecan-1, and von Willebrand factor. Long COVID patients showed a mean FMD of 4.1% compared with 7.8% in recovered controls, with values below the 5.5% threshold conferring substantially elevated cardiovascular risk. Glycocalyx degradation markers were elevated in 68% of symptomatic patients, suggesting persistent microvascular injury.

Clinical Event Outcomes

During median follow-up of 31 months, Long COVID patients with impaired FMD experienced 142 major adverse cardiovascular events including myocardial infarction, stroke, cardiovascular death, and new heart failure diagnosis, compared with 23 events in patients with preserved endothelial function. The hazard ratio remained significant after adjustment for traditional risk factors including age, hypertension, diabetes, lipid profile, and smoking status. Notably, 38% of events occurred in patients under age 55 with no prior cardiovascular history.

Subgroup analysis revealed that patients with concurrent microclot burden, as measured by fibrinaloid imaging, faced the highest risk with a hazard ratio of 4.3. The combination of endothelial dysfunction and elevated D-dimer identified a particularly high-risk phenotype that investigators have proposed designating as Long COVID vascular syndrome.

Implications for Screening and Treatment

Lead investigator Dr. Sanjay Rajagopalan of Case Western Reserve called for incorporation of vascular function testing into routine Long COVID follow-up, noting that current cardiovascular risk calculators substantially underestimate event probability in this population. The American Heart Association has convened a writing group to develop a scientific statement on cardiovascular risk assessment in post-acute COVID, expected later this year.

The study also identified several modifiable factors associated with endothelial recovery, including statin therapy, SGLT2 inhibitor use, and structured pacing programs. A nested intervention substudy showed that empagliflozin improved FMD by 1.8 percentage points over 12 weeks compared with 0.3 points on placebo, providing preliminary evidence for targeted vascular therapy. Several Phase 3 trials of vascular-protective interventions in Long COVID are now enrolling.

The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services indicated it is reviewing whether to expand coverage for endothelial function testing in Long COVID patients based on the new data.

Why this matters: The findings establish Long COVID as an independent cardiovascular risk state requiring proactive screening and intervention, with implications for tens of millions of patients who otherwise appear to be at low traditional risk.
