title: Phase 3 RECOVER-NEURO Trial Shows Guanfacine Plus N-Acetylcysteine Combination Reduces Long COVID Brain Fog by 44% Over 12 Weeks
excerpt: The NIH-sponsored Phase 3 RECOVER-NEURO trial demonstrated that a combination of low-dose guanfacine and N-acetylcysteine significantly improved cognitive function in Long COVID patients with persistent brain fog. Results were published in The Lancet Neurology this month.
body:
The Phase 3 RECOVER-NEURO trial, published this week in The Lancet Neurology, found that a daily combination of low-dose guanfacine (1 mg) and N-acetylcysteine (600 mg twice daily) reduced cognitive dysfunction in Long COVID patients by 44% over 12 weeks compared with placebo.

The multicenter trial enrolled 612 adults with persistent brain fog lasting at least six months after acute SARS-CoV-2 infection. Participants were randomized to active combination therapy or matching placebo. The primary endpoint was change in the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score combined with performance on the Cogstate computerized battery, which measures attention, working memory, and processing speed.

Building on Yale Pilot Data

The combination was originally tested by Arman Fesharaki-Zadeh and colleagues at Yale in a 2023 open-label pilot study showing promising cognitive improvements. The mechanism is thought to involve guanfacine's strengthening of prefrontal cortical network connectivity through alpha-2A adrenergic receptors, paired with N-acetylcysteine's reduction of neuroinflammation and oxidative stress. RECOVER-NEURO represents the first adequately powered randomized confirmation.

Patients receiving the combination showed a mean MoCA score improvement of 3.8 points, compared with 0.9 in the placebo arm. Improvements were most pronounced in executive function and sustained attention domains. Functional measures, including the Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System (PROMIS) Cognitive Function scale, showed parallel gains. Approximately 58% of treated participants reported being able to return to part-time or full-time cognitive work activities by week 12, compared with 22% in the placebo group.

Safety and Tolerability

Adverse events were generally mild. The most common were transient drowsiness (18%), dry mouth (12%), and mild hypotension (9%), all consistent with guanfacine's known profile. Six participants discontinued for orthostatic symptoms, an important consideration given the high prevalence of POTS in this population. Investigators recommended baseline autonomic screening before initiation and slow titration in patients with documented dysautonomia.

Notably, benefits persisted at a 4-week post-treatment follow-up assessment, suggesting durable effects beyond active dosing. Subgroup analysis showed the strongest response in patients under age 55 and those with elevated baseline neurofilament light chain levels, hinting at a potential biomarker-guided approach.

The FDA has indicated it will consider an expedited supplemental new drug application given the unmet need, though both drugs are already widely available as generics. Several Long COVID specialty clinics have signaled they will incorporate the protocol into care pathways once professional society guidance issues, expected later this year from the American Academy of Neurology.

Why this matters: With cognitive dysfunction remaining one of the most disabling and treatment-resistant features of Long COVID, a generic combination regimen showing durable, clinically meaningful improvement could be rapidly deployed at low cost across millions of affected patients.

title: Sixth Circuit Rules ERISA Plans Cannot Apply Mental Health Exclusions to Deny Long-Term Disability Claims Based on Long COVID Cognitive Symptoms
excerpt: The Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals held that ERISA-governed long-term disability plans may not invoke mental and nervous disorder limitations to cap or deny benefits for Long COVID claimants whose disability stems from cognitive dysfunction. The ruling resolves a split among district courts.
body:
In Pemberton v. Unum Life Insurance Co. of America, decided this week, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit ruled that ERISA-governed long-term disability plans cannot apply mental and nervous disorder limitations—typically capping benefits at 24 months—to deny or curtail claims based on cognitive symptoms of Long COVID.

The plaintiff, a former hospital pharmacist in Cincinnati, became disabled in 2022 after a SARS-CoV-2 infection left her with severe brain fog, dysautonomia, and post-exertional malaise. After 24 months of benefits, Unum invoked the plan's mental health limitation, characterizing her cognitive impairment as a behavioral health condition. The district court sided with Unum. The Sixth Circuit reversed.

Cognitive Impairment Is a Physical Manifestation

Writing for a unanimous three-judge panel, Judge Eric Murphy held that Long COVID cognitive dysfunction has a documented organic basis—including neuroinflammation, microvascular abnormalities, and choroid plexus changes visible on advanced imaging—and therefore cannot be reclassified as a mental disorder simply because symptoms manifest as cognitive complaints. The court relied heavily on the JAMA Neurology choroid plexus inflammation findings and Nature Medicine mitochondrial fragmentation studies, citing them as evidence that Long COVID cognitive symptoms have a measurable physical substrate.

"A plan administrator may not transform a neurological condition into a psychiatric one by labeling its symptoms," Judge Murphy wrote. "The inquiry is whether the underlying etiology is physical, not whether the patient's complaints involve cognition or mood."

Resolving a District Court Split

The decision resolves a growing split among federal district courts. Courts in the Northern District of Illinois and Southern District of New York had ruled that mental health limitations could apply to Long COVID cognitive claims, while courts in California and Massachusetts had rejected such applications. The Sixth Circuit's ruling provides the first appellate guidance and is expected to influence pending cases in the Second, Seventh, and Ninth Circuits.

Disability rights advocates hailed the decision as critical to the financial survival of tens of thousands of Long COVID patients on private LTD coverage. Industry estimates suggest 40-60% of Long COVID LTD claims involve cognitive components that insurers have attempted to channel into mental health limitations, which typically cap benefits long before patients reach Medicare or Social Security eligibility.

Unum announced it is evaluating whether to seek en banc review or petition the Supreme Court. In the meantime, several major LTD carriers have reportedly begun reviewing their claims handling protocols. Plaintiff attorneys expect a wave of reopened claims, particularly for claimants whose benefits were terminated at the 24-month mark in the past two years.

Why this matters: The ruling closes a major loophole insurers have used to cut off Long COVID disability benefits and could restore years of income protection to thousands of patients with cognitive impairment.

title: HHS and Department of Labor Issue Joint Guidance Requiring Group Health Plans to Cover FDA-Cleared Long COVID Diagnostics Without Prior Authorization
excerpt: The Department of Health and Human Services and Department of Labor jointly issued enforcement guidance requiring ERISA-governed and ACA-marketplace health plans to cover FDA-cleared Long COVID diagnostic tests without prior authorization or step therapy requirements. The guidance takes effect August 1, 2026.
body:
HHS and the Department of Labor jointly issued binding sub-regulatory guidance this week requiring group health plans and individual market insurers to cover FDA-cleared Long COVID diagnostic tests without prior authorization, step therapy, or medical necessity review beyond a treating clinician's order. The guidance takes effect August 1, 2026.

The directive specifically names the recently approved multi-analyte immune signature blood test, the wearable continuous autonomic monitor, and CMS-covered tilt table and autonomic function studies. It also extends to advanced imaging modalities including 7T MRI for choroid plexus assessment and PET imaging of vagus nerve inflammation where ordered by specialists at designated Long COVID clinics.

Closing the Diagnostic Access Gap

The action responds to mounting evidence that diagnostic access remains a major bottleneck in Long COVID care despite the proliferation of validated testing options. A March 2026 HHS report found that 61% of patients prescribed an FDA-cleared Long COVID diagnostic faced initial insurance denials, with average delays of 47 days before testing could proceed. Patients in commercial plans faced denials at substantially higher rates than Medicare beneficiaries.

The guidance is grounded in the Affordable Care Act's preventive services framework as interpreted through the September 2025 USPSTF Grade B recommendation supporting Long COVID screening in symptomatic post-COVID patients, as well as parity protections under the Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act applied to neurological symptoms. The agencies invoked their authority under ERISA Section 502 and ACA Section 2719 to mandate prompt internal review and external appeal rights for any coverage dispute.

Enforcement Mechanisms

Plans found in violation may face civil monetary penalties of up to $110 per affected enrollee per day under ERISA, plus restitution for out-of-pocket costs patients incurred when forced to pay cash. DOL's Employee Benefits Security Administration announced it will prioritize Long COVID diagnostic denials in its 2026-2027 enforcement work plan and will conduct targeted audits of the ten largest national insurers.

The guidance also requires plans to maintain in-network access to qualified Long COVID specialty clinics within 60 minutes' travel time in urban areas and 90 minutes in rural areas, or to cover out-of-network care at in-network cost-sharing levels. This network adequacy provision addresses persistent complaints that even patients with strong coverage cannot find providers willing to order or interpret advanced testing.

Insurance industry groups, including AHIP and the Blue Cross Blue Shield Association, expressed concerns about cost implications but stopped short of threatening legal challenge. Patient advocacy organizations including Long COVID Alliance and Body Politic praised the guidance as the most significant federal access expansion to date.

Why this matters: Removing prior authorization barriers to validated Long COVID diagnostics could finally enable timely diagnosis and treatment for millions of patients whose conditions have been dismissed or delayed by insurance gatekeeping.

title: Phase 3 RESTORE-MITO Trial Shows Urolithin A Improves Mitophagy and Reduces Post-Exertional Malaise in Long COVID by 35%
excerpt: The Phase 3 RESTORE-MITO trial demonstrated that 12 weeks of oral urolithin A supplementation reduced post-exertional malaise severity by 35% in Long COVID patients with documented mitochondrial dysfunction. The compound, which enhances mitophagy and clears damaged mitochondria, also improved six-minute walk distance and skeletal muscle ATP production on phosphorus MR spectroscopy.
body:
Results from the Phase 3 RESTORE-MITO trial, published in The Lancet last week, show that daily oral urolithin A reduced post-exertional malaise severity by 35% in Long COVID patients over 12 weeks compared with placebo. The multicenter trial enrolled 612 adults with Long COVID and documented mitochondrial dysfunction confirmed by elevated lactate-to-pyruvate ratios and abnormal phosphorus magnetic resonance spectroscopy of skeletal muscle.

Urolithin A is a gut microbial metabolite of ellagitannins, compounds found in pomegranates and walnuts, that selectively induces mitophagy — the cellular process by which damaged mitochondria are recycled. Preclinical work over the past decade has established urolithin A as a mitochondrial quality control agent, and smaller trials in age-related muscle decline have shown modest benefits. The RESTORE-MITO trial is the first large-scale demonstration of efficacy in a post-viral syndrome.

Trial Design and Results

Participants received either 1,000 mg of urolithin A daily or matched placebo for 12 weeks. The primary endpoint — change in the DePaul Symptom Questionnaire post-exertional malaise subscale — improved by a mean of 35.2% in the treatment arm versus 9.4% in placebo. Secondary endpoints showed a 22% increase in six-minute walk distance, a 41% improvement in skeletal muscle ATP synthesis rates measured by phosphorus MRS, and reduced circulating GDF-15, a marker of mitochondrial stress.

Notably, the trial used a two-day cardiopulmonary exercise testing protocol to objectively document post-exertional malaise at baseline and week 12. Patients receiving urolithin A showed preserved VO2 at the ventilatory threshold on day two of testing, while placebo recipients continued to demonstrate the characteristic day-two decline seen in myalgic encephalomyelitis populations.

Safety and Regulatory Path

Adverse events were mild and comparable between arms, with gastrointestinal discomfort reported in 8% of urolithin A recipients versus 6% in placebo. No serious treatment-related events occurred. Because urolithin A is currently marketed as a dietary supplement in the United States, the sponsor, Amazentis, has filed for FDA new drug application review to convert the compound to a prescription therapeutic indicated for post-viral mitochondrial dysfunction. An advisory committee meeting is anticipated in the fourth quarter of 2026.

Investigators cautioned that supplement-grade urolithin A products vary substantially in purity and bioavailability, and that off-label use of commercial preparations is unlikely to reproduce trial results. The pharmaceutical-grade formulation used in RESTORE-MITO achieved plasma concentrations roughly four times higher than typical supplements at equivalent doses.

Why this matters: RESTORE-MITO provides the strongest evidence yet that targeting mitochondrial quality control can measurably reduce the cardinal disabling symptom of Long COVID, offering a mechanism-based therapy for patients whose lives are constrained by post-exertional crashes.

title: Supreme Court Holds in Henderson v. Acme Logistics That Long COVID Brain Fog Qualifies as a Cognitive Disability Under the ADA Without Requiring Neuropsychological Testing
excerpt: In a 7-2 decision issued this week, the Supreme Court ruled that Long COVID-associated cognitive dysfunction constitutes a disability under the ADA based on credible self-report and clinical documentation, without requiring formal neuropsychological testing. The ruling resolves a circuit split and removes a major barrier to workplace accommodation claims.
body:
In Henderson v. Acme Logistics, Inc., decided 7-2 on May 28, the Supreme Court held that Long COVID-associated cognitive dysfunction — commonly described as brain fog — qualifies as a substantially limiting mental impairment under the Americans with Disabilities Act based on treating physician documentation and credible patient self-report, without requiring formal neuropsychological testing. The decision reverses the Eighth Circuit and resolves a longstanding split among the federal appellate courts.

The case arose when Melissa Henderson, a logistics coordinator, was terminated after requesting reduced screen time and extended deadlines following her Long COVID diagnosis. Her employer argued that absent quantitative neuropsychological evidence of impairment, her self-reported cognitive symptoms were insufficient to establish a disability. Lower courts agreed, dismissing her ADA claim at summary judgment.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for the majority, Justice Kagan emphasized that the 2008 ADA Amendments Act expressly broadened the definition of disability and rejected demanding evidentiary thresholds. The opinion noted that neuropsychological testing is expensive, often not covered by insurance, and frequently fails to detect the fluctuating, fatigue-dependent cognitive impairments characteristic of Long COVID. Requiring such testing, the Court held, would effectively read post-viral cognitive disorders out of the statute.

The majority cited the JAMA Neurology choroid plexus inflammation study and the NIH RECOVER-NEURO trial as evidence that Long COVID cognitive dysfunction has identifiable biological substrates even when standardized testing appears normal. The Court adopted a totality-of-the-circumstances framework requiring courts to consider treating physician opinions, validated symptom questionnaires, functional impact evidence, and patient testimony together.

Justice Alito, joined by Justice Thomas, dissented, arguing that the ruling invites subjective and unverifiable disability claims.

Practical Implications

Employment lawyers expect the decision to dramatically reshape accommodation litigation. The EEOC, which filed an amicus brief supporting Henderson, issued guidance within hours of the decision instructing employers that requests for accommodation based on Long COVID cognitive symptoms must be evaluated through the interactive process without demanding neuropsychological evaluation as a prerequisite. Disability insurers and ERISA plan administrators will also face heightened scrutiny when denying claims for lack of formal cognitive testing.

Disability rights advocates noted that the decision aligns ADA jurisprudence with how clinicians actually diagnose Long COVID cognitive dysfunction, which relies primarily on history, symptom inventories such as the MoCA when relevant, and functional assessment rather than expensive multi-hour neuropsychological batteries.

The case now returns to the district court for further proceedings on Henderson's accommodation and wrongful termination claims.

Why this matters: Millions of Long COVID patients whose cognitive symptoms have been dismissed for lack of objective testing now have Supreme Court backing to secure workplace accommodations and disability benefits based on the clinical realities of their condition.

title: VA Issues Final Rule Establishing Presumptive Service Connection for Long COVID in All Veterans Who Served During the Declared Public Health Emergency
excerpt: The Department of Veterans Affairs published a final rule this week establishing presumptive service connection for Long COVID in veterans who served on active duty between January 2020 and May 2023, eliminating the requirement that veterans prove their condition resulted from military exposure. The rule is expected to streamline claims for an estimated 280,000 veterans.
body:
The Department of Veterans Affairs published a final rule on May 30 establishing presumptive service connection for Long COVID in veterans who served on active duty between January 27, 2020, and May 11, 2023 — the dates of the federal COVID-19 public health emergency. Under the rule, affected veterans no longer need to prove that their Long COVID resulted from military service; the connection is presumed as a matter of law.

The rulemaking follows two years of pressure from veterans service organizations and a series of adverse federal court decisions, including the D.C. Circuit's ruling striking down the VA's prior policy that limited service connection to veterans hospitalized for acute COVID-19. The new rule extends eligibility to any veteran who served during the covered period and now has a current Long COVID diagnosis meeting WHO clinical criteria.

Scope and Eligibility

The VA estimates approximately 280,000 veterans will qualify under the rule, with an additional 40,000 to 60,000 cases expected to be identified through outreach over the next three years. Eligible diagnoses include post-COVID dysautonomia, post-COVID cognitive disorder, post-COVID chronic fatigue, post-acute COVID cardiopulmonary syndrome, and small fiber neuropathy attributable to SARS-CoV-2 infection.

Veterans must establish a current diagnosis from a VA or VA-authorized clinician, but documentation of an acute infection during service is not required — recognizing that asymptomatic and undocumented infections were widespread, particularly during early-pandemic deployments and shipboard outbreaks. Veterans previously denied service connection under the old rule may file supplemental claims and receive retroactive benefits to the date of their original application.

Disability Ratings and Companion Benefits

The rule adopts a new diagnostic code, 6354, with disability ratings ranging from 10% to 100% based on functional impairment, frequency of symptom exacerbations, and ability to maintain gainful employment. Ratings of 60% or higher carry eligibility for individual unemployability benefits, and veterans rated at 100% gain access to comprehensive caregiver support, dependent education benefits, and CHAMPVA coverage for family members.

The VA simultaneously announced expansion of its Long COVID specialty clinics to 47 medical centers, up from 18, and committed to hiring 1,200 additional clinicians with Long COVID expertise. A new Long COVID Center of Excellence will be established at the Washington DC VA Medical Center to coordinate research and clinical training across the system.

Veterans service organizations praised the rule but noted that implementation will require sustained oversight to ensure claims are adjudicated promptly and that ratings reflect the often invisible nature of Long COVID disability.

Why this matters: Hundreds of thousands of veterans who served during the pandemic and are now disabled by Long COVID will finally have a clear path to the benefits and care they earned, without years of evidentiary battles over a connection that was always obvious.

title: Phase 3 ATLAS-LC Trial Shows BC 007 Aptamer Neutralizes GPCR Autoantibodies and Improves Microvascular Function in Long COVID by 48%
excerpt: The international Phase 3 ATLAS-LC trial reported that the DNA aptamer BC 007, which binds and neutralizes G-protein-coupled receptor autoantibodies, produced a 48% improvement in retinal microvascular perfusion and significant symptom relief in long COVID patients.
body:
Berlin Cures and the Charité University Hospital announced final results from the Phase 3 ATLAS-LC trial on May 28, 2026, demonstrating that the DNA aptamer BC 007 produced a 48% improvement in retinal capillary perfusion and meaningful clinical benefit in long COVID patients carrying functional G-protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) autoantibodies. The randomized, placebo-controlled trial enrolled 612 patients across 34 sites in Germany, the Netherlands, Austria, and the United States.

BC 007 is a 15-nucleotide aptamer that binds and neutralizes circulating autoantibodies against beta-adrenergic and muscarinic receptors, a mechanism implicated in dysautonomia, microvascular dysfunction, and post-exertional malaise. Participants received a single intravenous infusion followed by a second dose at week 12, with primary endpoints assessed at week 24.

Key Findings

Patients receiving BC 007 showed a 48% improvement in retinal vessel density measured by optical coherence tomography angiography, compared with 6% in the placebo arm. Fatigue scores on the Chalder scale fell by an average of 11.4 points versus 3.1 points for placebo, and 41% of treated patients achieved the prespecified threshold for clinically meaningful recovery on the Post-COVID Functional Status scale. Cognitive performance, measured by the Cogstate battery, improved by 22% in treated patients.

Notably, the response was strongly correlated with baseline autoantibody titer. Patients in the top quartile of beta-1 adrenergic receptor autoantibody levels showed a 63% symptom reduction, suggesting that pre-treatment serology could identify likely responders. Adverse events were largely mild, with transient hypotension during infusion reported in 9% of patients.

Regulatory Path

Berlin Cures confirmed that it will submit a Biologics License Application to the FDA in the third quarter of 2026, with parallel filings to the European Medicines Agency. The FDA had previously granted BC 007 Fast Track designation for long COVID in 2024. If approved, BC 007 would become the first targeted therapy designed specifically to neutralize the autoantibody mechanism increasingly viewed as central to a major subset of long COVID cases.

Investigators emphasized that ATLAS-LC builds on smaller proof-of-concept studies from Erlangen and Charité Berlin, which had shown sustained capillary reperfusion lasting months after a single dose. The Phase 3 data confirm durability through week 24 and demonstrate scalable manufacturing of clinical-grade aptamer.

Independent commentators noted that BC 007 joins a growing armamentarium of mechanism-specific long COVID therapies, including efgartigimod, immunoadsorption, and daratumumab, that target distinct immunologic drivers of the syndrome.

Why this matters: ATLAS-LC provides the strongest evidence yet that neutralizing functional autoantibodies can reverse microvascular and autonomic dysfunction in long COVID, positioning BC 007 as a potential first-in-class disease-modifying therapy.

title: Second Circuit Rules Long COVID Qualifies as a Serious Health Condition Triggering COBRA Extension Rights for Workers Terminated During Disability Leave
excerpt: The Second Circuit held that long COVID constitutes a qualifying disability under COBRA's extended coverage provisions, entitling terminated employees to up to 29 months of continued health insurance rather than the standard 18.
body:
In a unanimous decision issued May 21, 2026, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit ruled in Mercer v. Sterling Financial Group that long COVID qualifies as a disability under the Social Security Administration's standards for purposes of COBRA's disability extension, entitling terminated workers to 29 months of continuation coverage rather than the standard 18 months. The decision is the first federal appellate ruling to address how COBRA's disability extension applies to long COVID claimants.

Background

The case was brought by Diane Mercer, a former compliance officer at Sterling Financial Group who was placed on disability leave in 2023 after developing severe post-exertional malaise, cognitive dysfunction, and POTS following a SARS-CoV-2 infection. After Sterling terminated her employment in early 2024, she elected COBRA continuation coverage and applied for the 11-month disability extension based on a Social Security determination that she became disabled within the first 60 days of her COBRA period.

Sterling's third-party administrator denied the extension, arguing that long COVID was not a recognized impairment under Title II of the Social Security Act at the relevant time and that Mercer's symptoms were too subjective to constitute a qualifying disability. The district court agreed and dismissed the case.

The Second Circuit's Reasoning

Writing for the panel, Judge Beth Robinson rejected Sterling's interpretation, holding that COBRA's disability extension provision incorporates SSA's substantive disability standards as they exist at the time of the determination, not at the time of the qualifying event. Because SSA added long COVID to its Compassionate Allowances program in 2025 and had long recognized post-viral fatigue syndromes as medically determinable impairments, Mercer's SSA award was dispositive.

The court further held that COBRA administrators may not look behind a valid SSA disability determination to challenge the medical basis for the underlying impairment, citing the statute's plain text and the Department of Labor's longstanding interpretive guidance.

The panel also addressed premium calculations, holding that the 150% premium cap applied to the entire 11-month extension period and that employers may not impose separate administrative surcharges for long COVID claimants.

Practical Impact

The ruling affects an estimated 380,000 long COVID workers nationwide who lose employer coverage each year while waiting for SSDI determinations. The decision creates a circuit-applicable precedent in New York, Connecticut, and Vermont, and aligns with informal guidance issued by the Department of Labor's Employee Benefits Security Administration in February 2026.

Why this matters: The ruling bridges a critical insurance gap for long COVID workers awaiting Medicare eligibility, ensuring they retain access to specialty care during the prolonged period between job loss and federal disability coverage.

title: CMS Establishes National Long COVID Quality Measure Set Tying Hospital Reimbursement to Diagnosis, Referral, and Functional Outcome Reporting
excerpt: The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services finalized a rule creating the first National Long COVID Quality Measure Set, linking a portion of hospital reimbursement to documentation of diagnosis, appropriate specialty referral, and functional outcome reporting.
body:
The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) issued a final rule on May 18, 2026, establishing the National Long COVID Quality Measure Set under the Hospital Inpatient Quality Reporting Program and the Merit-based Incentive Payment System. Effective January 1, 2027, hospitals and large physician groups will be required to report on six quality measures specific to long COVID care, with up to 2% of Medicare reimbursement tied to performance beginning in fiscal year 2028.

The Six Measures

The new measure set includes: (1) documented screening for post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 in patients with a prior COVID-19 diagnosis presenting with fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, or dyspnea; (2) timely referral to a multidisciplinary long COVID clinic or qualified specialist within 60 days of diagnosis; (3) baseline and follow-up functional assessment using the Post-COVID Functional Status scale or equivalent; (4) screening for orthostatic intolerance and post-exertional malaise; (5) avoidance of contraindicated graded exercise therapy in patients meeting criteria for ME/CFS-type presentation; and (6) patient-reported outcome measurement at six and twelve months.

The rule incorporates the WHO's 2026 clinical practice guideline, which recommended against graded exercise therapy and endorsed pacing-based rehabilitation. Hospitals that prescribe or refer patients to graded exercise programs for post-exertional malaise will receive negative scoring on Measure 5.

Equity Adjustments

CMS included risk adjustments to address disparities in long COVID care, with bonus points available for hospitals demonstrating equitable diagnosis rates across racial, ethnic, and language groups. The rule references CDC findings that Latino and Black patients are diagnosed with long COVID at significantly lower rates than white patients despite comparable symptom prevalence.

Critical access hospitals and rural facilities will receive a two-year reporting delay and access to a $40 million technical assistance fund to develop reporting infrastructure.

Industry Response

The American Hospital Association expressed support for standardized measurement but raised concerns about reporting burden, particularly the requirement for twelve-month follow-up outcomes. The Long COVID Alliance and Patient-Led Research Collaborative praised the rule, noting that it operationalizes years of patient advocacy by tying financial accountability to recognition and appropriate referral.

Several specialty societies, including the American Academy of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation and the American Autonomic Society, endorsed the measure set. The American College of Physicians issued conditional support while requesting clarification on the screening measure's denominator population.

CMS estimates that approximately 3,200 hospitals and 280,000 clinicians will be subject to the new measures, affecting care for an estimated 4.1 million Medicare beneficiaries with documented or suspected long COVID.

Why this matters: By tying reimbursement to recognition and appropriate care pathways, CMS is using its financial leverage to address persistent underdiagnosis and substandard treatment that have characterized long COVID care since the pandemic's earliest years.

title: Phase 3 RECOVER-RTX Trial Shows Low-Dose Rituximab Reduces Symptoms by 43% in Long COVID Patients With B-Cell Hyperactivation
excerpt: A 312-patient Phase 3 trial sponsored by NIH RECOVER found that low-dose rituximab produced a 43% reduction in symptom burden among Long COVID patients with documented B-cell hyperactivation and elevated autoantibody titers.
body:
A 312-patient Phase 3 trial sponsored by NIH RECOVER found that low-dose rituximab produced a 43% reduction in composite symptom burden among Long COVID patients with documented B-cell hyperactivation and elevated autoantibody titers over 24 weeks. Results were published this week in the New England Journal of Medicine.

The RECOVER-RTX trial enrolled adults with Long COVID lasting at least 12 months who showed evidence of B-cell dysregulation on flow cytometry, including expanded CD19+CD27- naive B-cell populations and detectable G-protein-coupled receptor autoantibodies. Participants received two infusions of rituximab at 500 mg dosing — substantially lower than the dose used in rheumatoid arthritis — separated by two weeks, with follow-up extending to 48 weeks.

Trial Design and Endpoints

The primary endpoint, a composite score derived from the PROMIS-29 and DePaul Symptom Questionnaire, improved by 43% in the treatment arm compared with 11% in the placebo arm. Secondary endpoints showed a 37% reduction in post-exertional malaise frequency, a 29% improvement in cognitive performance on the NIH Toolbox, and significant declines in circulating autoantibodies against beta-adrenergic and muscarinic receptors. Investigators reported that approximately 58% of treated participants met criteria for clinically meaningful response at week 24, compared with 19% on placebo.

Adverse events were consistent with rituximab's known profile, including transient infusion reactions and modest hypogammaglobulinemia, though no serious infections were reported during the follow-up window. Investigators noted that the low-dose regimen appeared to preserve more residual B-cell function than oncology-dose protocols, potentially reducing infection risk.

Implications for Treatment Stratification

Lead investigator Dr. Akiko Iwasaki of Yale, presenting at the International Long COVID Research Conference, emphasized that the results reinforce a precision medicine approach: rituximab worked specifically in the biomarker-defined subgroup and showed no benefit in an exploratory cohort lacking autoantibody signatures. The trial used a centralized autoantibody panel developed in collaboration with Charité Berlin, which investigators recommended as a prerequisite for treatment selection.

The findings echo earlier signals from open-label rituximab series in Norway and Germany, but the RECOVER-RTX trial provides the first placebo-controlled Phase 3 evidence of efficacy in a defined Long COVID subpopulation. FDA officials indicated that the sponsor plans to submit a supplemental Biologics License Application for an autoimmune Long COVID indication later this year, supported by the breakthrough designation granted in late 2025.

Clinicians caution that rituximab access remains limited by cost and the absence of validated commercial autoantibody assays in most U.S. labs, though several reference laboratories have announced plans to roll out the necessary panels by early 2027.

Why this matters: The RECOVER-RTX results provide the strongest randomized evidence yet that a subset of Long COVID is driven by autoreactive B-cell biology amenable to targeted immunotherapy, opening a viable treatment path for patients who have exhausted symptomatic options.

title: Ninth Circuit Rules State Long-Term Disability Programs Must Apply Same Standards to Long COVID as to Other Chronic Illnesses Under Equal Protection
excerpt: The Ninth Circuit ruled that state-administered long-term disability programs violate the Equal Protection Clause when they impose heightened evidentiary burdens on Long COVID claimants compared with applicants alleging other chronic conditions.
body:
The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled Monday that state-administered long-term disability programs violate the Equal Protection Clause when they impose heightened evidentiary burdens on Long COVID claimants compared with applicants alleging other chronic illnesses such as multiple sclerosis or lupus. The 2-1 decision in Hernandez v. California State Disability Insurance Fund revives a class action by more than 4,000 California claimants whose benefits were denied or terminated under disease-specific review protocols.

Background of the Dispute

The case arose after California's State Disability Insurance program adopted internal guidelines in 2024 requiring Long COVID applicants to submit objective laboratory or imaging evidence of organ dysfunction in addition to functional capacity assessments — a standard not applied to claimants with comparable contested conditions like fibromyalgia or chronic fatigue syndrome. Plaintiffs argued the policy resulted in denial rates of 71% for Long COVID applicants compared with 34% for applicants citing other multisystem chronic illnesses.

The district court initially dismissed the case, holding that disease-specific evidentiary standards survived rational-basis review because the state had a legitimate interest in preventing fraudulent claims. The Ninth Circuit reversed, finding that the trial record showed no evidence of disproportionate fraud risk in Long COVID applications and that the policy was driven by skepticism about the condition's legitimacy rather than any actuarial concern.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for the majority, Judge Lucy Koh held that even under deferential rational-basis scrutiny, the state must justify disparate treatment with evidence rather than presumption. The opinion cited the WHO's 2025 clinical practice guideline and the FDA's approval of a validated multi-analyte diagnostic blood test as undermining any rational basis for treating Long COVID as uniquely suspect. The court emphasized that requiring objective biomarkers from a condition for which validated biomarkers were only recently available created a circular and discriminatory burden.

The dissent argued that states retain broad discretion to manage disability fund solvency and that the majority improperly substituted its medical judgment for that of program administrators.

Reach Beyond California

The ruling directly binds state disability programs in California, Hawaii, and Washington, all of which administer their own short- and long-term disability insurance programs separate from SSDI. Disability rights attorneys said the decision is likely to influence litigation in New York, New Jersey, and Rhode Island, which operate similar state programs and have faced parallel complaints from Long COVID claimants.

The case will return to the district court, where plaintiffs are expected to seek classwide injunctive relief and retroactive benefits. California's Employment Development Department said it is reviewing the decision and has temporarily suspended Long COVID-specific evidentiary requirements pending further guidance.

Why this matters: The ruling establishes that singling out Long COVID for heightened scrutiny in state disability adjudications can violate constitutional equal protection, providing a powerful new legal tool for claimants in states with their own disability insurance systems.

title: FDA Approves Wearable Continuous Autonomic Monitor for Long COVID Diagnosis and Treatment Response Tracking
excerpt: The FDA granted de novo clearance to the CardioNomic Patch, the first wearable device authorized specifically for continuous autonomic function monitoring in suspected Long COVID, enabling at-home assessment of heart rate variability, orthostatic response, and baroreflex sensitivity.
body:
The FDA granted de novo clearance Tuesday to the CardioNomic Patch, the first wearable device authorized specifically for continuous autonomic function monitoring in suspected Long COVID. The decision enables at-home, multi-day assessment of heart rate variability, orthostatic blood pressure response, and baroreflex sensitivity — measurements that have historically required tilt-table testing in specialized cardiology labs.

How the Device Works

The patch, developed by NeuroCardia Diagnostics and refined through a five-year RECOVER-supported development program, is a single-use adhesive sensor worn on the chest for up to 14 days. It integrates a single-lead ECG, a photoplethysmography array, an accelerometer for posture detection, and a microfluidic cuffless blood pressure sensor validated against ambulatory cuff measurements. Data streams continuously to a cloud platform that generates standardized autonomic indices, including a Long COVID-specific composite score derived from the device's pivotal trial.

The pivotal trial, conducted at 14 U.S. sites and published in JAMA Cardiology, enrolled 1,847 patients with suspected Long COVID dysautonomia and 612 controls. The device demonstrated 84% sensitivity and 89% specificity for detecting POTS, inappropriate sinus tachycardia, and orthostatic hypotension when compared against confirmatory tilt-table testing. Critically, the multi-day monitoring window captured intermittent dysautonomic episodes that single-session tilt testing missed in approximately 31% of patients ultimately diagnosed with autonomic dysfunction.

Clinical and Access Implications

Cardiologists involved in the trial said the device addresses a major bottleneck in Long COVID care: nationwide capacity for tilt-table testing remains limited to roughly 600 centers, with wait times often exceeding six months. The wearable approach allows community physicians to initiate diagnostic workups and track responses to interventions like ivabradine, midodrine, or pyridostigmine without referral to a tertiary autonomic center.

The FDA clearance specifies that the device is indicated for adults with persistent symptoms following SARS-CoV-2 infection and is not approved as a standalone diagnostic tool — confirmatory clinical evaluation remains required. The agency also imposed post-market surveillance requirements, including a registry of 5,000 patients to assess real-world performance and any racial or sex-based performance variation, after pilot data suggested slightly lower sensitivity in patients with darker skin tones due to photoplethysmography limitations.

CMS issued a preliminary coverage statement signaling that Medicare will cover the device under the existing autonomic function studies determination finalized last year, with a national pricing decision expected within 90 days. Several large commercial insurers, including Aetna and UnitedHealthcare, indicated they will follow Medicare's lead. The device manufacturer set a list price of $895 per monitoring episode, comparable to ambulatory cardiac monitoring devices already in widespread use.

Patient advocacy groups praised the approval but cautioned that access will depend on whether community clinicians are trained to interpret the device's output and integrate findings into treatment plans.

Why this matters: The clearance dramatically expands diagnostic access for the millions of Long COVID patients with suspected dysautonomia who have been unable to obtain timely autonomic testing, potentially shortening the path from symptoms to evidence-based treatment by months.

title: Stanford Trial Finds Low-Dose Naltrexone Combined With Acetyl-L-Carnitine Reduces Long COVID Fatigue and Pain by 38% Over 16 Weeks
excerpt: A randomized Phase 2 trial from Stanford University demonstrates that combining low-dose naltrexone with acetyl-L-carnitine significantly reduces fatigue, widespread pain, and post-exertional symptoms in Long COVID patients compared to either agent alone.
body:
A Phase 2 randomized controlled trial conducted at Stanford University has found that combining low-dose naltrexone (LDN) with acetyl-L-carnitine produced a 38% reduction in fatigue and pain scores in Long COVID patients over 16 weeks, substantially outperforming either monotherapy. The findings, published this month in The Lancet Regional Health – Americas, provide the most rigorous evidence to date for a repurposed combination regimen targeting both neuroinflammation and mitochondrial dysfunction.

The trial enrolled 312 adults with Long COVID lasting more than 12 months and persistent moderate-to-severe fatigue. Participants were randomized to four arms: LDN 4.5 mg daily plus acetyl-L-carnitine 1,500 mg twice daily; LDN alone; acetyl-L-carnitine alone; or matched placebo. The combination arm showed a mean 38% reduction on the Chalder Fatigue Scale and a 34% reduction in Brief Pain Inventory scores, compared to 19% and 14% reductions respectively in the LDN-only arm, and 16% and 11% in the carnitine-only arm. Placebo response was 9% for fatigue.

Mechanistic Findings

Investigators reported significant reductions in serum interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor alpha, and glial fibrillary acidic protein in the combination arm, alongside improvements in muscle phosphocreatine recovery measured by magnetic resonance spectroscopy in a 48-patient substudy. Lead investigator Dr. Hector Bonilla noted that LDN appears to dampen microglial activation while acetyl-L-carnitine supports fatty acid transport into mitochondria, addressing two distinct but interacting pathways implicated in post-exertional malaise.

Importantly, the trial included a structured 48-hour symptom diary following a standardized cognitive and physical challenge at week 16. Patients in the combination arm reported 41% less severe post-exertional symptom flare compared to placebo, the first time a pharmacologic intervention has shown a measurable PEM-attenuation signal in a controlled trial of this size.

Safety and Accessibility

Adverse events were mild and comparable across arms, with vivid dreams (14%) and transient gastrointestinal upset (11%) most common in LDN recipients. Both agents are inexpensive, available through compounding pharmacies, and have decades of safety data in other indications, which investigators argue should accelerate clinical adoption while larger Phase 3 confirmation is pursued.

The Long COVID Research Consortium has announced it will incorporate the regimen into a confirmatory 1,200-patient Phase 3 trial beginning in September, with sites across the HHS Long COVID Clinical Care Network. Several insurance plans, including Kaiser Permanente and Geisinger, have indicated they will add LDN-carnitine combination protocols to their Long COVID treatment pathways based on the Stanford data.

Why this matters: With no FDA-approved therapy yet on the market and millions of patients waiting, a low-cost, well-tolerated combination of repurposed agents producing measurable reductions in both fatigue and post-exertional malaise could transform near-term clinical care.

title: Fourth Circuit Rules Health Insurers Cannot Deny Coverage for Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy in Long COVID Patients With Documented Cognitive Dysfunction
excerpt: The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit has ruled that health insurers cannot categorically deny coverage for hyperbaric oxygen therapy in Long COVID patients with documented cognitive impairment, finding such blanket denials violate ERISA's full and fair review requirements.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit ruled this week that health insurers governed by ERISA cannot categorically deny coverage for hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT) as 'experimental' in Long COVID patients with objectively documented cognitive dysfunction, holding that such blanket denials violate the statute's full and fair review requirements when peer-reviewed evidence supports clinical efficacy. The decision in Whitaker v. Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield marks the first federal appellate ruling on insurance coverage for HBOT in the post-COVID context.

The plaintiff, a 44-year-old former emergency room nurse from North Carolina, was prescribed a 40-session HBOT course after neuropsychological testing documented severe deficits in processing speed and working memory, and SPECT imaging showed reduced frontal cortical perfusion. Anthem denied coverage on grounds that HBOT for Long COVID was 'investigational,' relying on a 2022 internal medical policy that had not been updated despite the publication of multiple randomized trials, including the Tel Aviv University study showing significant cognitive and neuroimaging improvements.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for a unanimous three-judge panel, Judge Toby Heytens held that ERISA's reasoned decision-making requirement obligates insurers to engage substantively with the current state of peer-reviewed evidence rather than rely on stale categorical exclusions. The court found Anthem's medical policy 'frozen in time,' noting that it failed to address at least seven controlled trials published between 2022 and 2025. The panel also held that conflating 'lack of FDA-specific indication' with 'experimental' status is arbitrary when the therapy is FDA-cleared for general use and supported by published efficacy data in the relevant population.

The court remanded the case for benefit determination and signaled that similar categorical denials by other ERISA plans would likely fail review. Importantly, the opinion emphasized that objective biomarkers — neuropsychological testing, perfusion imaging, and the recently approved multi-analyte Long COVID blood test — provide the kind of documented impairment that distinguishes covered medically necessary care from elective wellness use.

Broader Implications

Patient advocacy organizations called the decision a significant breakthrough, as HBOT typically costs $15,000–$30,000 for a full course and has been largely inaccessible to patients without out-of-pocket means. The American Medical Association's Long COVID Practice Committee said the ruling should prompt rapid revision of commercial and Medicare Advantage medical policies. Anthem indicated it is reviewing the decision; however, Cigna and UnitedHealthcare both announced this week that they are convening medical policy reviews of HBOT for Long COVID cognitive indications.

Several state insurance commissioners, including in Maryland and Virginia, have signaled they may issue parallel guidance applicable to fully insured plans outside ERISA's reach.

Why this matters: The ruling could expand access to a costly but increasingly evidence-supported therapy for one of Long COVID's most disabling symptom clusters, while establishing a broader precedent that insurers must keep coverage policies current with evolving science.

title: CMS Finalizes Rule Requiring Medicare Advantage Plans to Cover Home-Based Cardiopulmonary Monitoring for Long COVID Patients With Dysautonomia
excerpt: The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services has issued a final rule requiring Medicare Advantage plans to cover continuous home-based cardiopulmonary and autonomic monitoring for Long COVID patients with documented dysautonomia, closing a major gap in chronic disease management.
body:
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services issued a final rule this week requiring all Medicare Advantage plans to cover continuous home-based cardiopulmonary and autonomic monitoring for Long COVID patients with documented dysautonomia, including POTS and orthostatic hypotension. The rule, effective January 1, 2027, closes a longstanding gap that had left most beneficiaries unable to access wearable cardiac rhythm monitors, ambulatory blood pressure devices, and continuous pulse oximetry outside of brief diagnostic windows.

The final rule follows an 18-month rulemaking process that drew over 14,000 public comments, the majority from Long COVID patients, clinicians, and disability advocates. Under the rule, plans must cover up to 90 days of continuous monitoring per year for patients meeting documented criteria, with extensions available for those with unstable symptoms or medication titration needs. Covered devices include FDA-cleared patch-based ECG monitors, wearable blood pressure cuffs validated for ambulatory use, and continuous pulse oximeters with cloud data transmission to treating clinicians.

Clinical Rationale

CMS cited evidence that Long COVID dysautonomia frequently produces episodic symptoms that escape detection during single office visits or tilt table tests. The agency referenced data from the NIH RECOVER-AUTONOMIC program showing that 58% of patients with confirmed dysautonomia had clinically significant findings — including paroxysmal tachycardia, nocturnal hypoxemia, and asymptomatic orthostatic drops — only identifiable through multi-day ambulatory monitoring. CMS also cited cost-effectiveness modeling showing that home monitoring reduced emergency department visits by 31% and hospital admissions by 24% in pilot programs at the Mayo Clinic and Vanderbilt.

The rule also establishes reimbursement codes for clinician interpretation of continuous monitoring data and for nurse-led remote management programs, addressing prior barriers in which devices were technically covered but the professional services to act on the data were not.

Equity and Access Provisions

In a notable provision, the rule requires plans to provide cellular-enabled devices at no additional cost to beneficiaries who lack reliable home internet, addressing a digital divide that had effectively excluded rural and low-income enrollees from earlier pilot programs. Plans must also offer Spanish-language device support and translated patient education materials. CMS Administrator Chiquita Brooks-LaSure called the rule 'a recognition that chronic, episodic conditions like Long COVID dysautonomia require continuous data, not snapshot diagnostics.'

The rule applies only to Medicare Advantage but is expected to drive parallel coverage changes in traditional Medicare through a separate National Coverage Determination process already underway. Several major commercial insurers, including Aetna and Humana's commercial lines, indicated they will align their policies with the new standard.

Why this matters: Continuous home monitoring could finally give clinicians the longitudinal data needed to tailor treatment for the millions of Long COVID patients whose disabling autonomic symptoms have long been dismissed when they failed to appear during brief in-office testing.

title: Phase 3 ENERGIZE-LC Trial Shows Mestinon Plus Pyridoxine Combination Improves Exercise Tolerance by 41% in Long COVID Patients With Neuromuscular Transmission Deficits
excerpt: A multicenter Phase 3 trial published in The Lancet found that combining pyridostigmine with pyridoxine produced significantly greater improvements in exercise tolerance than pyridostigmine alone in Long COVID patients with documented neuromuscular junction dysfunction.
body:
A multicenter Phase 3 trial published this week in The Lancet found that combining pyridostigmine (Mestinon) with high-dose pyridoxine produced a 41% improvement in exercise tolerance over 20 weeks in Long COVID patients with documented neuromuscular transmission deficits, substantially outperforming pyridostigmine monotherapy. The ENERGIZE-LC trial enrolled 612 patients across 28 sites in North America and Europe and represents the first large-scale confirmation that adjunctive vitamin B6 therapy potentiates cholinergic treatment in post-viral neuromuscular dysfunction.

Participants were required to have abnormal single-fiber electromyography findings and a baseline six-minute walk distance below 400 meters. They were randomized to pyridostigmine 60 mg three times daily plus pyridoxine 100 mg daily, pyridostigmine alone, or placebo. At week 20, the combination arm achieved a mean increase of 87 meters on the six-minute walk test, compared with 41 meters in the monotherapy arm and 12 meters with placebo. Secondary endpoints including grip strength, Chalder Fatigue Scale scores, and post-exertional malaise frequency all favored the combination.

Mechanistic Findings

Investigators reported that combination therapy was associated with reduced jitter on repeat single-fiber EMG and normalization of acetylcholine receptor antibody titers in a subset of participants. The team hypothesizes that pyridoxine supports neurotransmitter synthesis and may mitigate the homocysteine elevations associated with chronic cholinesterase inhibition. Adverse events were largely gastrointestinal and comparable across active arms, with no serious cardiac events reported.

Notably, the trial included a prespecified subgroup analysis of patients with concurrent small fiber neuropathy on skin biopsy. This subgroup, comprising 38% of enrolled patients, showed even larger treatment effects, with a 54% improvement in exercise tolerance. The authors caution that the combination should not be confused with treatment for myasthenia gravis, despite shared pharmacology, and emphasize that screening for neuromuscular involvement using single-fiber EMG remains essential to identifying likely responders.

Clinical Implementation

The principal investigator, based at the University of Toronto, stated that the trial provides the clearest evidence to date that a subset of Long COVID patients have measurable presynaptic and postsynaptic neuromuscular defects amenable to targeted pharmacotherapy. Several Long COVID specialty centers have already announced plans to integrate single-fiber EMG into diagnostic workups, though access to the test remains limited outside academic medical centers. The manufacturer has indicated it will pursue a supplemental new drug application later this year to add Long COVID-associated neuromuscular dysfunction as an approved indication.

Why this matters: The ENERGIZE-LC results validate neuromuscular transmission failure as a treatable Long COVID phenotype and offer a low-cost, widely available combination therapy that could meaningfully restore physical function in patients whose disability has been dismissed as deconditioning.

title: Fourth Circuit Rules Federal Employees With Long COVID Entitled to Telework as Default Accommodation Under Rehabilitation Act
excerpt: The Fourth Circuit Court of Appeals held that federal agencies must offer full-time telework as a presumptive reasonable accommodation for employees with documented Long COVID before requiring them to demonstrate why in-person work is impossible.
body:
The Fourth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled this week in Henderson v. Department of Commerce that federal agencies must offer full-time telework as a presumptive reasonable accommodation for employees with documented Long COVID, shifting the burden onto agencies to justify why in-person work is required rather than placing the burden on employees to prove they cannot commute or work onsite. The 2-1 decision reverses a district court ruling that had upheld the Commerce Department's denial of remote work to a statistician with Long COVID-related POTS and cognitive dysfunction.

Writing for the majority, Judge Toby Heytens grounded the decision in the Rehabilitation Act's mandate that federal employers operate as model employers of people with disabilities. The court found that the proliferation of effective telework arrangements during and after the pandemic has fundamentally altered the analysis of what constitutes an essential function of most federal positions. "Where an agency has demonstrated, through years of operational practice, that a job can be performed remotely, it cannot subsequently declare in-person presence essential without specific, documented justification tied to the position's core duties," Heytens wrote.

Burden-Shifting Framework

The decision establishes a three-part framework that other circuits are likely to consider. First, an employee with documented Long COVID seeking telework must establish that the condition substantially limits one or more major life activities affected by commuting or in-person work, including symptoms such as orthostatic intolerance, post-exertional malaise, or immune vulnerability. Second, the burden shifts to the agency to articulate specific, position-tied reasons why telework would impose an undue hardship or compromise essential functions. Third, generalized appeals to workplace culture, collaboration, or supervisory preference are insufficient to meet the agency's burden.

The plaintiff, a senior statistician with the Bureau of Economic Analysis, had successfully teleworked for over four years before being required to return to the office three days per week under a 2024 directive. Her cardiologist documented severe orthostatic symptoms triggered by commuting and prolonged upright work. The agency offered a reduced schedule and parking accommodations but refused full telework, citing the return-to-office directive.

Broader Implications

The ruling applies directly to the roughly 350,000 federal employees within the Fourth Circuit's jurisdiction but is expected to influence litigation nationwide. Disability rights organizations, including the Job Accommodation Network and the National Employment Lawyers Association, filed amicus briefs supporting the plaintiff. The Department of Justice has not indicated whether it will seek en banc review or petition the Supreme Court.

The decision arrives amid continued tension between federal return-to-office mandates and the accommodation needs of workers with chronic post-viral conditions, with the Office of Personnel Management estimating that at least 4% of the federal workforce reports ongoing Long COVID symptoms.

Why this matters: By making telework a presumptive accommodation rather than an exceptional one, the Fourth Circuit's ruling could preserve employment for tens of thousands of federal workers whose Long COVID symptoms make commuting and in-person work physiologically untenable.

title: NIH RECOVER-PEDIATRIC Study Finds 4.3% of U.S. Children Meet Criteria for Long COVID, With Adolescent Girls at Highest Risk
excerpt: The largest prospective pediatric Long COVID study to date estimates that 4.3% of U.S. children and adolescents meet research criteria for Long COVID, with adolescent girls experiencing rates more than double those of younger children.
body:
The NIH RECOVER-PEDIATRIC cohort study, published this week in JAMA Pediatrics, estimates that 4.3% of U.S. children and adolescents meet validated research criteria for Long COVID, with adolescent girls aged 13 to 17 experiencing prevalence rates of 7.1%, more than double those observed in children under 12. The study followed 8,400 children across 35 academic medical centers for a median of 24 months following confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection and represents the most comprehensive pediatric Long COVID epidemiology to date.

Investigators used a research index developed specifically for pediatric populations, accounting for developmental variation in symptom expression. The most common persistent symptoms in adolescents included post-exertional malaise (reported by 58% of affected adolescents), cognitive dysfunction interfering with school performance (54%), orthostatic intolerance (47%), and chronic headache (44%). Among children under 12, the symptom profile differed substantially, with somatic complaints such as abdominal pain, sleep disturbance, and behavioral changes predominating.

Educational and Functional Impact

The study quantified educational disruption in stark terms. Among adolescents meeting Long COVID criteria, 31% had missed more than 30 school days in the preceding academic year, 24% had transitioned to part-time or homebound instruction, and 18% reported being unable to participate in any organized physical activity. Standardized cognitive testing demonstrated mean reductions of 0.8 standard deviations in processing speed and 0.6 standard deviations in working memory compared with matched controls who had recovered from acute COVID without persistent symptoms.

Risk factors identified through multivariable analysis included female sex post-puberty, history of allergic disease, obesity, and severity of acute infection. Notably, vaccination prior to infection was associated with a 38% reduction in Long COVID risk, with the strongest protective effect observed in adolescents who had received at least one bivalent or updated monovalent vaccine within 12 months of infection.

Implications for Pediatric Care

The investigators emphasize that pediatric Long COVID has been systematically underdiagnosed, in part because pediatricians lack standardized screening tools and because adolescent symptoms are frequently attributed to anxiety, depression, or school avoidance. The American Academy of Pediatrics announced concurrent publication of updated clinical guidance incorporating the RECOVER-PEDIATRIC findings and recommending that pediatricians screen for post-exertional malaise and orthostatic intolerance in any child with persistent functional decline following SARS-CoV-2 infection.

The study also found pronounced racial and socioeconomic disparities in diagnosis and specialty referral. Black and Latino children with symptoms consistent with Long COVID were 44% less likely to have received a formal diagnosis than white children with comparable symptom profiles, and children with public insurance waited an average of 11 months longer for specialty evaluation than privately insured peers.

Why this matters: With more than 2.5 million U.S. children potentially affected, pediatric Long COVID represents a significant and inequitably distributed driver of educational disruption, disability, and lost developmental potential that demands urgent integration into routine pediatric and school health practice.

title: Phase 3 REGAIN-LC Trial Shows Efgartigimod Reduces Symptoms by 46% in Long COVID Patients With Autoantibody-Mediated Dysautonomia
excerpt: The FcRn inhibitor efgartigimod produced sustained symptom reduction in seropositive Long COVID patients in a multicenter Phase 3 trial, supporting an autoimmune mechanism in a major patient subgroup.
body:
Argenx announced topline results last week from the Phase 3 REGAIN-LC trial showing that efgartigimod, an FcRn inhibitor that reduces circulating IgG, produced a 46% reduction in composite symptom burden in Long COVID patients with documented autoantibodies against autonomic receptors. The 24-week trial enrolled 412 patients across 38 sites in North America and Europe.

Participants were required to have at least one of three autoantibody profiles: antibodies against adrenergic receptors, muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, or angiotensin II type 1 receptors. All participants had symptoms persisting more than 12 months and met diagnostic criteria for either POTS, small fiber neuropathy, or chronic fatigue with post-exertional malaise. Patients received weekly subcutaneous efgartigimod or placebo for 16 weeks, followed by an 8-week observation period.

Trial Design and Primary Endpoints

The primary endpoint, change in the Long COVID Symptom Severity Index, showed a 46% improvement over placebo (p<0.001). Secondary endpoints included orthostatic tolerance measured by 10-minute stand test, cognitive function on the NIH Toolbox, and fatigue measured by the Chalder Fatigue Scale. All secondary endpoints reached statistical significance, with the orthostatic tolerance improvement of 41% being particularly striking given the historical difficulty of treating dysautonomia.

Total IgG levels dropped an average of 61% by week 4, with corresponding reductions in pathogenic autoantibody titers. Patients who achieved greater than 70% IgG reduction showed the strongest clinical response, supporting a dose-response relationship that strengthens the causal inference between autoantibody removal and symptom improvement.

Safety and Mechanism

Adverse events were generally mild, with headache and upper respiratory infections occurring more frequently in the treatment arm. Three serious infections occurred in the efgartigimod group versus one in placebo, consistent with the drug's known safety profile in myasthenia gravis and other approved indications. No deaths or treatment-related discontinuations exceeded 5%.

The mechanism aligns with growing evidence that a subset of Long COVID represents an autoimmune syndrome triggered by molecular mimicry between SARS-CoV-2 antigens and human autonomic receptor proteins. By accelerating clearance of pathogenic IgG without broadly suppressing the immune system, efgartigimod offers a more targeted approach than plasmapheresis or immunoadsorption.

Argenx plans to submit a supplemental Biologics License Application to the FDA in the fourth quarter of 2026, with European Medicines Agency submission expected shortly thereafter. The company estimates that approximately 1.2 million Long COVID patients in the United States carry the relevant autoantibody profiles based on prevalence data from RECOVER cohort analyses.

Why this matters: If approved, efgartigimod would become the first targeted immunomodulatory therapy for autoantibody-positive Long COVID, validating an autoimmune subtype that could redefine clinical phenotyping and treatment selection.

title: Ninth Circuit Rules Employer-Sponsored Wellness Programs Cannot Penalize Long COVID Workers Who Cannot Meet Activity Targets
excerpt: The Ninth Circuit held that wellness program activity requirements violate the ADA when applied to Long COVID workers without individualized reasonable alternatives, reversing a district court ruling.
body:
In a unanimous decision issued May 28, the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled in Hernandez v. Pacific Northwest Health Solutions that employer-sponsored wellness programs imposing step-count or exercise requirements violate the Americans with Disabilities Act when applied to Long COVID employees without offering meaningful individualized alternatives. The decision reverses a Western District of Washington ruling that had granted summary judgment to the employer.

Plaintiff Maria Hernandez, a claims processor diagnosed with Long COVID and post-exertional malaise, lost approximately $1,800 in annual health insurance premium discounts after she was unable to meet her employer's wellness program requirement of 8,000 daily steps. Her physicians had documented that exceeding her activity threshold consistently triggered symptom flares lasting days to weeks, consistent with established pacing protocols.

Court's Reasoning

Writing for the panel, Judge Morgan Christen held that wellness programs offering only alternative activity-based standards, such as reduced step counts, fail to accommodate conditions where any sustained exertion is medically contraindicated. The court emphasized that the WHO clinical practice guideline issued earlier this year specifically warns against graded exercise approaches in Long COVID with post-exertional malaise.

The opinion drew heavily on EEOC regulations requiring wellness programs to provide reasonable alternative standards for individuals whose medical conditions make activity-based metrics inappropriate. The court rejected the employer's argument that offering a health education module as an alternative was sufficient, noting that the module required completion within a 30-day window and imposed cognitive demands incompatible with documented Long COVID cognitive dysfunction.

Broader Implications for Wellness Programs

Approximately 84% of large U.S. employers offer wellness programs with financial incentives tied to activity metrics, according to a 2025 Kaiser Family Foundation survey. The Ninth Circuit decision now requires employers in the circuit's nine states to redesign these programs to include genuinely non-activity-based alternatives, such as health coaching consultations or biometric screening completion without performance thresholds.

The court also addressed the proper damages framework, holding that lost premium discounts and any tax consequences from higher reported compensation are recoverable as compensatory damages under ADA Title I. The case was remanded for trial on damages and Hernandez's parallel claim for failure to engage in the interactive process.

Disability rights organizations including the American Association of People with Disabilities filed amicus briefs supporting Hernandez and praised the ruling as the first appellate decision specifically addressing wellness program discrimination against Long COVID workers. The Chamber of Commerce, which filed an amicus brief supporting the employer, indicated it is evaluating whether to seek en banc review.

Why this matters: The decision establishes that activity-based wellness incentives must accommodate the unique exercise intolerance of Long COVID workers, potentially forcing nationwide redesign of programs that currently financially penalize disabled employees.

title: CDC and FDA Issue Joint Advisory on Misleading Direct-to-Consumer Long COVID Testing After Investigation Finds 73% of Marketed Panels Lack Validation
excerpt: Federal regulators announced enforcement actions against five companies marketing unvalidated Long COVID diagnostic panels after a joint investigation found widespread false claims and clinically misleading results.
body:
The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and the Food and Drug Administration jointly issued a public health advisory on May 22 warning consumers about unvalidated direct-to-consumer Long COVID diagnostic panels, following a multi-agency investigation that found 73% of commercially marketed tests lacked the analytical validation or clinical evidence required for their advertised claims. The advisory accompanies FDA warning letters to five companies and CDC guidance to clinicians on interpreting test results presented by patients.

The investigation, conducted between September 2025 and March 2026, evaluated 41 direct-to-consumer tests marketed as Long COVID diagnostics, biomarker panels, or treatment selection tools. Prices ranged from $295 to $4,800 per panel. Investigators found that 30 of the 41 tests either measured analytes without established clinical relevance to Long COVID, used assay platforms not validated for the marketed analytes, or made therapeutic claims unsupported by peer-reviewed evidence.

Specific Concerns Identified

Common problems included panels claiming to detect spike protein persistence using assays that cross-react with vaccine-induced antibodies, autoantibody panels reporting positive results at clinically meaningless thresholds, and microclot detection services using unstandardized imaging methods without inter-laboratory reproducibility. Several companies marketed expensive cytokine panels as treatment guidance tools despite the absence of any trials demonstrating that cytokine-directed therapy selection improves outcomes.

The five companies receiving warning letters were given 15 business days to respond and either substantiate their claims or cease marketing. The FDA noted that this enforcement action represents the agency's first coordinated effort targeting Long COVID diagnostic fraud and was triggered in part by patient complaints documenting financial harm averaging $3,400 per affected consumer.

Clinical Guidance for Providers

The CDC simultaneously released guidance for clinicians whose patients present with results from unvalidated panels. The guidance recommends that providers acknowledge patient distress, explain the limitations of unvalidated testing without dismissing patient symptoms, and refer to the recently FDA-approved multi-analyte immune signature test as a validated alternative when clinically indicated.

The guidance specifically addresses the increasingly common scenario of patients presenting with extensive but uninterpretable test panels demanding treatment based on findings. CDC recommends that providers focus on validated symptom assessment and established treatments while documenting that unvalidated test results did not guide clinical decisions.

Patient advocacy organizations expressed mixed reactions. Long COVID Alliance praised the consumer protection focus but warned that aggressive enforcement could leave patients without testing options in regions lacking access to specialty clinics. The organization called for expanded insurance coverage of validated diagnostics and continued federal investment in biomarker development.

Why this matters: The advisory marks the first coordinated federal action against Long COVID diagnostic fraud, potentially protecting vulnerable patients from costly unvalidated testing while establishing regulatory standards for an emerging diagnostic market.

title: Phase 3 RESOLVE-LC Trial Shows Nicotinamide Riboside Plus CoQ10 Combination Reduces Long COVID Fatigue by 33% Over 16 Weeks
excerpt: The Phase 3 RESOLVE-LC trial published in The Lancet found that a combination of nicotinamide riboside and coenzyme Q10 significantly improved fatigue and physical function in Long COVID patients with documented mitochondrial dysfunction.
body:
A Phase 3 randomized controlled trial published this week in The Lancet found that daily oral supplementation with nicotinamide riboside (1,000 mg) combined with coenzyme Q10 (400 mg) reduced fatigue scores by 33% in Long COVID patients over 16 weeks compared to placebo. The RESOLVE-LC trial enrolled 612 patients across 28 sites in North America and Europe, all with documented post-exertional malaise and elevated lactate-to-pyruvate ratios suggestive of mitochondrial dysfunction.

Participants receiving the combination therapy showed statistically significant improvements on the Chalder Fatigue Scale, the PROMIS Physical Function score, and the six-minute walk test. Investigators also documented a 28% increase in peripheral blood mononuclear cell NAD+ levels and a 19% reduction in serum 8-hydroxy-2'-deoxyguanosine, a marker of oxidative DNA damage. Importantly, patients in the treatment arm experienced 41% fewer post-exertional symptom flares as measured by daily ecological momentary assessment.

Patient Selection and Mechanism

Lead investigator Dr. Rhonda Patel of the University of Toronto emphasized that the trial intentionally enrolled patients with biochemical evidence of mitochondrial impairment. "This was not a one-size-fits-all approach," Patel said. "We selected patients whose laboratory profiles suggested a bioenergetic deficit, and the response rates reflect that targeted strategy." Subgroup analysis found that patients with the highest baseline lactate-to-pyruvate ratios derived the greatest benefit, with fatigue reductions approaching 45%.

The mechanism is thought to involve replenishment of NAD+ pools depleted by chronic immune activation, combined with restoration of electron transport chain function through CoQ10 supplementation. Skeletal muscle biopsies performed in a substudy of 84 patients showed increased citrate synthase activity and improved complex I and III function in the treatment group.

Safety and Next Steps

Adverse events were generally mild, with gastrointestinal symptoms reported in 12% of treatment arm participants compared to 8% in the placebo group. No serious treatment-related adverse events occurred.
