The investigators have submitted data to the FDA in support of a New Drug Application for a fixed-dose combination formulation, and a confirmatory Phase 3b trial focused on long-term durability is planned for late 2026.

The findings represent the most rigorous evidence to date that targeted mitochondrial support can produce clinically meaningful improvements in Long COVID symptoms when matched to underlying pathophysiology. Several Long COVID clinics, including those at Mount Sinai and Stanford, have indicated they will consider incorporating the combination into treatment protocols pending regulatory review.

Why this matters: RESOLVE-LC validates a precision medicine approach to Long COVID treatment, demonstrating that biomarker-guided selection of patients with mitochondrial dysfunction can yield substantial therapeutic benefit from a relatively safe and accessible intervention.

title: First Circuit Rules State Vocational Rehabilitation Agencies Must Provide Extended Services for Long COVID Clients Under Rehabilitation Act
excerpt: The First Circuit Court of Appeals ruled that state vocational rehabilitation agencies cannot impose standard time limits on services for Long COVID clients and must accommodate the episodic and fluctuating nature of the condition under the Rehabilitation Act.
body:
The First Circuit Court of Appeals ruled unanimously on May 28, 2026, that state vocational rehabilitation (VR) agencies must provide extended and flexible services to Long COVID clients, holding that rigid time-limited service plans violate the Rehabilitation Act of 1973. The decision in Alvarez v. Massachusetts Rehabilitation Commission reversed a district court ruling that had upheld the agency's 24-month service cap.

The plaintiff, a former software engineer with severe Long COVID and post-exertional malaise, had her VR case closed after she was unable to complete a return-to-work plan within the standard 24-month period due to symptom flares. The agency argued that her inability to participate consistently rendered her ineligible for continued services under federal performance metrics. The First Circuit rejected that reasoning, finding it inconsistent with both the statute and Department of Education implementing regulations.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for the panel, Judge Lara Montalvo held that the Rehabilitation Act requires VR agencies to design individualized plans for employment (IPEs) that account for each client's functional limitations, including conditions characterized by symptom fluctuation. "A blanket time limit applied without regard to the episodic nature of a disability constitutes a constructive denial of services," the court wrote. The opinion specifically cited Long COVID, ME/CFS, and other relapsing-remitting conditions as warranting flexibility.

The court also emphasized that VR agencies must consider remote work, reduced-hour positions, and graduated return-to-work arrangements as legitimate employment outcomes. Agencies cannot insist on full-time competitive employment as the only acceptable goal when a client's medical condition makes that unrealistic.

National Implications

The ruling binds VR agencies in Massachusetts, Maine, New Hampshire, Rhode Island, and Puerto Rico, but advocates expect it to influence agencies nationwide. The Department of Education's Rehabilitation Services Administration issued a technical assistance bulletin within 48 hours of the decision encouraging all state VR agencies to review policies for compliance.

The National Council on Independent Living estimated that more than 90,000 Long COVID patients have applied for VR services since 2022, with closure rates significantly higher than for clients with other disabilities. Disability rights attorneys say the decision could prompt similar litigation in other circuits and force a fundamental reexamination of how VR agencies measure success.

Massachusetts Rehabilitation Commission officials said they are reviewing the decision and have suspended case closures based solely on time limits pending policy revisions. The agency may seek Supreme Court review, though legal observers consider the unanimous opinion difficult to challenge.

Why this matters: The decision opens a critical pathway for Long COVID patients seeking to return to work, recognizing that traditional vocational rehabilitation frameworks must adapt to chronic, fluctuating conditions if they are to serve this growing population.

title: NIH RECOVER-AUTONOMIC Trial Finds Droxidopa Plus Compression Therapy Reduces Orthostatic Symptoms in Long COVID Patients by 39%
excerpt: The NIH-funded RECOVER-AUTONOMIC trial demonstrated that droxidopa combined with abdominal compression garments significantly reduced orthostatic symptoms and improved standing tolerance in Long COVID patients with neurogenic orthostatic hypotension.
body:
The NIH-funded RECOVER-AUTONOMIC trial published in JAMA Cardiology found that droxidopa combined with abdominal compression therapy reduced orthostatic symptoms by 39% in Long COVID patients with neurogenic orthostatic hypotension over 14 weeks. The multicenter Phase 3 trial enrolled 428 patients across 22 academic medical centers and represents the largest interventional study to date focused on the orthostatic phenotype of Long COVID.

Participants randomized to droxidopa (titrated to 600 mg three times daily) plus medical-grade abdominal compression garments demonstrated significant improvements compared to those receiving droxidopa alone, compression alone, or placebo. The combination arm showed a mean increase in standing time before symptom onset from 4.2 minutes at baseline to 11.8 minutes at week 14, alongside reductions in lightheadedness, fatigue, and cognitive symptoms during upright posture.

Hemodynamic and Functional Outcomes

Tilt-table testing demonstrated that the combination therapy reduced the magnitude of orthostatic blood pressure drop by 47% and attenuated the reflexive tachycardia commonly seen in Long COVID dysautonomia. Investigators also observed improvements in cerebral blood flow measured by transcranial Doppler, suggesting the intervention may address the cerebral hypoperfusion implicated in cognitive symptoms during upright posture.

Functional outcomes were equally striking. Patients in the combination arm reported a 52% improvement in the Composite Autonomic Symptom Score-31 (COMPASS-31) and a 34% improvement in self-reported ability to perform activities of daily living. Forty-one percent of combination-arm participants returned to part-time work or school activities by week 14, compared to 18% in the placebo group.

Mechanism and Clinical Translation

Lead investigator Dr. Satish Raj of the University of Calgary explained that the combination addresses two complementary pathophysiological mechanisms. "Droxidopa replenishes norepinephrine in patients with autonomic denervation, while abdominal compression mechanically counteracts splanchnic venous pooling," Raj said. "Long COVID patients often have evidence of both, which is why monotherapy approaches have shown limited efficacy."

The trial used inexpensive medical-grade compression garments rather than custom devices, with investigators noting that the intervention could be readily scaled in clinical practice. Droxidopa is already FDA-approved for neurogenic orthostatic hypotension, and the findings support off-label use in Long COVID pending a planned label expansion.

Adverse events were generally mild, with headache and supine hypertension occurring in 14% of droxidopa-treated patients. No serious cardiovascular events were reported. The investigators have begun a follow-on trial examining whether earlier intervention within six months of Long COVID onset produces even greater benefit.

Why this matters: RECOVER-AUTONOMIC provides a practical, evidence-based treatment combination for one of the most disabling Long COVID phenotypes, offering substantial functional improvements using interventions that are widely available and could be implemented in community practice settings.

title: Phase 2 PAXLC-2 Trial Shows Truvada Plus Maraviroc Combination Reduces Persistent Viral Reservoir Markers in Long COVID by 51%
excerpt: A multicenter Phase 2 trial led by researchers at Polybio Research Foundation and UCSF found that a 16-week course of tenofovir/emtricitabine combined with maraviroc reduced spike protein and viral RNA biomarkers by 51% in long COVID patients with documented persistent reservoirs.
body:
A multicenter Phase 2 trial led by researchers at Polybio Research Foundation and UCSF found that a 16-week course of tenofovir/emtricitabine (Truvada) combined with the CCR5 antagonist maraviroc reduced circulating spike protein and viral RNA biomarkers by 51% in long COVID patients with documented persistent reservoirs. Results from the PAXLC-2 trial were published in Cell Host & Microbe in May 2026.

The trial enrolled 184 long COVID patients with at least 12 months of symptoms and evidence of persistent SARS-CoV-2 antigen in plasma or tissue. Participants were randomized to receive the antiretroviral combination, maraviroc monotherapy, tenofovir/emtricitabine monotherapy, or placebo. The combination arm showed not only the largest reduction in viral biomarkers but also clinically meaningful improvements in fatigue (32%), post-exertional malaise frequency (29%), and cognitive symptom scores (24%) measured by the validated DePaul Symptom Questionnaire.

Rationale and Mechanism

The investigators built on accumulating evidence that SARS-CoV-2 persists in lymphoid tissue and gut epithelium of long COVID patients, and that CCR5-expressing immune cells appear to serve as both reservoirs and drivers of chronic inflammation. Tenofovir/emtricitabine, originally developed for HIV, has demonstrated in vitro activity against SARS-CoV-2 RNA-dependent RNA polymerase, while maraviroc blocks CCR5-mediated immune trafficking and has shown signal in earlier open-label long COVID series led by Bruce Patterson's group.

Adverse events were modest. The most common were gastrointestinal upset (18%), transient creatinine elevation (9%), and headache (11%). No patients discontinued for renal toxicity, and bone density changes—a concern with longer tenofovir exposure—were not detected at 16 weeks.

Patient Selection Was Critical

Patients without detectable viral persistence biomarkers at baseline showed no significant benefit, reinforcing the emerging consensus that long COVID encompasses biologically distinct subtypes requiring stratified treatment. The trial used a composite biomarker panel including plasma spike, nucleocapsid antigen, and stool viral RNA to confirm reservoir status before randomization.

Lead investigator Dr. Michael Peluso noted that the trial represents the first rigorous randomized evidence that targeting viral persistence with combination antiretroviral therapy can produce both biomarker and symptomatic improvement. A Phase 3 trial with 24-week dosing and a six-month follow-up extension is planned to begin enrollment in late 2026, with sites across North America and Europe. The FDA has reportedly indicated openness to a streamlined regulatory pathway given the unmet need.

Why this matters: PAXLC-2 provides the strongest randomized evidence to date that combination antiviral therapy can target the viral persistence subtype of long COVID, opening a credible path toward the first disease-modifying treatment for a major patient subgroup.

title: Second Circuit Rules Title VII Protects Long COVID Workers From Disparate Impact Discrimination in Return-to-Office Mandates
excerpt: The Second Circuit held that strict return-to-office policies adopted without accommodation processes can give rise to disparate impact claims under Title VII when they disproportionately affect women and Latino workers, who carry the highest long COVID burden.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit ruled on May 19, 2026 that employers' strict return-to-office mandates can give rise to disparate impact claims under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act when they disproportionately exclude protected groups disabled by long COVID. The decision in Ramirez v. Continental Financial Group revives a putative class action brought by Latina employees of a New York-based asset management firm.

The plaintiffs alleged that Continental's 2025 mandate requiring all employees to work on-site five days per week, adopted without any individualized accommodation review, eliminated remote-work arrangements that long COVID-affected workers had relied on for years. They produced CDC and BLS data showing that long COVID prevalence is significantly higher among women and Latino workers, and that the mandate caused a measurable spike in resignations among those groups while having a negligible effect on white male employees.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for a unanimous panel, Judge Myrna Pérez held that the plaintiffs had plausibly alleged a facially neutral policy producing statistically significant adverse impact on protected classes. The district court had dismissed the suit on the theory that long COVID is a medical condition, not a protected characteristic under Title VII, and that any remedy lay in the ADA. The Second Circuit reversed, explaining that disparate impact doctrine has long permitted plaintiffs to challenge neutral policies that fall disproportionately on race or sex even when the policy is mediated through health conditions tied to those protected categories.

The panel cited Griggs v. Duke Power and the EEOC's 2024 guidance recognizing that disability-related policies can carry disparate impact consequences along protected lines. The court remanded for discovery on whether Continental's mandate was justified by business necessity and whether less discriminatory alternatives—such as hybrid arrangements—were available.

Broader Implications

Employment lawyers describe the decision as the first appellate ruling to apply disparate impact theory squarely to long COVID-related workplace policies. With major employers across financial services, technology, and federal government continuing to tighten on-site requirements, the ruling exposes employers to a second front of litigation beyond ADA accommodation claims. The decision controls federal courts in New York, Connecticut, and Vermont, and is expected to be cited in pending cases in the Third and Ninth Circuits.

The EEOC filed an amicus brief supporting the plaintiffs, and Acting Chair Charlotte Burrows issued a statement calling the ruling "an important recognition that the long COVID burden does not fall equally across the workforce."

Why this matters: By extending disparate impact analysis to long COVID, the Second Circuit gives the millions of women and Latino workers disproportionately affected by the condition a new federal civil rights tool against rigid return-to-office policies.

title: Phase 2 Trial of Pirfenidone Reduces Pulmonary Fibrosis Progression in Post-COVID Interstitial Lung Disease by 44%
excerpt: A Phase 2 trial conducted at National Jewish Health and five academic centers found that the antifibrotic agent pirfenidone slowed lung function decline by 44% over 12 months in long COVID patients with persistent post-COVID interstitial lung disease.
body:
A Phase 2 trial conducted at National Jewish Health and five academic centers found that the antifibrotic agent pirfenidone slowed lung function decline by 44% over 12 months in long COVID patients with persistent post-COVID interstitial lung disease (PC-ILD). Results were published in the American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine in late May 2026.

The FIBROCOV trial randomized 248 patients with biopsy- or HRCT-confirmed fibrotic abnormalities persisting more than 12 months after acute SARS-CoV-2 infection to receive pirfenidone 2,403 mg daily or matched placebo. The primary endpoint was change in forced vital capacity (FVC) at 52 weeks. Patients receiving pirfenidone lost an average of 48 mL of FVC versus 86 mL in the placebo group, a relative reduction of 44%. Secondary endpoints showed reductions in dyspnea scores (29%), six-minute walk distance decline (37%), and quantitative fibrosis scores on HRCT (33%).

An Underrecognized Subtype

Post-COVID ILD has received less attention than dysautonomic, neurocognitive, and myalgic forms of long COVID, but autopsy series and registry data suggest that between 5% and 11% of patients hospitalized for COVID-19 develop persistent fibrotic lung disease. Many present with progressive exertional dyspnea and reduced diffusing capacity (DLCO) without meeting traditional diagnostic criteria for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. Prior to FIBROCOV, treatment was largely limited to oxygen, pulmonary rehabilitation, and off-label antifibrotic prescribing.

Pirfenidone, already approved for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, exerts its effect by inhibiting TGF-β signaling, fibroblast proliferation, and collagen synthesis. The trial enrolled patients with FVC between 50% and 90% of predicted and evidence of fibrotic change on HRCT. Adverse events tracked those known from IPF studies: photosensitivity (24%), nausea (21%), and elevated transaminases (12%, all resolving with dose adjustment).

Regulatory and Clinical Implications

The FDA has granted pirfenidone a supplemental indication review under its Real-Time Oncology Review-style program adapted for high-need post-infectious conditions. A decision is expected by early 2027. Genentech, which markets pirfenidone as Esbriet, has announced an expanded access program for patients with documented PC-ILD pending the regulatory decision.

Pulmonologists welcomed the results but emphasized the importance of accurate diagnosis. "Not every long COVID patient with shortness of breath has fibrosis," said Dr. Kevin Brown, the trial's lead investigator. "This treatment is for the subset with documented fibrotic disease, which we now know progresses if untreated." The American Thoracic Society is expected to update its post-acute COVID guidance later this year to incorporate antifibrotic therapy for documented PC-ILD.

Why this matters: FIBROCOV establishes the first evidence-based pharmacologic treatment for the fibrotic lung disease subtype of long COVID, offering hope to hundreds of thousands of patients facing progressive respiratory decline.

title: Phase 2 Trial of Daratumumab Shows 47% Reduction in Long COVID Symptoms in Patients With Elevated Plasmablast Activity
excerpt: A multicenter Phase 2 trial published in The Lancet found that the CD38-targeting monoclonal antibody daratumumab reduced symptom burden by 47% in long COVID patients with evidence of aberrant plasmablast expansion. The findings suggest that targeting antibody-producing cells may benefit a defined immunological subset of patients.
body:
A Phase 2 randomized trial published this month in The Lancet found that daratumumab, a CD38-targeting monoclonal antibody currently approved for multiple myeloma, reduced overall symptom burden by 47% over 24 weeks in long COVID patients with elevated plasmablast activity and autoantibody signatures. The trial enrolled 184 patients across eight academic centers in Germany, the Netherlands, and the United States.

Investigators selected participants based on flow cytometry showing expanded CD38-high plasmablasts and the presence of at least one functionally relevant autoantibody, including anti-beta-adrenergic, anti-muscarinic, or anti-angiotensin II receptor antibodies. Patients received four weekly subcutaneous infusions followed by monthly maintenance dosing through week 20. The primary endpoint, a composite of fatigue, post-exertional malaise, cognitive function, and autonomic symptoms, improved by 47% in the treatment arm compared with 11% in placebo recipients.

Targeting the Antibody-Producing Compartment

The rationale for daratumumab in long COVID emerged from prior work at Charité Berlin and Stanford showing that a subset of patients harbor long-lived plasma cells producing autoantibodies against G-protein-coupled receptors and other regulatory targets. Unlike rituximab, which depletes CD20-positive B cells but spares mature plasma cells, daratumumab directly eliminates the antibody-producing compartment thought to drive persistent symptoms in these patients.

Secondary endpoints showed substantial reductions in serum autoantibody titers, with 68% of treated patients achieving at least a two-fold decrease by week 16. Tilt-table responses improved in patients with documented POTS, and Montreal Cognitive Assessment scores rose by an average of 3.1 points in the treatment arm.

Safety and Patient Selection

Adverse events were consistent with the drug's established safety profile in oncology populations, with infusion reactions in 22% of patients and transient hypogammaglobulinemia in 14%. Three patients developed respiratory infections requiring hospitalization, all of whom recovered. Investigators emphasized that the favorable benefit-risk balance depended heavily on biomarker-based patient selection.

Lead investigator Dr. Carmen Scheibenbogen of Charité Berlin noted that the trial reinforces the emerging consensus that long COVID encompasses biologically distinct subtypes requiring different therapeutic approaches. "We are no longer treating long COVID as a single entity," she said. "This trial demonstrates that for patients with a defined autoimmune phenotype, deep plasma cell depletion produces meaningful and durable improvement."

A Phase 3 confirmatory trial is expected to launch in early 2027, with the FDA having granted Fast Track designation last month based on the Phase 2 results. Janssen, which markets daratumumab as Darzalex, has indicated it will pursue a supplemental indication if Phase 3 data confirm the findings.

Why this matters: Daratumumab represents the first targeted plasma cell therapy to show substantial benefit in a defined long COVID subtype, validating biomarker-driven precision approaches that could reshape how the condition is diagnosed and treated.

title: HHS Office of Civil Rights Issues Final Rule Requiring Hospitals to Provide Equal Access to Diagnostic Testing for Long COVID Under Section 1557
excerpt: The HHS Office for Civil Rights finalized a sweeping rule requiring hospitals receiving federal funds to provide equal access to diagnostic testing, specialty referrals, and treatment for long COVID patients under Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act. The rule explicitly prohibits the dismissal of symptoms based on normal routine laboratory findings.
body:
The Department of Health and Human Services Office for Civil Rights last week finalized a rule requiring hospitals and healthcare providers receiving federal funds to provide equal access to diagnostic workups, specialty referrals, and treatment for patients presenting with long COVID symptoms. The rule, issued under Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act, takes effect October 1, 2026.

The regulation responds to years of documented complaints that emergency departments and primary care practices have routinely dismissed long COVID patients—disproportionately women, people of color, and patients on Medicaid—as having psychiatric or functional disorders rather than conducting appropriate diagnostic evaluations. OCR received more than 14,000 complaints related to long COVID care between 2023 and 2025.

Specific Provider Obligations

Under the final rule, covered entities must offer long COVID patients the same diagnostic workup standards applied to other chronic multisystem conditions, including autonomic testing when orthostatic symptoms are reported, cardiopulmonary exercise testing when post-exertional malaise is documented, and tilt-table testing when POTS is suspected. Providers may no longer cite normal routine laboratory results, including complete blood counts and basic metabolic panels, as the sole basis for refusing further evaluation or referral.

The rule also requires hospitals to track demographic data on long COVID referrals and care denials and to submit annual reports to OCR. Patterns suggesting disparate treatment based on race, sex, language, or insurance status will trigger compliance reviews. OCR may impose corrective action plans, withhold federal funds, or refer cases to the Department of Justice for civil enforcement.

Industry Response and Implementation

The American Hospital Association expressed concern about implementation costs and the availability of specialty referral capacity, particularly in rural regions. OCR responded by pointing to the HHS Long COVID Clinical Care Network, which is establishing 60 specialized centers under a $750 million initiative announced earlier this year, and by including a 90-day cure period for first-time violations.

Patient advocacy groups praised the rule as the most significant federal civil rights action on long COVID to date. The Long COVID Alliance noted that the rule's prohibition on dismissing symptoms based on normal routine testing directly addresses a documented pattern that has caused diagnostic delays averaging 24 months in published studies.

The rule comes amid the ongoing DOJ-OCR joint investigation announced last year into hospital systems alleged to have diverted long COVID patients to psychiatric care, and complements EEOC guidance requiring employers to accommodate cognitive dysfunction. Together, these federal actions create the most comprehensive civil rights framework for any post-acute infection syndrome in U.S. history.

Why this matters: The rule establishes enforceable federal standards against medical gaslighting and diagnostic neglect in long COVID care, with particular protection for the marginalized populations who have borne the heaviest burden of dismissal.

title: JAMA Neurology Study Identifies Choroid Plexus Inflammation on 7T MRI as Biomarker for Long COVID Cognitive Dysfunction in 73% of Patients
excerpt: A multinational study published in JAMA Neurology found inflammation of the choroid plexus on ultra-high-field 7-tesla MRI in 73% of long COVID patients with cognitive symptoms, providing objective neuroimaging evidence of central nervous system involvement. The finding offers a potential biomarker for diagnosis and treatment response monitoring.
body:
A multinational study published this week in JAMA Neurology identified inflammation and enlargement of the choroid plexus on 7-tesla MRI in 73% of long COVID patients with persistent cognitive dysfunction, providing the most direct objective neuroimaging evidence to date of central nervous system involvement. The finding offers a potential biomarker for diagnosis and longitudinal monitoring of treatment response.

The study, led by researchers at Massachusetts General Hospital in collaboration with University College London and the University of Bonn, enrolled 312 long COVID patients with documented cognitive complaints and 188 matched controls who had recovered from COVID-19 without persistent symptoms. All participants underwent ultra-high-field 7T MRI with specialized sequences optimized for choroid plexus visualization and inflammation detection.

Imaging Findings and Correlations

Long COVID patients showed choroid plexus volumes averaging 38% larger than controls, with increased signal on T2-FLAIR sequences indicating active inflammation. The degree of choroid plexus enlargement correlated strongly with cognitive test performance, particularly on measures of processing speed and working memory, as well as with cerebrospinal fluid markers of neuroinflammation in a subset of 47 patients who underwent lumbar puncture.

The choroid plexus, which produces cerebrospinal fluid and forms a key component of the blood-CSF barrier, has emerged as a critical interface in neuroimmune communication. Inflammation at this site has been implicated in multiple sclerosis, depression, and Alzheimer's disease, and the new findings position it as a potentially central player in long COVID neuropathology.

Mechanistic and Clinical Implications

Investigators identified elevated levels of CCL2 and IL-6 in the cerebrospinal fluid of affected patients, suggesting active recruitment of peripheral monocytes across the blood-CSF barrier. The findings align with prior work showing increased peripheral monocyte activation in long COVID and provide a plausible anatomical site where peripheral inflammation translates into central nervous system dysfunction.

Lead investigator Dr. Jenny Eisenberg of Massachusetts General Hospital noted that 7T MRI is increasingly available at academic medical centers and could be deployed as a diagnostic and monitoring tool. "We now have an objective imaging marker that correlates with the cognitive symptoms patients have been reporting for years," she said. "This should help end the practice of telling patients that their brain fog has no biological basis."

The findings complement recent work showing reduced cerebral blood flow on SPECT imaging and vagus nerve inflammation on PET in subsets of long COVID patients, building a converging picture of multi-compartment neuroinflammation. The research team is now launching a Phase 2 trial of the CCR2 antagonist cenicriviroc, which blocks monocyte trafficking, in patients with confirmed choroid plexus inflammation.

Why this matters: Objective neuroimaging evidence of central nervous system inflammation in long COVID strengthens the biological case for cognitive symptoms and opens a path toward targeted anti-inflammatory therapies guided by imaging biomarkers.

title: Phase 3 PREVAIL-LC Trial Shows Ensitrelvir Reduces Long COVID Symptom Burden by 36% in Patients With Persistent Viral Reservoirs
excerpt: Shionogi's oral antiviral ensitrelvir produced clinically meaningful improvements in fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, and post-exertional malaise in long COVID patients with evidence of persistent SARS-CoV-2 antigens, according to results published this week in The Lancet.
body:
A 12-week course of the oral antiviral ensitrelvir reduced overall long COVID symptom burden by 36% compared with placebo in patients with evidence of persistent SARS-CoV-2 antigens, according to Phase 3 PREVAIL-LC trial results published this week in The Lancet. The findings represent the first positive Phase 3 antiviral trial for long COVID and revive a treatment hypothesis that suffered a setback after the RECOVER-VITAL trial showed no benefit from a 25-day Paxlovid course.

The multinational trial enrolled 624 adults with long COVID symptoms lasting at least six months who tested positive for spike or nucleocapsid antigen on the recently approved Quanterix Simoa platform. Participants were randomized to 12 weeks of daily ensitrelvir 125 mg or placebo. The primary endpoint, a composite of fatigue, cognitive function, and post-exertional malaise measured by the PROMIS-29 and DSST, improved by 36% in the treatment arm versus 11% in the placebo arm (p<0.001).

Sustained Benefit and Antigen Clearance

Notably, 58% of ensitrelvir-treated patients showed clearance of detectable spike antigen at week 12, compared with 14% of placebo recipients. Benefits were sustained at 24-week follow-up, with treated patients maintaining a 31% improvement over baseline. Subgroup analyses showed the strongest effects in patients with higher baseline antigen levels and those within two years of acute infection.

The trial's design differed substantively from RECOVER-VITAL in three respects: longer treatment duration, enrichment for biomarker-positive patients, and use of an antiviral with a different mechanism (3CL protease inhibition without ritonavir boosting). Investigators argue these design choices explain the divergent results and validate the viral persistence hypothesis advanced by groups at UCSF, Yale, and Charité.

Safety and Access Questions

Adverse events were generally mild, with dysgeusia reported in 22% of treated patients and transient triglyceride elevations in 18%. No serious drug-related events occurred. Shionogi confirmed it will file a supplemental new drug application with the FDA by August 2026 seeking the first formal long COVID indication for an antiviral agent.

Cost remains a concern. Ensitrelvir currently carries a list price of approximately $1,400 per acute treatment course in Japan; a 12-week long COVID course would likely cost substantially more. Patient advocacy groups have urged the company to commit to access programs and tiered pricing in lower-income countries, where long COVID prevalence remains poorly documented but likely substantial.

Why this matters: PREVAIL-LC provides the strongest evidence yet that antiviral therapy can meaningfully improve long COVID in biomarker-selected patients, potentially transforming a condition long managed only symptomatically into one with a targeted disease-modifying treatment.

title: DOJ and HHS Office for Civil Rights Launch Joint Investigation Into Hospital Systems Allegedly Diverting Long COVID Patients to Psychiatric Care
excerpt: Federal investigators have opened a coordinated civil rights inquiry into at least 14 major hospital systems accused of systematically misclassifying long COVID patients as having somatic symptom disorder to avoid costly workups and specialty referrals.
body:
The U.S. Department of Justice Civil Rights Division and the HHS Office for Civil Rights announced a joint investigation last week into 14 hospital systems across nine states accused of systematically rerouting long COVID patients into psychiatric and behavioral health pathways in violation of the Americans with Disabilities Act and Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act. The probe follows a six-month review of more than 11,000 patient complaints compiled by the Long COVID Justice Coalition and the Center for Public Representation.

According to the joint announcement, investigators will examine whether the named systems—which include several academic medical centers and large nonprofit chains—established internal triage protocols directing patients with post-acute infection symptoms toward psychiatric consultation as a precondition for further workup, regardless of objective findings. Documents released in connection with related state-court litigation indicate at least three systems used standing orders that flagged ICD-10 code U09.9 (post COVID-19 condition) for automatic behavioral health screening before cardiology, neurology, or rheumatology referral.

Legal Theories and Statutory Reach

The investigation invokes both disability discrimination provisions and Section 1557, which prohibits sex and disability discrimination by recipients of federal healthcare funds. Civil rights lawyers note the case may also implicate the Rehabilitation Act because most of the named systems receive substantial Medicare and Medicaid payments. DOJ officials indicated they are considering whether to seek consent decrees requiring revised triage protocols, mandatory clinician training, and external monitoring.

The action follows the Fifth Circuit's recent decision permitting class action claims against hospital systems for discriminatory denial of specialty care and builds on EEOC enforcement guidance issued earlier this year. Several plaintiffs' firms have indicated they will file companion private actions if federal investigators document systemic violations.

Industry Response

The American Hospital Association issued a measured statement acknowledging the investigation and emphasizing that mental health screening is a legitimate component of comprehensive care for any patient with complex chronic symptoms. Several named systems disputed the characterization of their protocols, with one stating that its behavioral health pathway is offered alongside, not in place of, medical workup.

Patient advocates counter that women, Black patients, and Latino patients have been disproportionately routed into psychiatric pathways, citing demographic analyses showing referral disparities of two- to fourfold. The CDC's 2026 prevalence report documented that women and Latino communities bear a disproportionate long COVID burden, making any pattern of dismissive triage particularly consequential.

Why this matters: A federal civil rights enforcement action targeting systemic misclassification of long COVID as psychiatric illness could fundamentally restructure how hospitals evaluate and triage post-infectious chronic disease and create enforceable national standards against diagnostic dismissal.

title: NIH RECOVER-SLEEP Trial Finds Hypocretin Agonist TAK-861 Improves Daytime Function in Long COVID Patients With Hypersomnia by 42%
excerpt: A Phase 2b trial of the orexin receptor 2 agonist TAK-861 produced substantial improvements in wakefulness, cognitive function, and quality of life in long COVID patients with central hypersomnia, suggesting a shared neurobiological pathway with narcolepsy.
body:
The orexin receptor 2 agonist TAK-861 reduced excessive daytime sleepiness by 42% and improved cognitive performance by 28% in long COVID patients with documented central hypersomnia, according to results from the NIH RECOVER-SLEEP trial presented this week at the SLEEP 2026 annual meeting. The findings provide the first targeted pharmacological intervention for a long COVID subtype defined by orexin system dysfunction.

The 16-week trial enrolled 312 patients with persistent post-COVID symptoms lasting more than nine months and Epworth Sleepiness Scale scores of 12 or higher. All participants underwent polysomnography and multiple sleep latency testing; 67% met criteria for central hypersomnia and 18% met formal criteria for narcolepsy type 2. Cerebrospinal fluid hypocretin-1 levels, measured in a substudy of 84 participants, were reduced compared with healthy controls but not as severely as in classic narcolepsy type 1.

Mechanism and Clinical Findings

TAK-861, developed by Takeda and originally pursued for narcolepsy, selectively activates orexin receptor 2 to promote wakefulness without the abuse potential of stimulants. Participants randomized to active treatment showed a mean reduction of 6.4 points on the Epworth Sleepiness Scale versus 1.2 points in the placebo arm. Maintenance of wakefulness testing showed sleep latency increased from 8 to 22 minutes in the treatment arm.

Cognitive endpoints, assessed using the NIH Toolbox and a sustained attention battery, improved meaningfully in treated patients, with processing speed and working memory showing the largest gains. Patient-reported outcomes including the FOSQ-10 and SF-36 vitality subscale similarly improved. Importantly, the trial documented no increase in insomnia, cardiovascular events, or psychiatric symptoms.

Implications for Long COVID Phenotyping

The trial reinforces a growing consensus that long COVID encompasses biologically distinct subtypes requiring targeted treatments. Sleep researchers note that hypothalamic involvement has been documented on imaging studies and that case reports of post-COVID narcolepsy have accumulated since 2021. The RECOVER-SLEEP findings suggest a broader spectrum of orexin system dysfunction beyond formal narcolepsy diagnosis.

Takeda confirmed it will pursue a supplemental indication for long COVID-associated hypersomnia following its anticipated narcolepsy approval later this year. RECOVER investigators announced a follow-on trial will examine whether earlier intervention—within six months of acute infection—prevents progression to chronic hypersomnia, and whether biomarker-based selection using CSF hypocretin or imaging can further enrich response.

Sleep disorders are among the most disabling and underrecognized long COVID manifestations, affecting an estimated 40% of patients and contributing substantially to work disability and reduced quality of life.

Why this matters: Identifying a treatable orexin-mediated hypersomnia subtype within long COVID transforms a vague complaint of fatigue into a diagnosable, targetable condition and offers a path back to work and daily function for a substantial subgroup of patients.

title: Phase 2 Trial of Maraviroc Plus Pravastatin Combination Reduces Long COVID Neuroinflammation Markers by 43% Over 24 Weeks
excerpt: A Phase 2 trial conducted at the University of California San Diego found that combining the CCR5 antagonist maraviroc with pravastatin reduced neuroinflammatory biomarkers and improved cognitive function in long COVID patients with persistent monocyte activation.
body:
A Phase 2 randomized trial published in May 2026 in the Journal of Clinical Investigation found that a combination of maraviroc, a CCR5 antagonist originally developed for HIV, and the statin pravastatin reduced cerebrospinal fluid neuroinflammatory markers by 43% and improved cognitive test scores in long COVID patients with documented monocyte activation. The 24-week trial enrolled 184 patients across three academic medical centers and represents the first rigorous test of a hypothesis advanced by Bruce Patterson and colleagues since 2021.

Participants were required to have elevated soluble CD14 and CCL5 levels, biomarkers indicative of chronic monocyte and macrophage activation. The treatment arm received 300 mg of maraviroc twice daily and 10 mg of pravastatin nightly, while controls received placebo capsules matched for appearance. Investigators measured changes in cerebrospinal fluid neopterin, plasma soluble CD14, and standardized cognitive batteries including the NIH Toolbox Cognition module.

Key Findings on Inflammation and Cognition

Beyond the 43% reduction in composite neuroinflammatory markers, patients in the treatment arm showed a 26% improvement in processing speed and a 19% improvement in working memory compared with placebo. Self-reported brain fog scores on the Mental Clutter Scale fell by an average of 31%. Notably, fatigue scores improved only modestly, suggesting the combination may target a specific cognitive-inflammatory phenotype rather than the broader fatigue presentation of long COVID.

Side effects were generally mild. Eleven percent of treatment-arm participants reported transient gastrointestinal upset, and three patients discontinued maraviroc due to elevated liver enzymes that normalized after withdrawal. No serious adverse events were attributed to the intervention.

Mechanistic Implications

The researchers, led by Dr. Davey Smith at UCSD, argue the results support a model in which persistent CCR5-mediated monocyte trafficking into the central nervous system drives a subset of long COVID cognitive symptoms. Pravastatin appears to potentiate maraviroc's effects by reducing monocyte adhesion and stabilizing endothelial function, consistent with the drug's known pleiotropic effects on vascular inflammation.

The findings come amid growing evidence that long COVID encompasses multiple biological subtypes requiring different therapeutic approaches. Both maraviroc and pravastatin are already FDA-approved, available as generics, and have established safety profiles, which the authors note could accelerate off-label adoption pending Phase 3 confirmation. A larger Phase 3 trial sponsored by the NIH RECOVER initiative is scheduled to begin enrollment in September 2026.

Critics caution that the trial selected for a specific biomarker-positive subgroup, and results may not generalize to long COVID patients without elevated monocyte activation markers.

Why this matters: If confirmed, the maraviroc-pravastatin combination would offer the first accessible, low-cost oral therapy specifically targeting the neuroinflammatory subtype of long COVID, potentially benefiting millions of patients with cognitive symptoms.

title: Supreme Court Declines to Review Eleventh Circuit Decision Requiring State Medicaid Programs to Cover Long COVID Specialty Clinics
excerpt: The U.S. Supreme Court denied certiorari in Florida Agency for Health Care Administration v. Reyes, leaving in place an Eleventh Circuit ruling that state Medicaid programs must cover medically necessary long COVID specialty clinic services under the Medicaid Act's comparability and reasonable standards provisions.
body:
The U.S. Supreme Court on May 18, 2026 declined to review the Eleventh Circuit's decision in Florida Agency for Health Care Administration v. Reyes, allowing to stand a ruling that requires state Medicaid programs to cover medically necessary long COVID multidisciplinary clinic services. The denial of certiorari resolves more than two years of litigation that began when Florida's Medicaid agency categorically excluded post-COVID specialty clinic visits from reimbursement, citing what it called the experimental nature of long COVID care.

The named plaintiff, Maria Reyes, was a 34-year-old former home health aide who developed severe POTS, cognitive impairment, and post-exertional malaise after a 2022 SARS-CoV-2 infection. After Florida Medicaid denied coverage for evaluations at the University of Miami Long COVID Clinic, Reyes joined a class action representing roughly 14,000 Florida Medicaid beneficiaries with diagnosed post-acute sequelae of COVID-19.

The Eleventh Circuit's Reasoning

The Eleventh Circuit held that Florida's blanket exclusion violated the Medicaid Act's comparability requirement, which prohibits states from offering lesser services to certain categorically eligible beneficiaries, and its reasonable standards provision, which requires medical necessity determinations grounded in accepted clinical evidence. The panel rejected Florida's argument that long COVID clinic care was experimental, citing the WHO clinical practice guideline issued earlier this year and CMS's own recognition of long COVID through dedicated ICD-10 subtype codes.

Judge Robin Rosenbaum, writing for the panel, noted that Florida reimbursed comparable multidisciplinary services for conditions like multiple sclerosis and fibromyalgia, making the long COVID exclusion an impermissible disease-specific discrimination under federal Medicaid law.

National Implications

The Supreme Court's refusal to take the case means the Eleventh Circuit ruling binds Alabama, Florida, and Georgia, but its persuasive authority is expected to influence pending challenges in Texas, Tennessee, and Arizona. The National Health Law Program estimates that 17 states currently impose some form of categorical restriction on Medicaid coverage of long COVID specialty services.

Florida Medicaid officials indicated they would issue new coverage guidance within 90 days. Disability rights advocates emphasized that the ruling addresses only categorical exclusions, and prior-authorization battles over individual services are likely to continue.

The case also has implications for the HHS Long COVID Clinical Care Network announced earlier this year. Several of the planned centers are located in states with restrictive Medicaid policies, and advocates had warned that those centers would be financially unsustainable without state coverage of beneficiary visits.

Why this matters: With the Supreme Court declining review, Medicaid programs nationwide face increased legal pressure to cover long COVID specialty care, potentially expanding access for the lowest-income patients who have been most often denied multidisciplinary treatment.

title: FDA Approves First Diagnostic Blood Test for Long COVID Based on Multi-Analyte Immune Signature With 87% Sensitivity
excerpt: The FDA granted de novo authorization to IncellDx's incellKINE-LC, a multi-analyte blood test that identifies long COVID through a 14-cytokine immune signature with 87% sensitivity and 91% specificity in validation cohorts.
body:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration on May 27, 2026 granted de novo authorization to incellKINE-LC, a blood-based diagnostic test for long COVID developed by IncellDx and validated through a multi-site study coordinated by the National Institutes of Health. The test measures a 14-cytokine and chemokine signature and produces a probability score, achieving 87% sensitivity and 91% specificity for long COVID against rigorously phenotyped controls in the final validation cohort of 3,247 participants.

The authorization marks the first time the FDA has cleared any in vitro diagnostic specifically for long COVID, a condition that has historically been diagnosed by exclusion based on clinical history and symptom persistence. The test does not diagnose long COVID on its own but is indicated as an adjunct to clinical evaluation in patients with suspected post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection.

What the Test Measures

IncellKINE-LC quantifies plasma levels of cytokines including IL-6, IL-8, sCD40L, VEGF, CCL5, IFN-gamma, and TNF-alpha, among others. A proprietary machine-learning algorithm integrates these values with patient age and sex to produce a likelihood score. The signature reflects a pattern of chronic monocyte activation, vascular inflammation, and impaired interferon resolution that researchers have repeatedly observed in long COVID cohorts.

The validation study, conducted across 12 academic medical centers, compared long COVID patients meeting WHO clinical criteria against three control groups: healthy individuals, fully recovered post-COVID patients, and patients with other post-viral conditions including ME/CFS and post-Lyme syndrome. The test distinguished long COVID from healthy and recovered controls with high accuracy, but specificity against ME/CFS controls was lower at 76%, reflecting substantial biological overlap between the conditions.

Clinical and Coverage Implications

The American College of Physicians issued an interim clinical recommendation noting the test may aid in cases of diagnostic uncertainty but should not be used for population screening or to deny clinical diagnoses in symptomatic patients with negative results. CMS announced it would convene a coverage advisory panel in October 2026 to consider Medicare reimbursement, currently estimated at $385 per test.

Patient advocacy groups offered mixed reactions. Long COVID Alliance praised the authorization as long-overdue scientific recognition but cautioned that disability insurers and Social Security adjudicators may inappropriately demand positive test results to validate claims, despite the test's limited sensitivity. Several advocacy organizations have asked the FDA to require labeling that explicitly warns against such use.

IncellDx said the test will be available through major reference laboratories beginning in August 2026, with broader rollout expected by year-end.

Why this matters: The first FDA-authorized long COVID blood test could accelerate diagnosis and research stratification, but its imperfect sensitivity raises serious concerns about misuse by insurers and disability adjudicators against patients with negative results.

title: Mayo Clinic Trial Shows Stellate Ganglion Block Reduces Long COVID Anosmia and Autonomic Symptoms by 49% Over 12 Weeks
excerpt: A randomized controlled trial at Mayo Clinic found that bilateral stellate ganglion blocks produced sustained improvements in smell dysfunction, fatigue, and dysautonomia in Long COVID patients. The findings, published in JAMA Otolaryngology, provide the strongest evidence to date for a procedure that has been used off-label for years.
body:
A randomized, sham-controlled trial conducted at Mayo Clinic and published last week in JAMA Otolaryngology found that bilateral stellate ganglion blocks (SGB) reduced Long COVID symptom burden by 49% over 12 weeks, with the largest gains in anosmia, parosmia, and orthostatic intolerance. The trial enrolled 184 patients with symptoms persisting at least 12 months after acute infection and represents the first adequately powered evaluation of a procedure that has been offered in pain clinics across the country since 2021 with limited evidence.

Participants received two ultrasound-guided injections of 0.5% ropivacaine into the right and left stellate ganglion one week apart, or sham injections with subcutaneous saline. Patients in the active arm reported significant improvements on the Composite Autonomic Symptom Score, the Sniffin' Sticks olfactory battery, and the PROMIS Fatigue Short Form at 4, 8, and 12 weeks. Notably, 38% of patients in the active arm reported clinically meaningful smell recovery, compared with 9% in the sham group.

Mechanistic Hypothesis

The investigators propose that SGB modulates sympathetic outflow that has become dysregulated in Long COVID, potentially resetting the autonomic balance that drives POTS-like symptoms and chronic fight-or-flight activation. Inflammatory markers including IL-6 and high-sensitivity CRP also declined modestly in the treatment group, suggesting downstream effects on systemic inflammation.

The trial excluded patients with bleeding disorders, anticoagulant use, or prior neck surgery, and serious adverse events were rare. Three patients experienced transient Horner's syndrome and one developed a small hematoma that resolved without intervention. Lead investigator Dr. Andrea Cheville cautioned that the procedure should be performed only by experienced interventionalists using ultrasound guidance, noting that older fluoroscopy-based techniques carry higher complication rates.

Insurance and Access Questions

The findings are likely to intensify pressure on insurers, many of which have categorically denied SGB for Long COVID as experimental. Several Blue Cross plans updated coverage criteria within days of the publication to permit case-by-case review, while Aetna and UnitedHealthcare have indicated they are reviewing the data. CMS has not yet issued guidance, but the procedure already has established CPT codes for use in complex regional pain syndrome and PTSD.

Dr. Cheville's team has launched a follow-up study evaluating whether a third injection at 12 weeks extends durability of response, and whether biomarkers can predict which patients are most likely to benefit. Preliminary subgroup analyses suggest patients with predominant autonomic and olfactory phenotypes respond best, while those with predominant cognitive symptoms see smaller gains.

Why this matters: A widely available outpatient procedure now has rigorous evidence supporting its use in Long COVID, potentially shifting it from contested off-label practice to insurance-covered standard of care for a specific patient subset.

title: OSHA Proposes Permanent Standard Requiring Healthcare Employers to Track and Report Long COVID Cases Among Workers
excerpt: The Occupational Safety and Health Administration published a proposed rule requiring hospitals, nursing homes, and outpatient clinics to maintain registries of healthcare workers with Long COVID and report aggregate data annually. The rule would create the first systematic federal surveillance of occupational Long COVID.
body:
The Occupational Safety and Health Administration published a proposed rule on May 28 that would require all healthcare employers covered under the General Duty Clause to maintain confidential registries of workers diagnosed with Long COVID and submit anonymized aggregate data to OSHA annually. The proposal, issued under the Biden-era Healthcare Emergency Preparedness Authority that survived the change in administration, would create the first systematic federal surveillance of occupational post-acute COVID sequelae.

The rule applies to acute care hospitals, long-term care facilities, dialysis centers, home health agencies, and outpatient clinics with 25 or more employees. Covered employers would be required to provide affected workers with information about workers' compensation eligibility, the recently finalized Department of Labor presumption rule for frontline federal workers, and available accommodations under the ADA. Importantly, the rule prohibits retaliation against workers who report a Long COVID diagnosis and requires employers to engage in the interactive accommodation process within 14 days.

Surveillance Components

Employers would report the number of workers diagnosed, broad symptom categories, job classifications, vaccination status at time of acute infection, and outcomes including return to work, reduced schedule, accommodation, leave, or separation. OSHA estimates that approximately 1.2 million healthcare workers nationally meet criteria for Long COVID, with attrition contributing to ongoing staffing crises in nursing and respiratory therapy.

The proposal builds on data from the National Nurses United survey showing that 28% of bedside nurses report symptoms consistent with Long COVID and that 11% have reduced their clinical hours as a result. A separate analysis from the Health Resources and Services Administration estimated that occupational Long COVID has cost the U.S. healthcare workforce the equivalent of 78,000 full-time positions.

Industry Response

The American Hospital Association expressed concern about administrative burden and the potential for data to be used in litigation against employers, while the Service Employees International Union and National Nurses United endorsed the proposal as overdue. The AHA estimated compliance costs at $340 million annually, while OSHA's regulatory impact analysis projected costs of $190 million offset by reduced turnover and improved retention.

The comment period closes August 27, with a final rule expected before the end of 2026. OSHA has signaled that future rulemaking may extend similar surveillance requirements to meatpacking, transportation, education, and corrections workforces, where Long COVID prevalence is also believed to be elevated but remains poorly characterized.

Why this matters: Systematic occupational surveillance would for the first time quantify the toll Long COVID has taken on the healthcare workforce and create regulatory infrastructure for protecting affected workers from termination and discrimination.

title: Science Translational Medicine Study Finds Vagus Nerve Inflammation on PET Imaging in 64% of Long COVID Patients With Gastrointestinal Symptoms
excerpt: Researchers using a novel PET tracer targeting activated macrophages identified vagus nerve inflammation in nearly two-thirds of Long COVID patients with persistent GI symptoms. The findings provide an imaging biomarker that could redirect treatment toward neuroinflammatory pathways.
body:
A multicenter study published in Science Translational Medicine identified active inflammation along the cervical and abdominal vagus nerve in 64% of Long COVID patients with persistent gastrointestinal symptoms, using a novel PET tracer that binds to translocator protein expressed by activated macrophages. The finding offers the first in vivo imaging evidence linking vagal neuroinflammation to the gastroparesis, early satiety, and constipation that affect an estimated 40% of patients with Long COVID.

The study, led by investigators at Massachusetts General Hospital and the Karolinska Institute, enrolled 142 Long COVID patients with documented GI symptoms and 58 matched controls who had recovered fully from acute COVID. All participants underwent PET-MRI using the second-generation TSPO ligand [18F]GE-180, which has improved signal-to-noise compared with earlier tracers. Vagal uptake correlated strongly with gastric emptying time on scintigraphy, symptom severity on the Patient Assessment of Gastrointestinal Symptoms Index, and circulating levels of fecal calprotectin.

Connection to Persistent Antigen

In a subset of 38 patients who underwent gastric and duodenal biopsy, persistent SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein was detected in the enteric nervous system of 71% of those with high vagal PET signal, compared with 22% of those with low signal. The investigators propose that persistent viral antigen in the gut wall drives chronic local macrophage activation that propagates retrograde along vagal afferents, producing both peripheral GI dysmotility and central symptoms such as fatigue and brain fog mediated through vagal-brainstem circuits.

The findings dovetail with prior Cell Reports Medicine work identifying T-cell exhaustion signatures in patients with persistent gut viral RNA, and with the growing literature on vagus nerve stimulation as a Long COVID treatment. Senior author Dr. Michael VanElzakker noted that the imaging signature may identify patients most likely to benefit from anti-inflammatory and antiviral interventions targeting the gut-brain axis.

Clinical Translation

The TSPO PET protocol is currently available only at research centers, and the tracer has not been approved by the FDA for routine clinical use. However, the investigators are working with industry partners to develop a streamlined protocol and have initiated a Phase 2 trial evaluating whether a 15-day course of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir combined with low-dose naltrexone reduces vagal PET signal and improves symptoms in patients with high baseline uptake.

An accompanying editorial called for incorporation of TSPO PET into ongoing RECOVER trials as both an enrichment biomarker and a surrogate endpoint, arguing that objective imaging measures are needed to overcome the heterogeneity that has plagued prior trials.

Why this matters: A reproducible imaging biomarker of neuroinflammation could finally allow Long COVID trials to enrich for patients with a specific biological mechanism, accelerating the path to targeted therapies for the substantial subset with gut-vagal involvement.

title: First Circuit Rules Universities Must Provide Academic Accommodations for Long COVID Students Under Section 504 and ADA Title III
excerpt: The First Circuit Court of Appeals ruled on May 14, 2026 that colleges and universities receiving federal funding must provide individualized academic accommodations to students with documented Long COVID, including extended deadlines, reduced course loads without loss of full-time status, and remote attendance options.
body:
The First Circuit Court of Appeals ruled on May 14, 2026 that colleges and universities receiving federal funding must provide individualized academic accommodations to students with documented Long COVID, including extended deadlines, reduced course loads without loss of full-time status, and remote attendance options. The decision in Hernandez v. Trustees of Boston College reverses a district court ruling that had granted summary judgment to the university after it denied a junior's request to attend lectures via livestream during a six-month post-exertional malaise flare.

Writing for a unanimous three-judge panel, Judge Lara Montecalvo held that Long COVID, when supported by clinical documentation of cognitive dysfunction, autonomic symptoms, or post-exertional malaise, constitutes a disability under both Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act and Title III of the Americans with Disabilities Act. The court rejected Boston College's argument that in-person attendance was an 'essential requirement' of its undergraduate program, finding that the university had successfully delivered remote instruction during the pandemic and continued to offer hybrid options to faculty.

Implications for Higher Education

The ruling specifically addresses several accommodations universities have routinely denied: pro-rated tuition refunds when students must withdraw mid-semester due to symptom exacerbation, preservation of scholarship eligibility despite reduced course loads, and access to recorded lectures as a standing accommodation rather than on a class-by-class basis. The panel cited the Department of Education's January 2026 guidance reminding institutions that Long COVID falls within the broadened ADA definition affirmed in the Fourth Circuit's earlier ruling.

Disability services administrators across the First Circuit—covering Maine, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, Rhode Island, and Puerto Rico—must now revise accommodation policies to incorporate Long COVID-specific provisions. The court emphasized that universities cannot demand fluctuating symptoms be 'stabilized' before granting accommodations, recognizing the relapsing-remitting nature of the condition.

Plaintiff's Path Forward

The named plaintiff, Sofia Hernandez, contracted COVID-19 in fall 2023 and developed POTS, cognitive impairment, and severe post-exertional malaise. After Boston College denied her accommodation requests in spring 2024, she was forced to take medical leave and lost her merit scholarship. The First Circuit's ruling remands her case for trial on damages and reinstatement.

The Council for Higher Education Accreditation issued a statement endorsing the ruling and announced that accreditation reviews beginning in 2027 will include assessment of institutional Long COVID accommodation practices. Several Ivy League institutions, including Harvard and Brown, announced policy updates within days of the decision.

Why this matters: With an estimated 280,000 U.S. college students living with Long COVID, the ruling establishes binding legal protections that could prevent thousands of medical withdrawals and preserve educational trajectories for a generation of young adults disabled by the pandemic.

title: Phase 2 Trial of Sirolimus Shows 33% Reduction in Long COVID Fatigue and Improved Mitochondrial Function Over 20 Weeks
excerpt: A Phase 2 randomized controlled trial published in The Lancet found that low-dose sirolimus reduced fatigue severity by 33% and improved markers of mitochondrial respiration in Long COVID patients compared to placebo over 20 weeks.
body:
A Phase 2 randomized controlled trial published in The Lancet on May 28, 2026 found that low-dose sirolimus reduced fatigue severity by 33% and improved markers of mitochondrial respiration in Long COVID patients compared to placebo over 20 weeks. The multicenter trial, led by investigators at the University of Alabama at Birmingham and Newcastle University, enrolled 312 patients with moderate-to-severe Long COVID and randomized them 1:1 to receive 2 mg sirolimus weekly or matched placebo.

Targeting mTOR Dysregulation

Sirolimus, an mTOR inhibitor originally developed as an immunosuppressant for organ transplant recipients, was selected based on preclinical evidence that chronic mTOR hyperactivation drives cellular senescence, impaired autophagy, and mitochondrial dysfunction—pathways implicated in Long COVID pathophysiology. The trial protocol was informed by earlier RECOVER consortium data identifying senescent cell accumulation in Long COVID patients.

Primary endpoints included the Chalder Fatigue Scale and the 6-minute walk test. Secondary endpoints encompassed cognitive performance on the NIH Toolbox, post-exertional malaise frequency measured by digital symptom diary, and laboratory markers including peripheral blood mononuclear cell oxygen consumption rate. The sirolimus group demonstrated a mean 33% reduction in fatigue scores compared to 11% in the placebo arm, a statistically significant difference (p<0.001). Walk distance improved by an average of 64 meters in treated patients versus 18 meters in controls.

Biomarker Findings

Beyond symptomatic improvement, mitochondrial respiration assays showed a 41% increase in maximal oxygen consumption rate in treated patients' immune cells. Plasma levels of senescence-associated cytokines IL-6 and CXCL9 declined significantly, while markers of autophagy including LC3-II expression increased. Researchers suggested these molecular changes may explain the durability of benefit observed in a 4-week post-treatment follow-up, during which most patients maintained their improvements.

Adverse events were generally mild. The most common were transient mouth ulcers (14%), mild hyperlipidemia (9%), and headache (8%). Three patients discontinued due to elevated triglycerides. No serious infections occurred at the low weekly dose used, which is roughly one-tenth the dose used in transplant medicine.

Path to Phase 3

Principal investigator Dr. Daniel Tyrrell of UAB noted that the trial's intermittent low-dose protocol was designed to harness sirolimus's senolytic and autophagy-inducing properties while minimizing immunosuppression. A Phase 3 confirmatory trial is planned to begin enrollment in October 2026 across 40 sites, with FDA having granted Fast Track designation. The drug's generic availability and low cost—approximately $40 per month at the trial dose—could enable rapid scaling if results are confirmed.

Why this matters: Sirolimus represents the first inexpensive, widely available drug to demonstrate both symptomatic and mechanistic benefit in Long COVID, potentially offering an accessible treatment option years before novel therapeutics reach approval.

title: CMS Announces Medicare Coverage Determination for Tilt Table Testing and Autonomic Function Studies in Long COVID Patients
excerpt: The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services issued a National Coverage Determination on May 20, 2026 establishing Medicare reimbursement for tilt table testing, quantitative sudomotor axon reflex testing, and comprehensive autonomic function studies when ordered for Long COVID patients with suspected dysautonomia.
body:
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services issued a National Coverage Determination on May 20, 2026 establishing Medicare reimbursement for tilt table testing, quantitative sudomotor axon reflex testing (QSART), and comprehensive autonomic function studies when ordered for Long COVID patients with suspected dysautonomia. The NCD takes effect July 1, 2026 and resolves years of inconsistent coverage that left many patients paying out of pocket for diagnostic workup.

Scope of Coverage

Under the determination, Medicare will cover tilt table testing for beneficiaries with documented COVID-19 infection and symptoms consistent with orthostatic intolerance, including lightheadedness on standing, post-exertional symptoms, or unexplained tachycardia. QSART testing will be covered for evaluation of small fiber neuropathy, which RECOVER and independent studies have linked to a substantial subset of Long COVID cases. Comprehensive autonomic reflex screens including Valsalva maneuver and deep breathing heart rate variability assessment are also included.

The NCD also extends coverage to skin punch biopsy with intraepidermal nerve fiber density quantification when small fiber neuropathy is suspected. Previously, Medicare administrative contractors had applied widely varying coverage rules, with some regions denying these tests outright for Long COVID indications absent a separate qualifying diagnosis.

Implementation Requirements

CMS specified that testing must be ordered by a physician affiliated with a recognized Long COVID clinical program or a board-certified specialist in cardiology, neurology, or autonomic medicine. Documentation requirements include symptom duration of at least 12 weeks, prior COVID-19 infection confirmed by laboratory testing or clinical diagnosis, and exclusion of alternative causes through standard workup. The agency declined to impose a requirement for prior tilt-test-negative results before approving more advanced autonomic testing, a change from earlier draft guidance that disability advocates had criticized.

Reimbursement rates were set at the high end of CMS's existing fee schedules for these procedures, reflecting the technical complexity and time required for proper testing. A full autonomic reflex screen will reimburse at approximately $850, while tilt table testing is set at $475.

Private Payer Implications

Although the NCD binds only Medicare, commercial insurers historically follow CMS coverage policy, and major payers including UnitedHealthcare, Anthem, and Aetna have announced plans to align their medical policies with the new determination by the fourth quarter of 2026. Several state Medicaid programs in California, New York, and Washington have also signaled intent to adopt parallel coverage rules.

The American Autonomic Society praised the decision, noting that diagnostic workup is the gateway to evidence-based treatments including ivabradine, pyridostigmine, and IVIG that have demonstrated efficacy in recent trials.

Why this matters: Standardized Medicare coverage for autonomic testing removes a major financial and bureaucratic barrier that has prevented millions of Long COVID patients from obtaining the objective diagnoses needed to access targeted treatments and disability benefits.

title: Eleventh Circuit Rules Airlines Must Provide Reasonable Accommodations Under Air Carrier Access Act for Long COVID Passengers With POTS and Oxygen Needs
excerpt: A unanimous Eleventh Circuit panel held that Long COVID patients with documented dysautonomia and hypoxemia are 'qualified individuals with a disability' under the Air Carrier Access Act, requiring airlines to provide pre-boarding, supplemental oxygen accommodation, and seating modifications.
body:
A unanimous Eleventh Circuit panel ruled on May 19 that Long COVID patients with documented postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) and exertional hypoxemia qualify for protection under the Air Carrier Access Act (ACAA), requiring commercial airlines to provide reasonable accommodations including pre-boarding, supplemental oxygen, and modified seating arrangements.

The case, Reyes v. Delta Air Lines, was brought by a former flight attendant who developed Long COVID after a 2022 infection and was repeatedly denied accommodations on personal travel after her medical retirement. Delta had argued that Long COVID was not a recognized disability under Department of Transportation regulations and that accommodating passengers with portable oxygen concentrators imposed undue operational burdens. The panel rejected both arguments.

Writing for the court, Judge Britt Grant held that the ACAA's definition of disability tracks the Americans with Disabilities Act Amendments Act, which expressly covers conditions that substantially limit major bodily functions including cardiovascular, neurological, and respiratory systems. The court cited the Fourth Circuit's earlier ruling that Long COVID qualifies as an ADA disability without individualized severity showing, finding the same logic applies in the aviation context.

Specific Accommodations Required

The opinion lays out a non-exhaustive list of accommodations airlines must consider: pre-boarding for patients who cannot stand in jetway lines without syncope, bulkhead or aisle seating for patients requiring leg elevation, permission to use FAA-approved portable oxygen concentrators without 48-hour advance notice requirements, and access to wheelchair assistance regardless of whether the passenger uses a wheelchair in daily life.

The court was particularly critical of Delta's policy requiring physician documentation submitted 72 hours in advance, finding it inconsistent with the episodic and unpredictable nature of Long COVID symptoms. Judge Grant noted that 'a passenger whose POTS flares without warning cannot reasonably be expected to predict accommodation needs three days in advance of travel.'

Industry-Wide Implications

The ruling binds carriers operating in Alabama, Florida, and Georgia but is expected to influence DOT rulemaking. The Department of Transportation has had a proposed rule on chronic illness accommodations pending since late 2024, and disability advocates immediately called on Secretary Buttigieg's successor to finalize the rule incorporating the Eleventh Circuit's framework.

Airlines for America, the industry trade group, criticized the decision as 'operationally unworkable' and signaled possible en banc review. However, several carriers including United and JetBlue had already updated accommodation policies for Long COVID passengers earlier this year following EEOC enforcement guidance on cognitive dysfunction.

The decision also requires Delta to pay $340,000 in compensatory damages and attorney's fees to Reyes.

Why this matters: With roughly 17 million American adults living with Long COVID, the ruling removes a significant barrier to air travel for patients whose symptoms have effectively grounded them since infection.

title: Phase 3 RESTORE-LC Trial Shows Oxaloacetate Reduces Mental and Physical Fatigue by 37% in Long COVID Patients Over 12 Weeks
excerpt: Results from the 412-patient RESTORE-LC trial published in The Lancet show that oral anhydrous enol-oxaloacetate produced significant, durable reductions in fatigue scores compared to placebo, with benefits sustained four weeks after treatment discontinuation.
body:
Results from the Phase 3 RESTORE-LC trial, published May 28 in The Lancet, show that 12 weeks of oral anhydrous enol-oxaloacetate reduced mental and physical fatigue scores by 37% in Long COVID patients compared to 11% with placebo, with benefits sustained through a four-week post-treatment follow-up.

The multicenter, randomized, double-blind trial enrolled 412 patients across 23 sites in the United States, United Kingdom, and Australia. Participants had documented Long COVID for at least six months with moderate-to-severe fatigue measured by the Chalder Fatigue Scale. Patients received either 2,000 mg daily oxaloacetate or matched placebo, with stratification by sex, baseline severity, and presence of post-exertional malaise.

Mechanism and Trial Design

Oxaloacetate is a Krebs cycle intermediate that researchers hypothesize addresses the mitochondrial dysfunction increasingly recognized as central to Long COVID pathophysiology. The trial built on earlier observational data from the Bateman Horne Center showing fatigue reductions in ME/CFS patients, a population with substantial clinical overlap with Long COVID.

The primary endpoint was change in Chalder Fatigue Scale total score at week 12. Treated patients showed a mean reduction of 9.4 points compared to 2.8 points for placebo (p<0.001). Secondary endpoints including the DePaul Symptom Questionnaire post-exertional malaise subscale, the SF-36 physical function domain, and accelerometer-measured daily step counts all favored oxaloacetate with statistical significance.

Notably, the subgroup with the most severe baseline post-exertional malaise showed the largest response, with a 44% reduction in PEM frequency and severity. Researchers measured serum lactate-to-pyruvate ratios as a biomarker of mitochondrial function and found significant improvement in the treatment arm.

Safety and Next Steps

Adverse events were generally mild, with gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, loose stools) most common at 14% versus 9% in placebo. No serious adverse events were attributed to the study drug. Three patients discontinued for GI intolerance.

The study was funded by a consortium including the Open Medicine Foundation and a $14 million NIH RECOVER-TREAT supplemental grant. Lead investigator David Systrom of Brigham and Women's Hospital noted that oxaloacetate is currently available as a dietary supplement, which raises both access opportunities and quality-control concerns.

The trial sponsor has filed for FDA review under the agency's new Long COVID regulatory pathway established in 2025, seeking approval as a prescription medication that would enable insurance coverage and pharmaceutical-grade manufacturing standards. An FDA advisory committee meeting is expected in the fourth quarter of 2026.

Systrom cautioned that oxaloacetate is 'not a cure' but represents 'a meaningful tool addressing the bioenergetic deficit at the core of this illness.'

Why this matters: RESTORE-LC provides the strongest Phase 3 evidence to date that targeting mitochondrial metabolism produces clinically meaningful improvement, potentially establishing the second FDA-approved Long COVID therapy after AXA1125.
