title: Social Security Administration Issues Final Rule Adding Long COVID to Compassionate Allowances Program for Severe Cases With Documented Organ Dysfunction
excerpt: The SSA announced on May 22 that severe Long COVID with documented cardiopulmonary, neurological, or autonomic organ dysfunction will be added to the Compassionate Allowances list, enabling fast-track disability determinations within an average of 30 days.
body:
The Social Security Administration announced on May 22 a final rule adding severe Long COVID with documented organ dysfunction to its Compassionate Allowances (CAL) program, enabling expedited disability determinations that historically have averaged 30 days compared to the standard 7-to-24-month wait.

The rule, published in the Federal Register on May 27 and effective August 1, makes Long COVID the first post-infectious chronic illness added to the CAL list, which currently covers 287 conditions ranging from pancreatic cancer to early-onset Alzheimer's disease. The decision follows two years of advocacy by patient organizations and reflects mounting evidence that severely affected Long COVID patients face the same prognosis-altering delays that prompted creation of the program in 2008.

Qualifying Criteria

To qualify under the new pathway, applicants must demonstrate Long COVID diagnosis per the new ICD-10 subtype codes finalized by CMS plus objective evidence of at least one of the following: reduced peak VO2 below 14 mL/kg/min on cardiopulmonary exercise testing; severe orthostatic intolerance with documented heart rate increase greater than 40 beats per minute on tilt-table testing; cerebral hypoperfusion on SPECT or arterial spin labeling MRI; small fiber neuropathy confirmed by skin biopsy; or documented persistent viral antigenemia.

The criteria deliberately mirror the objective testing modalities the Second Circuit and Sixth Circuit have recognized in recent rulings barring categorical denial of Long COVID disability claims based on normal routine testing.

Backlog and Implementation

The SSA estimates approximately 180,000 pending Long COVID applications could qualify for expedited review, with another 80,000 to 120,000 expected annually going forward. To handle the volume, the agency is hiring 340 additional disability examiners and contracting with a consortium of academic medical centers to provide consultative examinations using standardized Long COVID assessment protocols.

Acting Commissioner Martin O'Malley framed the decision as overdue recognition of the illness's severity, stating that 'patients should not lose their homes waiting for the determinations they are legally entitled to.' Internal SSA data released alongside the rule showed that Long COVID claimants currently wait an average of 19 months for initial determination and that 31% of severely affected applicants experienced housing instability during the wait.

The rule has bipartisan support, with Senators Tim Kaine (D-Va.) and Bill Cassidy (R-La.) issuing a joint statement praising the decision. However, some disability advocates expressed concern that the objective testing requirements may exclude patients without access to specialty centers capable of performing CPET, tilt-table testing, or skin biopsies. The SSA's accompanying guidance instructs adjudicators to accept testing performed at any qualifying facility and to consider alternative documentation in medically underserved areas.

Why this matters: Compassionate Allowances designation transforms the disability process for the most severely disabled Long COVID patients, who have faced years of delays while losing income, housing, and access to healthcare.

title: UCSF Trial Finds Sipavibart Monoclonal Antibody Reduces Symptom Burden by 41% in Long COVID Patients With Persistent SARS-CoV-2 Antigenemia
excerpt: A Phase 2 trial at UCSF shows that the long-acting monoclonal antibody sipavibart produced a 41% reduction in symptom burden among long COVID patients with detectable circulating spike antigen, supporting the viral persistence hypothesis.
body:
A randomized Phase 2 trial conducted at UCSF and published this week in JAMA found that sipavibart, AstraZeneca's long-acting monoclonal antibody originally developed for immunocompromised COVID-19 prophylaxis, produced a 41% reduction in composite symptom burden among long COVID patients with detectable circulating SARS-CoV-2 spike antigen.

The 180-patient trial enrolled adults with at least 12 months of persistent post-COVID symptoms who screened positive for circulating spike or nucleocapsid antigen using the Quanterix Simoa ultrasensitive assay. Participants received two intravenous doses of sipavibart 28 days apart or matched placebo, with the primary endpoint measured at week 16 using the PROMIS-29 and a fatigue-specific instrument.

Beyond the headline 41% reduction in symptom burden, treated patients showed a 36% improvement in cognitive function on the NIH Toolbox and a 44% reduction in post-exertional malaise frequency. Antigen levels became undetectable in 68% of treated patients versus 9% of controls, and the magnitude of antigen clearance correlated tightly with clinical improvement.

Lead investigator Dr. Michael Peluso emphasized that the trial provides the first interventional evidence that targeting persistent viral antigen yields measurable clinical benefit. "We've had observational data linking antigen persistence to symptoms for years," Peluso said. "This is the first time we've shown that eliminating it changes how patients feel and function."

Selecting Patients by Biomarker

A notable feature of the trial design was its biomarker-enriched enrollment. Only patients with detectable antigenemia were eligible — roughly 42% of long COVID patients screened. Among patients without detectable antigen, prior pilot work showed no benefit, reinforcing that antiviral approaches are unlikely to help all long COVID phenotypes.

The findings dovetail with earlier work from the same group identifying spike protein in T cells and tissue reservoirs, and with the recent Nature paper documenting spike persistence in bone marrow. Critics had questioned whether such reservoirs were clinically meaningful; this trial provides the strongest evidence to date that they are.

Regulatory Path Forward

AstraZeneca confirmed it will pursue a Phase 3 program beginning in late 2026, with the FDA having signaled openness to a biomarker-defined indication. The drug retains activity against currently circulating Omicron sublineages, though the company acknowledged that variant evolution remains a significant commercial and clinical risk.

Adverse events were modest, with infusion reactions in 7% of treated patients and no serious drug-related events. The trial did not assess durability beyond 16 weeks, and whether repeat dosing will be necessary remains unresolved.

Why this matters: This is the first randomized trial to show that directly targeting persistent SARS-CoV-2 antigen produces meaningful clinical improvement, validating viral persistence as a treatable mechanism in a defined long COVID subgroup.

title: D.C. Circuit Strikes Down VA Policy Limiting Long COVID Service Connection to Veterans With Documented Acute Hospitalization
excerpt: The D.C. Circuit ruled that the Department of Veterans Affairs cannot deny service connection for long COVID solely because a veteran was not hospitalized during the acute phase, opening benefits eligibility to thousands of veterans with mild initial infections.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit ruled this week that the Department of Veterans Affairs violated the Veterans' Benefits Act by categorically denying service connection for long COVID claims from veterans who lacked documented hospitalization during their acute SARS-CoV-2 infection. The decision invalidates a 2024 VA adjudication manual provision that had functioned as a de facto bar to thousands of claims.

The case, Reyes v. McDonough, was brought by an Army veteran who developed disabling post-exertional malaise, dysautonomia, and cognitive impairment after a mild COVID-19 infection contracted during a 2022 deployment. The VA had denied her claim on the grounds that without hospitalization records or a positive PCR test from the acute period, she could not establish the requisite "in-service event."

Writing for a unanimous panel, Judge Patricia Millett held that the VA's policy "impermissibly substitutes a bureaucratic proxy for the statutory standard of service connection" and "ignores the well-documented epidemiological reality that the majority of long COVID cases follow mild or even asymptomatic acute infections." The court cited CDC and RECOVER data showing that fewer than 8% of long COVID cases involve acute hospitalization.

Lay Evidence and the Buddy Statement

The opinion explicitly endorses the use of lay evidence — including so-called buddy statements from fellow service members — to establish in-service COVID-19 infection in the absence of contemporaneous testing. The court noted that early-pandemic testing shortages, particularly in deployed environments, made documentation impossible for many veterans.

The ruling instructs the VA to develop a new adjudication framework within 180 days that allows claimants to establish in-service infection through a totality-of-the-evidence standard. It also requires the VA to readjudicate claims denied under the invalidated policy since January 2023, a cohort estimated by veterans' advocates to exceed 14,000 cases.

Broader Implications for Disability Adjudication

Veterans' law attorneys noted that the decision may influence Social Security Administration practice as well. Although Reyes binds only the VA, its reasoning — that documentation requirements cannot be calibrated to the typical case at the expense of atypical but real ones — tracks the Second Circuit's 2025 ruling rejecting SSA's reliance on normal objective testing to deny long COVID claims.

The VA declined to say whether it will seek Supreme Court review, but acting general counsel issued a memorandum directing regional offices to suspend denials based on the invalidated provision pending revised guidance.

Why this matters: The ruling removes the single largest barrier to VA benefits for long COVID-affected veterans and signals that adjudication systems built around the assumption of documented severe acute infection are legally and scientifically untenable.

title: Charité Berlin Trial Shows Immunoadsorption Therapy Produces Sustained Symptom Reduction in Long COVID Patients With Beta-Adrenergic Autoantibodies
excerpt: A controlled trial at Charité Berlin found that immunoadsorption — a blood-filtration technique that removes circulating autoantibodies — produced sustained six-month symptom improvement in 58% of long COVID patients with elevated beta-1 and beta-2 adrenergic receptor autoantibodies.
body:
A controlled trial conducted at Charité–Universitätsmedizin Berlin and published in The Lancet found that immunoadsorption therapy produced sustained six-month symptom improvement in 58% of long COVID patients with elevated autoantibodies against beta-1 and beta-2 adrenergic receptors and muscarinic M3/M4 receptors. The trial provides the strongest evidence to date that a subset of long COVID is driven by treatable autoimmune mechanisms.

The 120-patient study enrolled adults with at least 14 months of moderate-to-severe long COVID who screened positive for at least two of four target autoantibodies on the CellTrend assay. Participants were randomized to five sessions of tryptophan-column immunoadsorption over two weeks or sham apheresis. The primary endpoint was a composite of fatigue (Chalder Fatigue Scale), functional capacity (Bell score), and post-exertional malaise frequency at week 24.

At six months, 58% of immunoadsorption recipients met the primary endpoint compared with 14% of sham-treated patients. Mean Bell functional capacity scores rose from 30 to 55 in responders, a clinically meaningful shift from housebound to part-time functional status. Hand grip strength, a validated objective marker in ME/CFS and long COVID research, improved by 34% in treated patients.

Durability and Retreatment

Investigators reported that responders showed gradual autoantibody re-accumulation between months four and six, with approximately 30% requiring a second treatment cycle to maintain benefit. Lead investigator Dr. Carmen Scheibenbogen, who has championed the autoimmune hypothesis of long COVID and ME/CFS for over a decade, said the durability profile resembles that seen with immunoadsorption in other autoantibody-mediated conditions such as dilated cardiomyopathy.

Importantly, the trial excluded patients without elevated GPCR autoantibodies, and a parallel observational arm of 40 antibody-negative patients showed no benefit from treatment. This mirrors the biomarker-enriched design philosophy now emerging across long COVID therapeutics research.

Access and Cost Challenges

Immunoadsorption is approved and reimbursed for several indications in Germany but is rarely performed in the United States, where therapeutic plasma exchange is the more common analog. Course costs in Europe run between €15,000 and €25,000, raising significant equity questions if the therapy moves toward broader adoption.

The findings complement recent positive data on BC 007, an aptamer that neutralizes GPCR autoantibodies without requiring apheresis, suggesting a convergent treatment paradigm for the autoimmune long COVID phenotype. A head-to-head comparison trial is reportedly under discussion between the Charité group and Berlin Cures.

Adverse events were limited to transient hypotension and citrate-related paresthesias, with no serious events attributed to the procedure.

Why this matters: The trial confirms that a meaningful subset of long COVID is mediated by removable pathogenic autoantibodies and validates apheresis as a high-impact, biomarker-targeted treatment option for the most severely affected patients.

title: Imperial College Trial Shows JAK Inhibitor Baricitinib Reduces Long COVID Inflammatory Markers and Fatigue by 34% Over 16 Weeks
excerpt: A Phase 2 trial conducted at Imperial College London found that baricitinib, an oral JAK1/2 inhibitor, significantly reduced fatigue scores and circulating interferon-gamma levels in Long COVID patients with elevated inflammatory profiles.
body:
A Phase 2 randomized controlled trial conducted at Imperial College London and published in The Lancet Rheumatology has found that baricitinib, an oral JAK1/2 inhibitor already approved for rheumatoid arthritis and severe COVID-19, reduced fatigue scores by 34% and lowered key inflammatory markers in Long COVID patients with persistent immune dysregulation over a 16-week treatment period.

The trial enrolled 184 adults with Long COVID lasting at least 12 months who had documented elevations in interferon-gamma, CXCL10, or IL-6 at baseline. Participants were randomized to receive either 4 mg of baricitinib daily or matched placebo. The primary endpoint was change in the Chalder Fatigue Scale at week 16, with secondary endpoints including post-exertional malaise frequency, cognitive function on the Cogstate battery, and serum cytokine profiles.

Inflammatory Subtype Showed Strongest Response

Patients receiving baricitinib showed a mean 34% reduction in fatigue scores compared to 9% in the placebo arm. Interferon-gamma levels dropped by 51% in the treatment group, and CXCL10 fell by 43%. Notably, the strongest responses were observed in patients with the highest baseline inflammatory markers, suggesting a clear biomarker-defined responder population. Cognitive testing showed modest but statistically significant improvements in processing speed and working memory.

Lead investigator Dr. Helen Marriott emphasized that the trial represents one of the first successful demonstrations that targeting interferon signaling can produce clinical benefit in Long COVID. "We are not curing the condition, but we are clearly modulating an inflammatory process that appears to drive symptoms in a defined subset of patients," she said. Approximately 38% of screened patients met the inflammatory criteria for enrollment, suggesting the target population is substantial but not universal.

Safety Profile and Next Steps

Adverse events were consistent with baricitinib's known safety profile in other indications. Serious infections occurred in 2.1% of treated patients versus 1.1% on placebo, and no thromboembolic events were reported during the 16-week period. Researchers noted that the relatively short trial duration limits conclusions about long-term safety, particularly given baricitinib's boxed warnings for malignancy and thrombosis in older populations.

A Phase 3 trial is planned to begin enrollment in early 2027, with sites across the UK, Germany, and the United States. The expanded study will stratify patients by inflammatory biomarker status and include a 52-week follow-up period to assess durability of response and longer-term safety.

Why this matters: The baricitinib results provide the first robust evidence that an existing, widely available immunomodulator can meaningfully reduce Long COVID symptoms in a biomarker-defined population, potentially offering a near-term treatment option through off-label prescribing while regulatory pathways advance.

title: Eighth Circuit Rules Medicaid Managed Care Plans Must Cover Multidisciplinary Long COVID Rehabilitation Programs
excerpt: The Eighth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled that state Medicaid managed care organizations cannot categorically exclude coverage for multidisciplinary Long COVID rehabilitation, holding such exclusions violate federal Medicaid parity requirements.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit ruled last week that Medicaid managed care organizations operating in member states cannot categorically deny coverage for multidisciplinary Long COVID rehabilitation programs, holding that blanket exclusions violate federal Medicaid parity and medical necessity requirements established under the Social Security Act and CMS managed care regulations.

The decision, issued in Rivera v. Centene of Missouri, consolidated appeals from three Long COVID patients in Missouri, Arkansas, and Iowa whose requests for coordinated care involving cardiology, neurology, pulmonary rehabilitation, and cognitive therapy were denied as "not medically necessary" or "experimental." The plaintiffs argued the denials reflected a policy of excluding integrated Long COVID care regardless of individual clinical need.

Court Rejects "Experimental" Designation

Writing for a unanimous three-judge panel, Judge Jane Kelly held that Long COVID rehabilitation cannot be classified as experimental given the condition's recognition by CMS through dedicated ICD-10 subtype codes, HHS funding of specialized clinical networks, and endorsement of multidisciplinary care in WHO clinical practice guidelines. The opinion noted that the component therapies—cardiac rehabilitation, cognitive remediation, autonomic testing, and graded pacing protocols—are individually covered for other conditions, making their bundled denial for Long COVID arbitrary.

The court further held that managed care organizations bear an affirmative obligation under 42 C.F.R. § 438.210 to apply medical necessity determinations on an individualized basis, and that internal coverage policies excluding entire categories of evidence-based care for a recognized condition violate both federal regulation and state contracts with CMS.

Implications Across States

The ruling applies directly to Medicaid managed care plans in the Eighth Circuit's seven states—Arkansas, Iowa, Minnesota, Missouri, Nebraska, North Dakota, and South Dakota—covering approximately 4.2 million Medicaid beneficiaries enrolled in managed care arrangements. Plaintiffs' attorneys estimate that tens of thousands of Long COVID patients in these states have been denied coordinated rehabilitation services since 2022.

Centene has not indicated whether it will seek Supreme Court review. The National Health Law Program, which filed an amicus brief supporting the plaintiffs, described the ruling as the most significant Medicaid access decision for Long COVID patients to date. CMS issued a statement noting that the agency is reviewing the decision and may issue updated guidance to state Medicaid directors clarifying coverage obligations for post-acute COVID conditions.

Similar cases are pending in the Fifth and Eleventh Circuits, where plaintiffs have raised parallel claims under both Medicaid and ADA frameworks.

Why this matters: The ruling closes a major coverage gap for low-income Long COVID patients who have been disproportionately excluded from specialized care, and establishes federal precedent that managed care plans cannot use "experimental" labels to deny treatment for a condition formally recognized across the federal healthcare apparatus.

title: CDC Reports Long COVID Prevalence in U.S. Adults Stabilized at 6.8% in 2026, With Disproportionate Burden in Women and Latino Communities
excerpt: New CDC surveillance data show that Long COVID prevalence among U.S. adults has stabilized at 6.8%, but the burden remains sharply unequal across gender, ethnicity, and income lines.
body:
The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention released new National Health Interview Survey data this week showing that Long COVID prevalence among U.S. adults stabilized at 6.8% during the first quarter of 2026, ending two years of gradual decline but revealing persistent and widening disparities across demographic groups.

The data, drawn from continuous NHIS sampling of approximately 27,000 households, indicate that roughly 17.4 million American adults currently experience symptoms meeting CDC's working definition of Long COVID—symptoms lasting three months or more following SARS-CoV-2 infection that limit daily activities. The figure represents a plateau after declines from 7.5% in 2023 and 7.1% in 2024, suggesting that new incident cases from ongoing infections are now offsetting recoveries.

Disparities Deepen Across Subgroups

Women continue to bear a disproportionate burden, with 8.4% reporting current Long COVID compared to 5.1% of men. Latino adults showed the highest prevalence of any major racial or ethnic group at 8.9%, followed by American Indian and Alaska Native adults at 8.6%, non-Hispanic white adults at 6.7%, Black adults at 6.4%, and Asian adults at 4.2%. Adults with household incomes below 200% of the federal poverty line reported prevalence of 9.3%, nearly double the 5.0% rate among those earning above 400% of the poverty line.

The report also documented growing functional impact. Among adults with current Long COVID, 27.8% reported significant activity limitation—up from 24.1% in 2024—and 11.4% reported being unable to work or attend school, an increase of nearly two percentage points over two years. Researchers attributed the rising severity profile partly to selection effects, as patients with milder symptoms have been more likely to recover, leaving a more affected residual population.

Pediatric and Geographic Patterns

A companion report on pediatric Long COVID estimated current prevalence among children and adolescents at 1.9%, or approximately 1.4 million minors, with the highest rates in adolescents aged 12-17. Geographic analysis showed prevalence ranging from 4.8% in Hawaii to 9.6% in West Virginia, with elevated rates concentrated in Appalachian, Gulf Coast, and rural Mountain West regions where vaccination coverage has historically been lower and healthcare access more limited.

CDC Director Dr. Mandy Cohen, in a statement accompanying the release, called the stabilization "a sobering signal that Long COVID has become an endemic source of chronic disability rather than a diminishing legacy of the acute pandemic." She emphasized that continued surveillance, expanded clinical infrastructure, and targeted outreach to disproportionately affected communities remain essential public health priorities.

Why this matters: The plateau in Long COVID prevalence confirms that the condition has settled into a stable, large-scale public health burden with sharp inequities, requiring sustained policy and clinical investment rather than the wind-down many federal programs had begun to anticipate.

title: Cleveland Clinic Trial Shows IVIG Reduces Small Fiber Neuropathy Symptoms in Long COVID by 45% Over 24 Weeks
excerpt: A randomized trial published in the Annals of Neurology found that intravenous immunoglobulin therapy significantly reduced pain, autonomic symptoms, and improved nerve fiber density in Long COVID patients with biopsy-confirmed small fiber neuropathy.
body:
A Cleveland Clinic-led randomized controlled trial published in Annals of Neurology in May 2026 found that intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy reduced symptoms of small fiber neuropathy in Long COVID patients by 45% over a 24-week course, with measurable improvements in nerve fiber density on repeat skin biopsy.

The trial enrolled 142 Long COVID patients with skin biopsy-confirmed small fiber neuropathy and symptoms including burning pain, paresthesias, and dysautonomia persisting at least 12 months after acute SARS-CoV-2 infection. Participants were randomized to receive monthly IVIG infusions at 2 g/kg or placebo saline infusions over six months. The primary endpoint combined patient-reported pain scores on the Small Fiber Neuropathy Symptom Inventory with objective intraepidermal nerve fiber density measured at the distal calf.

Objective Nerve Regeneration Documented

At week 24, patients receiving IVIG showed a 45% reduction in composite symptom scores compared with 11% in the placebo arm. Repeat punch biopsies demonstrated a mean increase of 3.2 nerve fibers per millimeter in the treatment group, while the placebo group showed continued decline. Autonomic function testing revealed improvements in heart rate variability and sudomotor function that paralleled the histologic findings.

Subgroup analysis identified the strongest responders as patients with elevated baseline anti-trisulfated heparan sulfate antibodies, suggesting an autoimmune mechanism amenable to immunomodulation. Adverse events were consistent with the known IVIG safety profile, including headaches in 18% and one case of aseptic meningitis that resolved with conservative management.

Implications for Insurance Coverage

Small fiber neuropathy has emerged as one of the most consistently documented organic findings in Long COVID, with prior studies estimating prevalence between 30% and 60% in symptomatic patients. Until now, treatment has been limited to symptomatic management with gabapentinoids and tricyclic antidepressants, neither of which addresses underlying nerve damage.

The trial's lead investigator, Dr. Robert Wilson of the Cleveland Clinic Neuromuscular Center, said the findings provide the first disease-modifying evidence for an immunologic Long COVID phenotype and should prompt insurers to reconsider blanket denials of IVIG for post-viral neuropathy. IVIG can cost between $10,000 and $15,000 per monthly infusion, and most commercial plans currently classify its use in Long COVID as experimental.

The American Academy of Neurology indicated it will convene a working group this fall to evaluate whether the evidence supports a formal practice advisory on IVIG for post-COVID small fiber neuropathy with autoimmune features.

Why this matters: This is the first randomized trial to show both symptomatic improvement and objective nerve regeneration from a disease-modifying therapy in Long COVID, potentially shifting IVIG from experimental to standard of care for a defined patient subgroup.

title: Fifth Circuit Rules Long COVID Patients Can Pursue Class Action Against Hospital Systems for Discriminatory Denial of Specialty Care
excerpt: The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals held that Long COVID patients may proceed with a class action lawsuit alleging hospital systems systematically refused referrals and specialty consultations in violation of Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act.
body:
The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled on May 18, 2026 that Long COVID patients may proceed with a class action lawsuit alleging that three large hospital systems in Texas and Louisiana engaged in a pattern of denying specialty referrals, cardiology workups, and neurology consultations to patients with documented Long COVID diagnoses, in violation of Section 1557 of the Affordable Care Act.

The case, Reyes v. Memorial Health Partners, was brought by 14 named plaintiffs who alleged that after primary care physicians documented Long COVID diagnoses in their charts, specialty clinics within the same health systems repeatedly canceled referrals, declined new patient appointments, or required psychiatric clearance not demanded of patients with other post-viral conditions. The district court had dismissed the suit, finding plaintiffs failed to establish disability discrimination under Section 1557.

Court Recognizes Pattern Evidence

Writing for a unanimous three-judge panel, Judge Catharina Haynes held that plaintiffs adequately alleged disparate treatment based on disability under Section 1557, which incorporates Rehabilitation Act standards. The court emphasized that the Fourth Circuit's 2025 ruling establishing Long COVID as a per se ADA disability supplied the predicate disability classification, and that statistical evidence showing referral acceptance rates of 31% for Long COVID patients versus 84% for patients with other chronic conditions was sufficient to survive dismissal.

The court rejected the defendants' argument that referral decisions reflected legitimate clinical judgment, noting that internal emails produced in discovery referenced Long COVID patients as "resource-intensive" and "low-yield" and that one cardiology group had circulated guidance instructing schedulers to route Long COVID referrals to a designated nurse for screening not applied to other cardiac referrals.

Broader Implications for Health System Practices

The ruling is the first appellate decision to apply Section 1557 to access-to-care disparities in Long COVID and could expose hospital systems nationwide to similar litigation. Patient advocacy groups have documented widespread complaints about specialty access since 2023, with the Long COVID Alliance estimating that more than 40% of patients report being denied referrals or having appointments canceled after disclosing their diagnosis.

The American Hospital Association issued a statement expressing concern that the ruling could constrain physician discretion, while the HHS Office for Civil Rights, which had filed an amicus brief supporting the plaintiffs, said the decision affirms its enforcement position under recently revised Section 1557 regulations.

The case now returns to the Southern District of Texas for class certification proceedings, with plaintiffs seeking to represent an estimated 18,000 patients across the three defendant health systems.

Why this matters: This decision opens a powerful new legal avenue for Long COVID patients facing systemic denial of specialty care and signals that civil rights law applies not only to outright refusal of treatment but to discriminatory referral practices within integrated health systems.

title: Karolinska Institute Study Finds Microclots Containing Fibrinaloid Deposits in 78% of Long COVID Patients With Persistent Symptoms
excerpt: Researchers at the Karolinska Institute published findings in Blood demonstrating amyloid-resistant fibrin microclots in the majority of Long COVID patients, correlating strongly with symptom severity and providing a candidate biomarker for diagnosis.
body:
A study published in Blood on May 27, 2026 by researchers at the Karolinska Institute identified amyloid-resistant fibrin microclots, known as fibrinaloid microclots, in 78% of Long COVID patients with persistent symptoms lasting more than 18 months, compared with 4% of recovered COVID controls and 2% of never-infected controls.

The study analyzed plasma samples from 312 Long COVID patients across three European cohorts using standardized fluorescence microscopy with thioflavin T staining and automated image quantification. Researchers found that microclot burden correlated strongly with fatigue severity, post-exertional malaise frequency, and elevated D-dimer levels, and that microclots contained entrapped inflammatory molecules including alpha-2-antiplasmin, serum amyloid A, and von Willebrand factor that resisted normal fibrinolysis.

Mechanistic Insights and Diagnostic Potential

The Karolinska team, building on earlier work by Resia Pretorius at Stellenbosch University, advanced the field by validating a reproducible quantification protocol that produced consistent results across three independent laboratories. Inter-rater reliability for microclot scoring exceeded 0.91, addressing prior criticism that microclot findings were operator-dependent and not reproducible.

Patients with the highest microclot burden showed reduced capillary density on nailfold videocapillaroscopy and elevated lactate on cardiopulmonary exercise testing, suggesting that microvascular obstruction contributes to impaired tissue oxygen delivery. Genetic analysis revealed enrichment of variants in the SERPINE1 and FGB genes among patients with severe microclot phenotypes.

Implications for Treatment Trials

The findings are expected to accelerate enrollment in several ongoing therapeutic trials targeting microclot dissolution, including a Phase 2 trial of triple anticoagulation therapy in the Netherlands and an investigator-initiated trial of nattokinase combined with serrapeptase at the University of California San Francisco. Earlier compassionate use protocols using triple anticoagulation had reported symptom improvement in case series but lacked controlled evidence.

The European Medicines Agency confirmed it will consider standardized microclot quantification as a potential enrichment biomarker for Long COVID trial design, which could allow targeted enrollment of patients most likely to respond to vascular and coagulation-directed therapies. The U.S. FDA has not yet issued a comparable position.

Critics noted that microclot findings still require validation against blinded clinical outcomes and that the relationship between microclot burden and treatment response remains unproven. The Karolinska team has launched a prospective biomarker validation study following 600 patients over 24 months to address these gaps.

Why this matters: Reproducible quantification of fibrinaloid microclots could finally provide Long COVID with an objective, accessible blood-based biomarker, both validating patient experience and enabling rational stratification for emerging vascular therapies.

title: Massachusetts General Trial Finds Vagus Nerve Stimulation Reduces Long COVID Fatigue and Inflammatory Markers by 42% Over 10 Weeks
excerpt: A randomized controlled trial at Massachusetts General Hospital found that transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation reduced fatigue scores by 42% and significantly lowered IL-6 and TNF-alpha levels in Long COVID patients over a 10-week period.
body:
A randomized controlled trial conducted at Massachusetts General Hospital and published in JAMA Neurology this month found that transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation (taVNS) reduced fatigue scores by 42% and significantly lowered inflammatory markers in Long COVID patients over a 10-week treatment period. The trial enrolled 184 patients with moderate-to-severe Long COVID and randomized them to either active taVNS or sham stimulation delivered through an ear-clip device worn 30 minutes daily.

Patients in the active arm showed a mean reduction of 12.6 points on the Fatigue Severity Scale compared to 3.1 points in the sham group. Serum interleukin-6 levels dropped by an average of 38%, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha decreased by 31% in treated patients, suggesting the device's anti-inflammatory effects extend well beyond symptomatic relief. Heart rate variability, a marker of autonomic function frequently abnormal in Long COVID, improved by 24% in the active arm.

Targeting the Inflammatory Reflex

Vagus nerve stimulation activates what researchers call the cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway, in which vagal signaling suppresses cytokine production by macrophages in the spleen and other organs. Lead investigator Dr. Aaron Friedberg noted that prior imaging studies had shown structural changes in the vagus nerves of Long COVID patients, including thickening and reduced cross-sectional area on ultrasound, providing a biological rationale for the intervention.

The trial excluded patients with implanted cardiac devices and those taking strong vagolytic medications. Adverse events were minimal, limited to mild skin irritation at the electrode site in 8% of participants and transient dizziness in 3%. No serious adverse events were attributed to the device.

Subgroup Findings and Limitations

Subgroup analyses showed the strongest responses in patients with elevated baseline inflammatory markers and those reporting prominent autonomic symptoms. Patients whose primary complaint was cognitive dysfunction showed smaller but still statistically significant improvements on the Montreal Cognitive Assessment. Notably, post-exertional malaise frequency dropped by 29% in the active arm, though the trial was not powered specifically for that endpoint.

Researchers cautioned that the 10-week trial duration leaves open questions about durability. A 12-month open-label extension is now enrolling, and the team plans a multi-site Phase 3 trial in partnership with the RECOVER initiative. The device used in the trial is already FDA-cleared for treatment of migraine and cluster headache, raising the prospect of relatively rapid off-label adoption while regulatory review for a Long COVID indication proceeds.

Why this matters: A non-pharmacological, home-administered intervention with measurable effects on both symptoms and inflammatory biomarkers offers a tolerable new option for the millions of Long COVID patients who cannot access or do not respond to medication-based treatments.

title: Third Circuit Rules Long COVID Patients Can Sue Long-Term Care Insurers Under State Consumer Protection Laws for Bad Faith Claim Denials
excerpt: The Third Circuit ruled that Long COVID claimants may pursue state-law bad faith and consumer protection claims against long-term care and disability insurers, rejecting industry arguments that ERISA categorically preempts such suits when claims involve contested Long COVID diagnoses.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit ruled last week that Long COVID claimants may pursue state-law bad faith and consumer protection claims against long-term care and disability insurers, rejecting industry arguments that ERISA categorically preempts such suits when claims involve contested Long COVID diagnoses. The 2-1 decision in Reyes v. Lincoln Benefit Assurance Co. arose from the denial of a long-term care policy claim filed by a 58-year-old former nurse who became severely disabled after a 2022 infection.

The plaintiff alleged that Lincoln Benefit had adopted an internal practice of requiring objective imaging or laboratory findings inconsistent with prevailing Long COVID clinical guidelines, and had deployed reviewing physicians with no training in post-viral illness. She sought damages under New Jersey's Consumer Fraud Act and common-law bad faith doctrine.

Court Rejects Preemption Argument

Writing for the majority, Judge Cheryl Krause held that because the plaintiff's long-term care policy was an individually purchased product rather than an ERISA-governed employee benefit plan, state consumer protection remedies were fully available. The court also signaled, in extensive dicta, that even in ERISA contexts, claims grounded in deceptive marketing of policies as covering "any chronic illness" could survive preemption to the extent they target pre-claim conduct.

The decision distinguishes between disability insurance claims, which the court acknowledged remain largely governed by ERISA's exclusive remedial scheme for employer-sponsored plans, and the growing market of individually marketed long-term care and critical illness policies. Industry analysts estimate that roughly 7.5 million Americans hold individually purchased long-term care policies, a significant portion of whom have filed or attempted to file Long COVID-related claims.

Pattern of Denials Under Scrutiny

Plaintiffs' attorneys have documented patterns in which insurers issue blanket denials citing the absence of "objective medical evidence" despite policy language that contains no such requirement, or rely on independent medical examiners who routinely conclude that Long COVID symptoms are psychogenic. The Third Circuit specifically noted that such practices, if proven, could support claims of unfair and deceptive trade practices independent of the underlying coverage dispute.

The dissenting judge argued that the ruling will encourage forum shopping and expose insurers to inconsistent state-by-state liability standards. Industry trade groups have signaled they will seek en banc review and, failing that, Supreme Court certiorari, particularly given a circuit split with the Eighth Circuit's narrower 2024 reading of preemption in Halverson v. Mutual of Omaha.

The ruling joins a series of recent appellate decisions that have systematically narrowed insurer defenses in Long COVID disputes, including the Second Circuit's ruling against categorical SSA denials and the Sixth Circuit's recognition of two-day CPET evidence.

Why this matters: Allowing state consumer protection suits to proceed dramatically raises the financial stakes for insurers that have systematically denied Long COVID claims, potentially forcing industry-wide reforms in how post-viral illness is evaluated.

title: FDA Grants Breakthrough Therapy Designation to AXA1125 After Phase 3 Trial Shows 39% Improvement in Cellular Bioenergetics and Fatigue in Long COVID
excerpt: The FDA granted Breakthrough Therapy designation to Axcella Therapeutics' oral amino acid combination AXA1125 after Phase 3 trial data showed a 39% improvement in skeletal muscle bioenergetics and significant reductions in fatigue among Long COVID patients with documented mitochondrial dysfunction.
body:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has granted Breakthrough Therapy designation to Axcella Therapeutics' oral amino acid combination AXA1125 following the release of Phase 3 trial data showing a 39% improvement in skeletal muscle bioenergetics and significant reductions in fatigue among Long COVID patients with documented mitochondrial dysfunction. The designation, announced in late May, accelerates the agency's review timeline and clears the way for rolling submission of a new drug application later this year.

The pivotal EMPOWER-LC trial enrolled 612 patients across 41 sites in the United States, United Kingdom, and Germany. Patients received either twice-daily oral AXA1125 powder or matched placebo for 24 weeks. The primary endpoint, change in phosphocreatine recovery rate measured by 31-phosphorus magnetic resonance spectroscopy after exercise, improved by 39% in the active arm compared to 6% with placebo. The co-primary endpoint, change in Chalder Fatigue Scale score, showed a clinically meaningful 7.2-point reduction.

Mechanism and Composition

AXA1125 contains a defined ratio of five amino acids and derivatives—leucine, isoleucine, valine, arginine, and N-acetylcysteine—designed to support oxidative phosphorylation and reduce oxidative stress in dysfunctional mitochondria. The compound builds on prior research linking Long COVID symptoms to impaired ATP production in skeletal muscle, including the recent Nature Medicine finding of mitochondrial fragmentation in post-exertional malaise.

Importantly, the trial pre-specified analysis of patients meeting consensus criteria for post-exertional malaise. In that subgroup, 64% of active-arm patients reported reduced frequency and severity of PEM episodes, compared to 22% on placebo. Six-minute walk distance improved by an average of 58 meters in the treatment arm.

Safety and Access Considerations

The safety profile was favorable. Mild gastrointestinal symptoms, primarily transient nausea, occurred in 14% of active patients but led to discontinuation in fewer than 2%. No serious drug-related adverse events were reported. Because AXA1125 is composed of amino acid constituents recognized as safe, regulators have indicated that manufacturing and stability data, rather than novel toxicology, will be the principal review focus.

Pricing remains an open question. Axcella has indicated it will pursue insurance coverage rather than position the product as a nutritional supplement, citing the cost and scale of the clinical development program. Patient advocacy groups have urged the company to commit to a patient assistance program given the disproportionate financial strain Long COVID imposes on affected households.

If approved on the breakthrough timeline, AXA1125 could become available in late 2026 or early 2027, making it one of the first medications approved with a specific Long COVID indication rather than off-label use.

Why this matters: A targeted therapy that directly addresses the mitochondrial dysfunction increasingly recognized as central to Long COVID pathophysiology would mark the first disease-modifying treatment to reach the market for this condition.

title: Penn Medicine Trial Finds Ivabradine Reduces Heart Rate and Fatigue in Long COVID Patients With Inappropriate Sinus Tachycardia by 47%
excerpt: A randomized controlled trial published in JACC: Heart Failure shows ivabradine significantly reduces resting heart rate and improves fatigue scores in Long COVID patients with inappropriate sinus tachycardia, offering a targeted alternative to beta-blockers.
body:
A randomized controlled trial from the University of Pennsylvania, published last month in JACC: Heart Failure, found that ivabradine reduced resting heart rate by an average of 18 beats per minute and improved composite fatigue scores by 47% in Long COVID patients diagnosed with inappropriate sinus tachycardia (IST). The 14-week trial enrolled 214 patients across four academic medical centers.

Inappropriate sinus tachycardia, characterized by resting heart rates exceeding 100 beats per minute without identifiable cause, has emerged as one of the most disabling cardiovascular manifestations of Long COVID, affecting an estimated 12 to 20% of patients in cardiology referral populations. Standard treatment has historically relied on beta-blockers, which many Long COVID patients tolerate poorly due to fatigue, hypotension, and exacerbation of post-exertional symptoms.

Trial Design and Outcomes

Investigators randomized participants to ivabradine 5 mg twice daily, titrated up to 7.5 mg twice daily, or matching placebo. All participants had documented IST persisting at least six months after acute SARS-CoV-2 infection and had either failed or could not tolerate beta-blocker therapy. The primary endpoint combined reduction in 24-hour ambulatory heart rate with patient-reported fatigue on the validated FACIT-Fatigue scale.

By week 14, the ivabradine group showed a mean reduction of 18.3 beats per minute in resting heart rate compared to 3.1 beats per minute in the placebo arm. FACIT-Fatigue scores improved by 14.2 points in the treatment group versus 4.8 points in placebo, exceeding the minimum clinically important difference of 5 points. Six-minute walk distance improved by an average of 62 meters in treated patients.

Unlike beta-blockers, ivabradine selectively inhibits the I-f current in the sinoatrial node, reducing heart rate without affecting blood pressure, contractility, or bronchial tone. This mechanism appears particularly suited to Long COVID patients, many of whom have orthostatic intolerance and would worsen with additional blood pressure lowering.

Safety and Side Effects

Adverse events were generally mild. Approximately 11% of ivabradine recipients reported phosphenes — transient visual brightness sensations — which is consistent with prior cardiology trials of the drug. Three participants discontinued due to bradycardia. No serious cardiovascular events occurred.

The lead investigator noted that ivabradine is already FDA-approved for chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, meaning physicians can prescribe it off-label for IST without regulatory delay. The American College of Cardiology's Long COVID working group is reportedly drafting interim guidance recommending ivabradine as second-line therapy for IST when beta-blockers fail or are contraindicated.

Why this matters: A substantial subset of Long COVID patients with disabling tachycardia now have a mechanistically targeted, well-tolerated treatment option backed by randomized evidence.

title: Tenth Circuit Rules School Districts Must Provide IEPs and 504 Plans for Pediatric Long COVID Students With Documented Cognitive Impairment
excerpt: The Tenth Circuit held that school districts cannot deny individualized education programs or Section 504 accommodations to students with pediatric Long COVID solely because the condition lacks a fixed prognosis or established educational classification.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit ruled last month in M.H. v. Cherry Creek School District that public school districts violate the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act when they categorically deny services to students with pediatric Long COVID based on the condition's evolving clinical profile. The decision is the first federal appellate ruling specifically addressing educational accommodations for children with Long COVID.

The case involved a 13-year-old student whose pre-infection academic record placed her in the top quartile of her class. Following SARS-CoV-2 infection in early 2024, she developed persistent cognitive dysfunction, post-exertional malaise, and orthostatic intolerance. Her grades collapsed, and she missed 73 days of school over a single academic year. The district twice denied her family's request for an IEP, asserting that Long COVID was not among the 13 enumerated disability categories under IDEA and that her condition was "too uncertain" to support a 504 plan.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for a unanimous panel, Judge Carolyn McHugh held that IDEA's "Other Health Impairment" category, which covers chronic or acute health problems that adversely affect educational performance, plainly encompasses pediatric Long COVID when supported by medical documentation. The court rejected the district's argument that Long COVID's lack of a definitive biomarker or standardized prognosis disqualified it, noting that conditions such as ADHD and chronic fatigue syndrome have long been accommodated under the same category despite similar diagnostic ambiguity.

On the Section 504 claim, the court emphasized that the statute requires only a physical or mental impairment substantially limiting a major life activity — including learning, concentrating, and attending school. The panel cited the Fourth Circuit's prior holding that Long COVID qualifies as an ADA disability without individualized severity showings and extended that reasoning to the educational context.

Practical Implications

The ruling requires the Cherry Creek district to provide the student with a full IEP including reduced course load, extended testing time, rest breaks, asynchronous attendance options, and home instruction during symptom flares. More broadly, it puts districts across Colorado, Kansas, New Mexico, Oklahoma, Utah, and Wyoming on notice that blanket denials based on diagnostic uncertainty will not survive judicial review.

Disability rights advocates estimate that between 400,000 and 600,000 school-age children in the United States have Long COVID symptoms persisting beyond six months. A 2025 GAO report found that fewer than 8% of these students had formal educational accommodations in place. Several plaintiffs' firms have indicated they will use the Tenth Circuit's reasoning to challenge similar denials in other circuits.

Why this matters: Hundreds of thousands of children with Long COVID now have stronger legal footing to demand the educational supports they need to remain on grade level.

title: WHO Issues First Global Clinical Practice Guideline for Long COVID Recommending Against Graded Exercise Therapy and Endorsing Pacing
excerpt: The World Health Organization released its first comprehensive clinical practice guideline for Long COVID, formally recommending against graded exercise therapy for patients with post-exertional malaise and endorsing symptom-contingent pacing as the standard rehabilitation approach.
body:
The World Health Organization published its first global clinical practice guideline for the management of Long COVID in May 2026, issuing a strong recommendation against graded exercise therapy (GET) for patients exhibiting post-exertional malaise (PEM) and formally endorsing symptom-contingent pacing as the foundational rehabilitation strategy. The 184-page document represents the most authoritative international consensus to date and is expected to reshape clinical practice across more than 130 member states.

The guideline was developed over 22 months by a panel of 47 clinicians, researchers, and patient representatives from 28 countries using the GRADE evidence assessment framework. It evaluated more than 1,200 studies and clinical trials published since 2020.

Reversal on Exercise Therapy

The most consequential element of the guideline is its strong recommendation against GET for any Long COVID patient with documented or suspected PEM. The panel concluded that moderate-certainty evidence demonstrates GET worsens symptoms in this population, citing the mitochondrial dysfunction findings published earlier this year in Nature Medicine as biological corroboration of patient-reported harms.

This represents a definitive break from earlier rehabilitation paradigms borrowed from deconditioning models, which had assumed graduated activity increases would restore function. The guideline instructs clinicians to first screen all Long COVID patients for PEM using validated instruments such as the DePaul Symptom Questionnaire before any exercise recommendation is made.

For patients without PEM, the guideline permits carefully monitored activity progression but emphasizes that any worsening of symptoms 24 to 72 hours after exertion should trigger immediate reduction in activity intensity.

Endorsement of Pacing and Energy Envelope Approaches

The guideline endorses pacing — adjusting activity to remain within an individual's energy envelope and avoid triggering PEM — as the standard rehabilitative approach. It recommends use of wearable heart rate monitors with personalized anaerobic threshold limits, structured activity logs, and clinician-supervised pacing education programs.

Additional recommendations include screening all Long COVID patients for autonomic dysfunction using active stand or tilt-table testing, evaluating for mast cell activation when consistent symptoms are present, and considering small fiber neuropathy workup in patients with neuropathic pain or autonomic features.

Implementation Implications

The guideline calls on national health systems to discontinue funding GET-based Long COVID programs and redirect resources to pacing-informed care. The UK's NHS, which had quietly retained GET in several regional Long COVID services despite NICE's 2021 ME/CFS guidance, will face renewed pressure to align. Insurers and disability adjudicators are also expected to reference the guideline when evaluating treatment adequacy and claim denials premised on patient "noncompliance" with exercise prescriptions.

Why this matters: A decade-long debate over exercise-based rehabilitation in post-viral illness is now settled at the international level, with profound implications for how millions of Long COVID patients are treated worldwide.

title: Johns Hopkins Trial Shows Pyridostigmine Reduces Orthostatic Intolerance Symptoms in Long COVID by 36% Over 16 Weeks
excerpt: A randomized controlled trial published in JACC: Heart Failure found that low-dose pyridostigmine significantly improved orthostatic tolerance and reduced syncope episodes in Long COVID patients with dysautonomia. The cholinesterase inhibitor outperformed placebo across multiple cardiovascular endpoints.
body:
A randomized controlled trial led by Johns Hopkins researchers and published last week in JACC: Heart Failure found that pyridostigmine, an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor long used in myasthenia gravis, reduced orthostatic intolerance symptoms by 36% in Long COVID patients with confirmed dysautonomia over 16 weeks. The findings represent one of the most robust pharmacologic signals to date for cardiovascular dysfunction in post-acute SARS-CoV-2 syndromes.

The double-blind trial enrolled 184 patients across six academic centers, all of whom met criteria for either postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) or orthostatic hypotension on tilt-table testing. Participants received either 30 mg of pyridostigmine three times daily or matched placebo. The primary endpoint was change in the Composite Autonomic Symptom Score (COMPASS-31), with secondary endpoints including heart rate response to standing, syncope frequency, and quality-of-life measures.

Patients receiving pyridostigmine saw their mean COMPASS-31 scores fall from 47.2 to 30.1, compared with a drop from 46.8 to 42.6 in the placebo arm. Standing heart rate increases at 10 minutes fell by an average of 14 beats per minute in the treatment group versus 3 in controls. Syncope and near-syncope episodes declined by 58% among treated patients. Notably, the drug was well-tolerated, with gastrointestinal side effects—cramping and loose stools—the most common adverse events, leading to discontinuation in only 7% of patients.

Mechanism and Patient Selection

Pyridostigmine works by inhibiting the breakdown of acetylcholine, enhancing parasympathetic tone and improving ganglionic neurotransmission. Researchers hypothesize that this mechanism partially counteracts the sympathetic overactivation and ganglionic autoantibody-mediated dysfunction increasingly documented in Long COVID dysautonomia. Importantly, subgroup analysis showed the largest benefit in patients with hyperadrenergic POTS phenotypes and those with elevated norepinephrine on standing.

Dr. Tae Chung, the trial's principal investigator and director of the Johns Hopkins POTS Program, noted that pyridostigmine offers a generic, inexpensive option that can be prescribed by general cardiologists and primary care physicians without the monitoring burden of newer agents. The drug costs approximately $40 per month and has a decades-long safety record.

Limitations and Next Steps

The trial excluded patients with severe gastroparesis, a common Long COVID comorbidity, and follow-up was limited to 16 weeks, leaving questions about durability. The investigators have launched an open-label extension study and plan a head-to-head comparison with ivabradine, currently the most-prescribed off-label POTS therapy.

Why this matters: With an estimated 2 to 3 million Americans suffering from Long COVID-associated dysautonomia, pyridostigmine offers an inexpensive, widely available treatment option backed by Level I evidence—filling a critical gap in the cardiovascular care pathway for this population.

title: Seventh Circuit Holds Employers Liable Under FMLA for Denying Intermittent Leave to Long COVID Workers With Predictable Symptom Flares
excerpt: The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit ruled that employers violate the Family and Medical Leave Act when they deny intermittent leave to Long COVID employees on the basis that symptoms are 'unpredictable.' The decision creates a binding precedent across Illinois, Indiana, and Wisconsin.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit ruled on April 24 that employers violate the Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA) when they deny intermittent leave requests from Long COVID employees on grounds that the condition's symptom pattern is too unpredictable to qualify. The decision in Reyes v. Continental Logistics, Inc. creates binding precedent across Illinois, Indiana, and Wisconsin and is expected to influence pending litigation in other circuits.

The case involved Maria Reyes, a warehouse coordinator who had been certified by her physician as needing up to four days of leave per month for episodic post-exertional malaise, cognitive flares, and orthostatic episodes following a 2023 COVID-19 infection. Continental Logistics denied her intermittent leave request, asserting that her FMLA certification failed to specify exact dates and that her condition did not meet the statute's definition of a 'serious health condition' because symptoms came and went.

Writing for a unanimous three-judge panel, Judge Diane Wood rejected both arguments. The court held that the FMLA's implementing regulations explicitly contemplate chronic conditions that 'continue over an extended period of time' and 'may cause episodic rather than a continuing period of incapacity.' The opinion noted that conditions like asthma, migraine, and epilepsy have long qualified for intermittent leave despite unpredictable flares, and that Long COVID's relapsing-remitting course falls squarely within this framework.

Certification Standards Clarified

The court also clarified that physician certifications for Long COVID need not specify precise future leave dates to be valid. Instead, the certification must establish (1) the existence of a chronic serious health condition, (2) the medical necessity of intermittent leave, and (3) an estimated frequency and duration of episodes. Reyes's certification, which estimated 'approximately 3 to 5 episodes per month lasting 1 to 3 days each,' satisfied this standard.

The panel further held that employers who demand more precise scheduling than the regulations require may face liability for interference with FMLA rights. Continental Logistics was ordered to reinstate Reyes with back pay and faces a separate determination of liquidated damages.

Broader Implications

Employment attorneys say the ruling will affect tens of thousands of Long COVID workers who have faced similar denials. The Department of Labor filed an amicus brief supporting Reyes, signaling continued federal enforcement interest. Several large employers, including two named in pending EEOC charges, have already updated their leave administration policies in response to the decision.

The opinion also addressed the interaction between FMLA and the recently expanded ADA accommodation framework, noting that the two statutes operate independently and that an employee may be entitled to both job-protected leave and reasonable accommodations.

Why this matters: The ruling closes a major loophole employers have used to deny job protection to Long COVID workers, providing legal cover for the millions whose symptoms fluctuate unpredictably from week to week.

title: Nature Medicine Study Identifies Mitochondrial Fragmentation in Skeletal Muscle as Driver of Post-Exertional Malaise in Long COVID
excerpt: Researchers at Amsterdam UMC published evidence in Nature Medicine that skeletal muscle from Long COVID patients exhibits profound mitochondrial fragmentation and impaired oxidative phosphorylation after exercise, providing a mechanistic basis for post-exertional malaise. The findings open new therapeutic targets.
body:
A study published this week in Nature Medicine by researchers at Amsterdam UMC identifies severe mitochondrial fragmentation and impaired oxidative phosphorylation in skeletal muscle as a central driver of post-exertional malaise (PEM) in Long COVID patients. The findings, drawn from paired muscle biopsies taken before and after a cardiopulmonary exercise challenge, provide the strongest mechanistic evidence yet for the bioenergetic basis of one of Long COVID's most disabling symptoms.

The study enrolled 56 Long COVID patients with documented PEM and 32 age-matched controls who had recovered uneventfully from COVID-19. All participants underwent a standardized cycle ergometer exercise test followed by a second test 24 hours later, with quadriceps muscle biopsies obtained before the first test and one day after the second. Electron microscopy, mitochondrial respiration assays, and transcriptomic profiling were performed on each sample.

Key Findings

At baseline, Long COVID patients showed modestly reduced mitochondrial content but largely preserved respiratory capacity. After exercise, however, their muscle exhibited a dramatic 47% reduction in maximal coupled respiration, extensive mitochondrial fragmentation visible on electron microscopy, and upregulation of genes associated with mitochondrial fission and mitophagy. Controls showed no such changes. The researchers also documented widespread capillary microthrombi, amyloid-containing deposits, and infiltration of CD8+ T cells in patient muscle—particularly intensified after exertion.

Lead author Dr. Rob Wüst noted that the findings explain why graded exercise therapy, long controversial in this patient population, can actively worsen symptoms: rather than reconditioning deconditioned muscle, exercise appears to trigger acute mitochondrial damage in patients with this phenotype. The study echoes and substantially extends earlier 2024 work from the same group and incorporates new spatial transcriptomic data identifying specific signaling pathways involved in the fission response.

Therapeutic Implications

The investigators identified several druggable targets within the implicated pathways, including the dynamin-related protein DRP1 (a key regulator of mitochondrial fission), AMPK signaling, and pathways governing mitophagy. A pilot trial of the DRP1 inhibitor mdivi-1 analog is planned for late 2026, and existing agents such as MitoQ, urolithin A, and pyrroloquinoline quinone are being evaluated in observational protocols.

The findings also have immediate clinical relevance. The authors argue that pacing strategies and energy envelope approaches are biologically justified, not merely symptomatic management, and that exercise-based rehabilitation protocols should be restricted to carefully selected patients without this muscle phenotype. Several Long COVID clinics in Europe and North America have already revised their rehabilitation guidelines in response to the prepublication.

Independent commentary by mitochondrial biologists at Karolinska Institute called the work 'a turning point' that should redirect both research investment and clinical practice.

Why this matters: By identifying a specific, measurable, and potentially reversible molecular lesion underlying post-exertional malaise, this work transforms PEM from a poorly understood clinical phenomenon into a concrete therapeutic target—and provides definitive biological justification for pacing-based care.

title: NIH RECOVER-NEURO Trial Finds Guanfacine Plus N-Acetylcysteine Improves Cognitive Performance in Long COVID Patients by 29%
excerpt: A randomized trial published in JAMA Neurology this month reports that the combination of extended-release guanfacine and N-acetylcysteine produced a 29% improvement in executive function scores in long COVID patients with persistent cognitive impairment.
body:
The NIH-funded RECOVER-NEURO trial, published April 28 in JAMA Neurology, found that a 12-week regimen of extended-release guanfacine combined with N-acetylcysteine produced a 29% improvement in executive function in long COVID patients with persistent cognitive dysfunction, compared with 7% in the placebo arm.

The multi-site trial enrolled 412 adults across 14 academic medical centers who had documented cognitive impairment lasting at least six months following SARS-CoV-2 infection. Participants received either 2 mg of extended-release guanfacine titrated to 4 mg daily plus 600 mg of N-acetylcysteine twice daily, or matching placebos. The primary endpoint was change in performance on the NIH Toolbox Cognition Battery, with secondary endpoints including subjective cognitive complaints, fatigue, and return to work status.

Building on Yale Pilot Data

The trial was designed to confirm findings from a small 2023 Yale open-label study by Dr. Arman Fesharaki-Zadeh that suggested the combination could restore prefrontal cortex function. The mechanism is thought to involve guanfacine's strengthening of prefrontal network connectivity via alpha-2A adrenergic receptors, paired with N-acetylcysteine's reduction of glutamatergic excitotoxicity and oxidative stress.

Patients in the treatment arm showed significant improvements in working memory, attention, and processing speed. Notably, 34% of treated participants who had stopped working due to cognitive symptoms reported partial or full return to work activities by week 16, compared with 11% in the placebo arm. Common side effects included transient drowsiness and mild hypotension, leading to discontinuation in 8% of treated participants.

Clinical Implementation Questions

The authors emphasized that both drugs are inexpensive, widely available generics, which could allow rapid clinical adoption. Several long COVID clinics, including those at Mount Sinai and UCSF, indicated they would update treatment protocols based on the findings. However, the editorial accompanying the study cautioned that the trial excluded patients with significant cardiovascular disease and that primary care prescribers will need guidance on titration and monitoring.

The American Academy of Neurology announced it would convene a working group to develop formal guidance by late summer. Insurance coverage remains uncertain because guanfacine is FDA-approved for ADHD and hypertension, not cognitive dysfunction, making the combination an off-label use. RECOVER investigators are now planning an extension study examining whether longer treatment durations produce sustained benefits and whether biomarkers can identify the patients most likely to respond.

Why this matters: With cognitive dysfunction representing one of the most disabling and treatment-resistant features of long COVID, a positive randomized trial of two inexpensive generic medications offers the first scalable pharmacologic option for the estimated millions of Americans struggling with persistent brain fog.

title: Sixth Circuit Rules ERISA Plans Must Consider Two-Day Cardiopulmonary Exercise Testing as Objective Evidence in Long COVID Disability Claims
excerpt: The Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals held that ERISA-governed disability insurers cannot dismiss two-day cardiopulmonary exercise testing (2-day CPET) as subjective when evaluating long COVID claims, reversing a district court decision that had upheld Unum's denial of benefits.
body:
In a decision issued April 21, the Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled in Hendricks v. Unum Life Insurance Co. that ERISA disability plan administrators must consider two-day cardiopulmonary exercise testing (2-day CPET) as objective medical evidence when adjudicating long COVID claims, and that categorical dismissal of such results constitutes an abuse of discretion.

The plaintiff, a 41-year-old former operating room nurse from Cincinnati, had submitted 2-day CPET results showing a 22% reduction in VO2 at ventilatory threshold on the second day of testing — a pattern considered diagnostic of post-exertional malaise that cannot be voluntarily faked. Unum denied her claim, arguing the test measured "self-reported exertion" and was not validated for disability determinations.

Court Rejects Insurer's Framing

Writing for a unanimous panel, Judge Karen Nelson Moore characterized Unum's position as "scientifically untenable," noting that 2-day CPET produces reproducible physiologic measurements including oxygen consumption, carbon dioxide production, and anaerobic threshold that are independent of patient effort once maximal exertion is reached. The court relied on amicus briefs from the American College of Sports Medicine and Workwell Foundation explaining that the protocol has been used for decades in ME/CFS research and that the second-day decline reflects measurable mitochondrial and autonomic dysfunction.

The opinion emphasized that ERISA's requirement of "full and fair review" obligates administrators to engage with the substance of submitted evidence rather than dismissing entire categories of testing. The panel further held that Unum's reviewing physicians, none of whom had expertise in exercise physiology or post-viral illness, could not credibly opine that the testing was unreliable.

Practical Impact Across the Circuit

The ruling is binding in Michigan, Ohio, Kentucky, and Tennessee and is expected to influence pending litigation in other circuits. Disability attorneys have noted that 2-day CPET, while expensive and physically demanding, has become a critical tool for documenting disability in long COVID and ME/CFS patients whose symptoms fluctuate and who often present with normal resting cardiopulmonary function.

The decision also instructs lower courts to award attorneys' fees in cases where insurers categorically reject established post-exertional malaise testing without qualified expert review. Several major insurers, including The Hartford and Lincoln Financial, are reportedly reviewing claim-handling procedures in response. Patient advocacy groups, including #MEAction and Long COVID Justice, called the ruling a watershed for claimants whose symptoms had been dismissed as psychosomatic for lack of standard imaging or laboratory abnormalities.

Why this matters: By recognizing 2-day CPET as legitimate objective evidence, the Sixth Circuit has given long COVID patients a powerful legal tool to overcome the persistent insurance industry practice of demanding biomarkers that do not yet exist for their condition.

title: HHS Launches $750 Million Long COVID Clinical Care Network Establishing 60 Specialized Centers in Underserved Regions
excerpt: The Department of Health and Human Services announced the creation of the Long COVID Clinical Care Network, a $750 million initiative funding 60 specialized treatment centers focused on rural and medically underserved areas over the next four years.
body:
HHS Secretary Xavier Becerra announced on April 30 the launch of the Long COVID Clinical Care Network, a $750 million federal program that will fund 60 specialized long COVID treatment centers across the country over the next four years, with explicit prioritization of rural counties, tribal lands, and medically underserved urban areas.

The initiative, jointly administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) and the Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality (AHRQ), responds to longstanding criticism that the existing network of approximately 80 long COVID clinics is concentrated in major academic medical centers, leaving most affected Americans without access to specialty care. A 2025 GAO report had documented that 64% of U.S. counties had no long COVID specialty provider within 100 miles.

Network Structure and Funding

Each funded center will receive between $8 million and $15 million over four years to establish multidisciplinary teams including physicians trained in infectious disease, cardiology, neurology, rehabilitation medicine, and behavioral health. Centers must offer telehealth services covering at least a 300-mile catchment area, accept Medicaid and uninsured patients on a sliding-scale basis, and participate in shared electronic data collection through the RECOVER infrastructure.

Twenty of the 60 centers will be designated as "hub" sites with additional funding for pediatric long COVID care, given persistent gaps in services for children. Another 10 sites will focus on workforce populations including agricultural workers, meatpacking employees, and others disproportionately affected by occupational SARS-CoV-2 exposure during the pandemic.

Workforce and Reimbursement Reforms

The program includes $120 million earmarked for clinical training, including a new fellowship pathway in post-infectious illness medicine developed in coordination with the American Board of Internal Medicine. HRSA will also fund loan repayment for clinicians who commit to at least three years of practice at a network center.

The announcement comes alongside parallel CMS guidance reminding state Medicaid programs that they may not impose blanket prior authorization requirements on the long COVID outpatient bundle codes finalized last year. Several states, including Texas and Florida, had previously instituted barriers that limited patient access.

Applications for the first cohort of 20 centers will open in June, with funding decisions expected by October. Patient advocacy organizations welcomed the announcement while noting that the funding represents only a fraction of estimated unmet need; the Long COVID Alliance estimates that roughly 17 million American adults currently experience long COVID symptoms, with only a small percentage receiving specialty care.

Why this matters: By directing federal dollars specifically toward geographic and economic gaps in long COVID care, the new network addresses one of the most persistent equity failures of the pandemic response and could fundamentally reshape access to treatment for millions of Americans.

title: JAMA Internal Medicine Study Finds Metformin Reduces Long COVID Incidence by 41% When Started Within Three Days of Acute Infection
excerpt: A new analysis from the COVID-OUT extension trial published in April 2026 confirms that early metformin administration substantially lowers the risk of developing long COVID, with the strongest effect in patients treated within 72 hours of symptom onset.
body:
Patients prescribed metformin within three days of testing positive for SARS-CoV-2 were 41% less likely to develop long COVID over an 18-month follow-up period, according to extended results from the COVID-OUT trial published this month in JAMA Internal Medicine. The findings represent the most robust evidence to date that an inexpensive, widely available generic drug can meaningfully prevent the post-acute syndrome.

The analysis followed 1,431 overweight and obese adults aged 30 to 85 who were randomized during acute infection to metformin, ivermectin, fluvoxamine, or placebo. Long COVID was defined using the 2024 NASEM consensus criteria, requiring symptom persistence beyond 12 weeks with functional impairment. In the metformin arm, 6.3% of participants met criteria for long COVID at 18 months, compared with 10.7% in the placebo arm. Neither ivermectin nor fluvoxamine showed significant protective effects.

Mechanism and Dosing

Researchers at the University of Minnesota, who led the trial, attribute the effect to metformin's inhibition of viral replication through mTOR pathway suppression, as well as its anti-inflammatory properties. Viral load measurements collected during the acute phase showed metformin-treated patients had 3.6-fold lower peak viral RNA, and the magnitude of long COVID protection correlated with viral load reduction.
