The standard dosing protocol used was 500 mg twice daily on day one, 500 mg three times daily for four days, then 500 mg twice daily for nine days.

Notably, the protective effect held across vaccination status, age strata, and dominant variants circulating during the trial period, including Omicron subvariants. Subgroup analysis showed the benefit was largest in unvaccinated participants (53% relative risk reduction) but remained significant in fully vaccinated individuals (32% reduction).

Clinical Practice Implications

The Infectious Diseases Society of America has indicated it will review the data at its June 2026 guidelines committee meeting, with multiple members publicly suggesting metformin should be added as a conditional recommendation for outpatient COVID-19 management in adults with elevated BMI. The American College of Physicians issued a clinical alert on April 22 recommending clinicians discuss the option with eligible patients pending formal guideline updates.

Critics note that the trial population was limited to patients with BMI of 25 or higher, leaving questions about generalizability to normal-weight individuals. A companion trial, COVID-OUT-2, enrolling lean adults began recruiting in March 2026 and is expected to report in 2027. Cost-effectiveness modeling published alongside the main paper estimates the intervention at $87 per case of long COVID prevented.

Why this matters: With no approved treatments for established long COVID and millions still developing the condition annually, a cheap generic drug taken during acute infection could prevent hundreds of thousands of new cases each year if widely adopted.

title: Department of Labor Issues Final Rule Classifying Long COVID as Presumptive Occupational Illness for Healthcare and Frontline Federal Workers
excerpt: The Department of Labor's Office of Workers' Compensation Programs finalized a rule in April 2026 establishing a presumption that long COVID in covered federal employees is work-related, dramatically easing access to FECA benefits.
body:
Federal healthcare workers, TSA screeners, postal employees, and other frontline federal workers who develop long COVID will now be presumed to have contracted the illness in the course of employment under a final rule published by the Department of Labor on April 28, 2026. The rule, which takes effect July 1, shifts the burden of proof from injured workers to the government in Federal Employees' Compensation Act claims.

Under the prior framework, federal workers seeking FECA benefits for long COVID had to affirmatively demonstrate that their SARS-CoV-2 infection occurred at work — a near-impossible standard given community transmission. OWCP data showed that only 34% of long COVID FECA claims filed between 2022 and 2025 were approved on initial review, with the average claim taking 11 months to adjudicate. Advocacy groups including the American Federation of Government Employees and National Nurses United had petitioned for the change since 2023.

Scope of the Presumption

The rule applies to employees in occupational categories with documented elevated SARS-CoV-2 exposure risk, including VA hospital staff, Indian Health Service clinicians, Bureau of Prisons personnel, CBP and TSA officers, and USPS employees with public-facing duties. To trigger the presumption, a claimant must provide documentation of a positive SARS-CoV-2 test (PCR, antigen, or antibody) and a long COVID diagnosis using the new ICD-10 subtype codes finalized by CMS, along with employment in a covered position during the 60 days preceding infection.

The presumption is rebuttable, but the rule requires the agency to produce "substantial evidence" of non-occupational exposure to overcome it — a high bar that effectively shifts most cases toward approval. OWCP estimates the rule will result in approximately 18,000 additional approved claims annually and add $340 million to FECA outlays.

State-Level Ripple Effects

The federal action is expected to accelerate parallel efforts in state workers' compensation systems. California, New York, Illinois, and Washington already have COVID-19 occupational presumptions for healthcare workers and first responders, but most states require workers to prove workplace exposure. Legislation modeled on the DOL rule has been introduced in Massachusetts, Michigan, and Oregon during the current legislative sessions, and the National Council on Compensation Insurance announced it would issue updated guidance to state regulators in July.

Employer groups including the U.S. Chamber of Commerce have signaled they will challenge state-level expansions, arguing presumptions distort risk-based ratemaking. The DOL rule itself faces a likely Congressional Review Act challenge, though the 60-day window for resolution and current Senate composition make repeal unlikely.

Why this matters: Presumptive coverage removes the single biggest barrier to compensation for hundreds of thousands of frontline workers disabled by long COVID, and federal action typically catalyzes broader state and private insurer reforms.

title: Lancet Respiratory Medicine Trial Shows Temelimab Reduces Fatigue Scores by 44% in Long COVID Patients With Elevated HERV-W Envelope Protein
excerpt: A Phase 2 trial published in Lancet Respiratory Medicine in April 2026 found that temelimab, a monoclonal antibody targeting human endogenous retrovirus W envelope protein, significantly improved fatigue and cognitive symptoms in a biomarker-defined subset of long COVID patients.
body:
Long COVID patients with elevated levels of human endogenous retrovirus W envelope protein (HERV-W ENV) experienced a 44% reduction in fatigue severity after 24 weeks of treatment with temelimab, according to results from the GNK-301 trial published April 19 in Lancet Respiratory Medicine. The biomarker-stratified study represents the first positive Phase 2 result for a precision medicine approach to long COVID.

The trial enrolled 312 patients across 28 sites in Europe and North America who met criteria for long COVID with predominant fatigue and cognitive symptoms persisting at least nine months. All participants were screened for serum HERV-W ENV levels, with 188 patients above the prespecified threshold of 250 pg/mL randomized to receive monthly infusions of temelimab (18 mg/kg) or placebo. Patients with normal HERV-W ENV levels were enrolled in a parallel observational arm.

Biomarker-Driven Response

In the biomarker-positive group, Chalder Fatigue Scale scores fell from a baseline mean of 24.8 to 13.9 at week 24 in the temelimab arm, compared with a reduction to 22.1 in the placebo arm. Secondary endpoints showed significant improvements in cognitive testing (Cogstate composite score improved 0.61 standard deviations versus 0.12 in placebo) and 6-minute walk distance (mean increase of 67 meters versus 19 meters). The treatment was well tolerated, with infusion reactions in 8% of patients and no serious adverse events attributed to the drug.

HERV-W ENV is a protein encoded by an ancient retroviral sequence integrated into the human genome that is normally silenced but becomes reactivated by certain viral infections including SARS-CoV-2, Epstein-Barr virus, and HIV. Prior work from the Pasteur Institute and GeNeuro, the drug's developer, established that the protein activates TLR4 receptors on microglia and endothelial cells, driving neuroinflammation and small vessel dysfunction. Temelimab neutralizes the circulating protein without affecting host genome function.

Path Forward and Limitations

Approximately 38% of long COVID patients screened for the trial had elevated HERV-W ENV, suggesting the biomarker identifies a substantial but distinct subgroup. Patients with HERV-W ENV levels below threshold who received temelimab in an exploratory cohort showed no benefit, reinforcing the importance of patient selection. The drug previously showed efficacy signals in multiple sclerosis and was acquired by a larger biotech in 2024 specifically to pursue the long COVID indication.

GeNeuro announced it will meet with the FDA in June to discuss Phase 3 trial design, with the company indicating it will seek a companion diagnostic approval alongside the therapeutic. Analysts estimate a potential 2028 approval if Phase 3 data replicate. The trial was not powered to assess effects on post-exertional malaise specifically, and patients with severe ME/CFS-like presentations were underrepresented.

Why this matters: After dozens of failed trials, temelimab offers the first compelling evidence that biomarker-stratified treatment can produce clinically meaningful improvements in long COVID, validating a precision medicine framework for the heterogeneous condition.

title: Mount Sinai Trial Finds Inspiratory Muscle Training Improves Exercise Capacity in Long COVID by 31% Over 12 Weeks
excerpt: A randomized controlled trial published in CHEST this April found that supervised inspiratory muscle training (IMT) significantly improved exercise tolerance and dyspnea scores in Long COVID patients with persistent breathlessness. The intervention requires no medication and can be performed at home with a handheld device.
body:
A randomized controlled trial from Mount Sinai's Cohen Center for Recovery from Complex Chronic Illnesses, published in CHEST in April 2026, found that a 12-week program of inspiratory muscle training (IMT) improved six-minute walk distance by 31% and reduced Borg dyspnea scores by nearly half in Long COVID patients with persistent breathlessness. The trial enrolled 184 patients who had remained symptomatic for at least 12 months after acute infection.

Participants used a threshold IMT device set to 50% of their maximum inspiratory pressure, performing 30 breaths twice daily, five days per week. Sessions were monitored remotely through a connected app that adjusted resistance weekly based on pressure measurements. The control group received standard pulmonary rehabilitation referrals without the IMT protocol.

Results Across Multiple Endpoints

Beyond walk distance, the IMT group showed a 27% improvement in maximal inspiratory pressure, a 19% reduction in resting respiratory rate, and statistically significant gains on the King's Brief Interstitial Lung Disease questionnaire. Notably, patients with documented diaphragmatic dysfunction on ultrasound at baseline showed the largest treatment effect, suggesting the intervention addresses a specific mechanical deficit rather than acting through general conditioning.

Importantly, the researchers screened out patients with post-exertional malaise (PEM) using the DePaul Symptom Questionnaire before enrollment, and no PEM flares were reported during the trial. Lead investigator Dr. David Putrino emphasized that IMT is fundamentally different from graded exercise therapy because it targets respiratory musculature without provoking the systemic exertion that triggers PEM in patients with ME/CFS-like presentations.

Mechanistic Considerations

Prior imaging work has documented diaphragm thinning and reduced excursion in a substantial subset of Long COVID patients, even those without abnormal pulmonary function tests. The Mount Sinai team hypothesizes that SARS-CoV-2 may produce a subacute myopathy of respiratory muscles that responds to targeted strengthening, much as IMT has been shown to benefit patients with heart failure and COPD.

The devices used in the trial retail for under $50, and the protocol does not require ongoing physician supervision once initial pressure measurements are obtained. Several major pulmonary rehabilitation programs, including those at Johns Hopkins and UCSF, have already announced plans to incorporate the protocol into their Long COVID clinics this summer.

An accompanying editorial cautioned that IMT should not be confused with broader exercise prescriptions and warned clinicians against extrapolating these results to patients with predominant PEM, for whom pacing remains the standard of care.

Why this matters: IMT offers a low-cost, scalable intervention for the breathless subtype of Long COVID that can be delivered without specialized equipment or graded exercise protocols that risk worsening post-exertional malaise.

title: Second Circuit Rules SSA Cannot Categorically Reject Long COVID Disability Claims Based on Normal Objective Testing
excerpt: In Hernandez v. Commissioner of Social Security, the Second Circuit held that administrative law judges may not deny Long COVID disability claims solely because routine laboratory and imaging studies appear normal. The ruling could reshape how SSA adjudicates tens of thousands of pending claims.
body:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit ruled on April 14 that the Social Security Administration may not deny Long COVID disability claims simply because conventional laboratory tests, chest imaging, and pulmonary function studies return within normal limits. The decision in Hernandez v. Commissioner of Social Security vacated an administrative law judge's denial and remanded the case with explicit instructions on how Long COVID symptoms must be evaluated.

The claimant, a 43-year-old former emergency room nurse, had been denied benefits after an ALJ concluded that her reports of disabling fatigue, cognitive impairment, and orthostatic intolerance were not supported by "objective medical evidence" because her CBC, CMP, thyroid panel, echocardiogram, and chest CT were all unremarkable. The Second Circuit found this reasoning legally erroneous.

The Court's Framework

Writing for a unanimous panel, Judge Susan Carney held that Long COVID, like fibromyalgia and chronic fatigue syndrome before it, is a condition for which routine testing is not expected to reveal abnormalities. The court relied heavily on SSA's own 2023 ruling SSR 23-1p, which acknowledged Long COVID as a medically determinable impairment, and on the CDC's diagnostic criteria, which do not require any specific laboratory finding.

The opinion identified categories of evidence that ALJs must consider as objective support, including documented tachycardia on tilt-table or active stand testing, reduced oxygen extraction on invasive cardiopulmonary exercise testing, abnormal findings on cognitive testing batteries such as the NIH Toolbox, and contemporaneous medical records documenting symptom onset following acute infection.

Implications for Pending Claims

SSA's most recent data show approximately 78,000 pending disability claims listing Long COVID or post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 as a primary or secondary impairment. Advocates estimate that a substantial majority of denials at the ALJ level have relied on reasoning materially similar to that rejected in Hernandez.

The ruling is binding in New York, Connecticut, and Vermont, but disability attorneys across the country are already citing it in briefs before other circuits. The Third and Ninth Circuits have pending Long COVID disability appeals that raise overlapping issues, and the Solicitor General has not indicated whether the government will seek Supreme Court review.

SSA issued a brief statement noting that it is "reviewing the decision and assessing operational implications." Several disability rights organizations, including the National Organization of Social Security Claimants' Representatives, have called on the agency to issue new internal guidance rather than continuing to litigate the question circuit by circuit.

Why this matters: The ruling removes a primary barrier that has caused systematic denial of Long COVID disability claims and establishes a roadmap for the kinds of evidence that must be credited in adjudication.

title: Cell Reports Medicine Study Identifies Distinct T-Cell Exhaustion Signature in Long COVID Patients With Persistent Viral RNA in Gut Biopsies
excerpt: Researchers at UCSF and the Karolinska Institute have identified a specific pattern of T-cell exhaustion in Long COVID patients whose gut biopsies show persistent SARS-CoV-2 RNA more than two years after infection. The findings point to viral persistence as a driver of immune dysfunction in a defined subgroup.
body:
A study published in Cell Reports Medicine in late April identified a distinct immunological signature in Long COVID patients harboring persistent SARS-CoV-2 RNA in intestinal tissue, characterized by exhausted CD8+ T cells expressing high levels of PD-1, TIM-3, and TIGIT. The work, a collaboration between UCSF's Long COVID Tissue Pathology Initiative and the Karolinska Institute, examined 94 Long COVID patients and 47 fully recovered controls who underwent voluntary colonoscopy with biopsies.

Viral RNA was detected by ultrasensitive digital droplet PCR in the terminal ileum or colon of 41% of Long COVID patients compared to 4% of recovered controls, with most patients more than 24 months past their acute infection. Patients with detectable tissue RNA scored significantly worse on validated symptom measures for fatigue, gastrointestinal dysfunction, and post-exertional malaise.

Immunological Findings

Single-cell RNA sequencing of peripheral blood from RNA-positive patients revealed a population of CD8+ T cells with a transcriptional profile resembling that seen in chronic viral infections such as HIV and hepatitis C. These cells showed reduced cytokine production upon stimulation and elevated expression of multiple checkpoint inhibitors. RNA-negative Long COVID patients did not show this signature, suggesting they represent a biologically distinct subgroup.

The exhausted T-cell population correlated with circulating levels of interferon gamma–induced protein 10 (IP-10) and soluble CD25, both markers of ongoing immune activation. Notably, several patients in the RNA-positive group had previously received extended courses of Paxlovid without symptom resolution, consistent with the recent RECOVER-VITAL findings that prolonged protease inhibition alone is insufficient to clear tissue reservoirs.

Therapeutic Implications

The authors argue that their findings provide a rationale for trials combining direct antivirals with immunomodulatory agents that can rescue exhausted T-cell function. A small investigator-initiated trial at UCSF is already enrolling RNA-positive patients to receive a 30-day course of ensitrelvir combined with low-dose pembrolizumab, though the use of checkpoint inhibitors in a non-malignant population remains controversial and is being conducted under careful safety monitoring.

Other groups are exploring whether less aggressive immunomodulation, such as IL-7 administration or targeting of regulatory T cells, might achieve similar effects with better safety profiles. The Karolinska team is also examining whether stool-based biomarkers might identify RNA-positive patients without requiring colonoscopy, which would make the subgroup easier to enrich for in future clinical trials.

Editorialists noted that the findings strengthen the biological case for viral persistence as one—though likely not the only—mechanism of Long COVID, and that subtyping patients by reservoir status may be essential for interpreting future trial results.

Why this matters: Linking tissue viral persistence to a specific immune dysfunction signature defines a treatable subgroup of Long COVID and provides a clear biological target for combination antiviral-immunomodulatory therapy.

title: Yale Study Links Long COVID Brain Fog to Reduced Cerebral Blood Flow on SPECT Imaging in 71% of Patients
excerpt: A Yale School of Medicine study published in JAMA Neurology found that 71% of Long COVID patients with cognitive symptoms showed measurable hypoperfusion in the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus on SPECT imaging, providing the first large-scale objective neuroimaging biomarker for the condition's cognitive manifestations.
body:
A Yale School of Medicine study published in JAMA Neurology this April found that 71% of Long COVID patients reporting cognitive dysfunction showed measurable reductions in cerebral blood flow on single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) imaging, marking the largest objective neuroimaging finding to date for what patients have long called "brain fog."

The study enrolled 412 patients with persistent cognitive complaints at least 12 months after acute SARS-CoV-2 infection, alongside 198 matched controls. Researchers led by Dr. Serena Spudich identified consistent hypoperfusion patterns in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, anterior cingulate, and hippocampal regions—areas governing executive function, attention, and memory consolidation. The magnitude of perfusion deficit correlated strongly with performance on the Montreal Cognitive Assessment and with patient-reported severity on the Cognitive Failures Questionnaire.

A Validated Biomarker After Years of Skepticism

For patients who have spent years being told their cognitive symptoms are psychogenic or stress-related, the finding offers something previously elusive: an imaging signature visible on a clinically available scan. SPECT is widely deployed in hospital nuclear medicine departments and costs roughly one-third of a research-grade functional MRI, making clinical adoption feasible.

The Yale team also performed serial scans on a subset of 87 patients over 18 months. Patients whose symptoms improved clinically showed corresponding normalization of perfusion, while those who remained symptomatic showed persistent or worsening deficits. This longitudinal correlation strengthens the case that the imaging finding reflects underlying pathophysiology rather than incidental variation.

Mechanistic Implications

The authors propose that the hypoperfusion likely reflects microvascular endothelial dysfunction, consistent with prior autopsy and biopsy work showing microclots and capillary rarefaction in Long COVID. Notably, patients with elevated von Willebrand factor and D-dimer levels at enrollment showed the most pronounced perfusion deficits, suggesting a thromboinflammatory mechanism amenable to targeted intervention.

Several ongoing trials—including the RECOVER-NEURO platform and a Mount Sinai antiplatelet study—are now incorporating SPECT as a secondary endpoint, allowing investigators to assess whether candidate therapies produce measurable changes in brain perfusion rather than relying solely on subjective cognitive scales.

Clinical and Disability Implications

Disability attorneys have already begun citing the Yale findings in Social Security Disability Insurance appeals, where adjudicators have historically discounted cognitive complaints lacking objective correlates. The American Academy of Neurology is reportedly drafting updated guidance on when SPECT is appropriate in suspected Long COVID, though the organization has cautioned against routine screening absent persistent, functionally significant symptoms.

Why this matters: After four years in which Long COVID brain fog has been one of the most disabling yet hardest-to-document symptoms of the condition, clinicians now have an objective, accessible imaging test that validates patient experience and creates a measurable target for therapeutic trials.

title: Ninth Circuit Holds Long-Term Disability Insurers Cannot Apply 24-Month Mental Illness Limitation to Long COVID Cognitive Symptoms
excerpt: In Hernandez v. Unum Life Insurance, the Ninth Circuit ruled that insurers cannot invoke the standard 24-month mental and nervous disorder limitation to terminate Long COVID disability benefits, finding that the condition's cognitive symptoms have demonstrated physical etiology.
body:
The Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals ruled last month in Hernandez v. Unum Life Insurance Company of America that long-term disability insurers cannot apply the standard 24-month "mental and nervous disorder" benefit limitation to claimants disabled by Long COVID, even when the disabling symptoms are primarily cognitive or psychiatric in presentation.

The decision, written by Judge Morgan Christen, resolves a question that has consumed ERISA litigation for the past three years as the earliest cohorts of Long COVID claimants approached the two-year mark on their policies. Most group LTD contracts limit benefits for conditions classified as mental, nervous, or psychiatric to 24 months, while physical conditions can be paid through age 65 or beyond.

The Underlying Dispute

Plaintiff Maria Hernandez, a 44-year-old radiology technician from Phoenix, contracted COVID-19 in late 2021 and developed persistent cognitive impairment, autonomic dysfunction, and post-exertional malaise. Unum paid benefits for 23 months before notifying her that her remaining symptoms—principally cognitive dysfunction, anxiety, and depression—fell within the policy's mental illness limitation and that payments would cease.

Hernandez submitted neurological workups documenting orthostatic intolerance on tilt-table testing, abnormal cerebral perfusion on SPECT, and elevated inflammatory markers. The district court sided with Unum on deferential ERISA review. The Ninth Circuit reversed.

The Court's Reasoning

Judge Christen wrote that the mental illness limitation, properly construed, applies to conditions whose etiology is psychiatric, not to conditions whose symptoms happen to manifest in mood or cognition. "A claimant with multiple sclerosis who develops depression secondary to demyelinating lesions is not subject to the mental illness limitation," the opinion states. "Neither is a claimant whose cognitive and affective symptoms arise from documented post-viral neuroinflammation."

The panel found that the scientific record on Long COVID—including persistent viral antigen findings, microvascular pathology, and reproducible immune dysregulation—established a physical basis sufficient to defeat the limitation as a matter of plan interpretation. The court rejected Unum's argument that the absence of a single diagnostic test rendered the etiology inherently psychiatric.

Industry Response

The ruling is binding in the Ninth Circuit and creates persuasive authority nationwide. Several major carriers, including Unum, Lincoln Financial, and Hartford, have collectively terminated benefits for an estimated 14,000 Long COVID claimants on mental illness grounds since 2023. Plaintiffs' attorneys are now preparing class certification motions and individual reinstatement claims.

The American Council of Life Insurers has signaled it may seek Supreme Court review or push for policy language amendments in future contracts. In the meantime, claims handlers at several carriers have reportedly paused mental-illness-limitation terminations involving Long COVID pending internal review.

Why this matters: Tens of thousands of Long COVID patients facing benefit cutoffs at the two-year mark now have a controlling federal appellate decision establishing that their disability is physical in nature and entitled to full policy benefits.

title: Phase 2b Trial of BC 007 Shows 52% Reduction in POTS Symptoms in Long COVID Patients With Autoantibodies to G-Protein-Coupled Receptors
excerpt: Berlin Cures announced positive Phase 2b results for BC 007, an aptamer that neutralizes autoantibodies targeting G-protein-coupled receptors, showing a 52% reduction in postural orthostatic tachycardia symptoms in Long COVID patients with confirmed autoantibody positivity.
body:
Berlin Cures announced this month that its Phase 2b trial of BC 007 met its primary endpoint, with Long COVID patients receiving the aptamer therapy showing a 52% reduction in postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) symptoms compared with 11% in the placebo arm at 12 weeks. The trial enrolled 248 patients across 22 sites in Germany, the Netherlands, and the United States.

BC 007 is a DNA aptamer that binds and neutralizes circulating autoantibodies directed against G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), particularly the beta-adrenergic and muscarinic receptors that regulate cardiovascular tone. Elevated GPCR autoantibodies have been identified in roughly 60–70% of Long COVID patients with significant autonomic dysfunction and are increasingly viewed as a driver of dysautonomia, exercise intolerance, and microvascular dysfunction.

Trial Design and Results

The trial, called reCOVer, enrolled patients who met established Long COVID criteria, had documented POTS on active stand or tilt-table testing, and were confirmed positive for at least one GPCR autoantibody on screening. Participants received a single intravenous infusion of BC 007 or matched placebo, with the option of a second infusion at week 8 for non-responders.

Beyond the primary POTS symptom endpoint, BC 007 produced significant improvements on the Composite Autonomic Symptom Score, the Chalder Fatigue Scale, and six-minute walk distance. Notably, 38% of treated patients moved from severe to mild functional impairment on the PEM-DSQ, suggesting effects extending beyond cardiovascular tone alone. Autoantibody titers dropped by an average of 64% within 48 hours of infusion and remained suppressed at 12 weeks in most responders.

Safety and Open Questions

The safety profile was favorable, with no serious treatment-related adverse events. Mild infusion reactions and transient headaches occurred in roughly 8% of participants. The most striking unknown is durability: about one-third of responders showed autoantibody rebound by week 20, suggesting that some patients may require periodic redosing.

The trial also raises selection questions. Patients negative for GPCR autoantibodies were excluded, and clinical autoantibody testing remains available at only a handful of specialty laboratories in Germany and the United States. Broad clinical deployment will require validated commercial assays and reimbursement pathways, neither of which currently exists in most U.S. health systems.

Regulatory Path

Berlin Cures has indicated it will seek a Phase 3 program in late 2026 with the goal of FDA and EMA submissions in 2028. The company is in discussions with the FDA about a possible breakthrough therapy designation given the absence of any approved disease-modifying treatment for Long COVID-associated dysautonomia.

Why this matters: BC 007 represents the first late-stage candidate to target a specific mechanistic subgroup of Long COVID with biomarker-confirmed selection, offering a credible path toward the first approved disease-modifying therapy for the condition.

title: Stanford-Led Trial Shows Low-Dose Naltrexone Reduces Post-Exertional Malaise in Long COVID by 38%
excerpt: A randomized controlled trial published in JAMA Internal Medicine in April 2026 found that low-dose naltrexone (LDN) significantly reduced post-exertional malaise and fatigue scores in long COVID patients over 16 weeks. The findings represent the first robust positive signal for a widely available, inexpensive repurposed drug in long COVID.
body:
Low-dose naltrexone reduced post-exertional malaise severity by 38% compared to placebo in a 312-patient randomized trial led by Stanford University and published in JAMA Internal Medicine in April 2026. The result is the first statistically robust positive finding from a placebo-controlled trial of a repurposed drug for the post-exertional malaise (PEM) phenotype that affects an estimated 60-70% of people with long COVID.

Participants, all of whom had documented PEM lasting at least 12 months after a confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection, were randomized to receive either 4.5 mg of naltrexone nightly or matching placebo for 16 weeks. The primary endpoint, change in the DePaul Symptom Questionnaire PEM subscore, improved by a mean of 4.1 points in the treatment arm versus 1.3 in the placebo arm. Secondary endpoints including the FACIT-Fatigue scale, cognitive function as measured by the Cogstate battery, and step-count tolerance on wearable devices all favored LDN, though cognitive improvements did not reach statistical significance.

Adverse events were modest. Vivid dreams and transient insomnia occurred in roughly 14% of the LDN group during the first two weeks, but discontinuation rates were nearly identical between arms at around 7%. No serious adverse events were attributed to the drug.

The proposed mechanism involves transient blockade of opioid receptors leading to upregulation of endogenous opioid signaling, alongside microglial inhibition and Toll-like receptor 4 antagonism — pathways implicated in the neuroinflammation hypothesis of long COVID. Lead investigator Dr. Hector Bonilla cautioned that while the effect size is clinically meaningful, LDN is not a cure and patients with severe ME/CFS-like presentations showed more modest benefit than those with predominantly fatigue-driven symptoms.

Implications for Off-Label Prescribing

LDN has been used off-label in long COVID clinics for several years based on anecdote and small open-label series, but insurance coverage and physician willingness to prescribe have varied widely. The compound is not patent-protected and costs roughly $30-50 per month when sourced from compounding pharmacies. Several major long COVID clinics, including those at Mount Sinai and UCSF, indicated they would update their treatment protocols based on the new data.

The American Academy of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation is expected to incorporate the findings into a forthcoming update of its long COVID consensus guidance later this year. A larger Phase 3 trial powered to detect cognitive and functional return-to-work endpoints is in planning at the NIH.

Why this matters: LDN now becomes the first repurposed pharmacotherapy with rigorous evidence of benefit for the most disabling feature of long COVID, giving clinicians a defensible, low-cost option while disease-modifying therapies remain in development.

title: EEOC Issues Enforcement Guidance Requiring Employers to Accommodate Cognitive Dysfunction in Long COVID
excerpt: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission released formal enforcement guidance in March 2026 directing employers to treat long COVID cognitive symptoms — including brain fog, processing delays, and working memory deficits — as presumptively covered impairments requiring reasonable accommodation. The guidance shifts the burden of proof in workplace disputes and is expected to drive a sharp increase in accommodation requests.
body:
The U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission issued formal enforcement guidance on March 18, 2026 establishing that cognitive impairments associated with long COVID — including diminished concentration, slowed information processing, and impaired working memory — are presumptively covered impairments under the Americans with Disabilities Act and the Rehabilitation Act. The guidance, EEOC-NVTA-2026-1, directs investigators to credit medically documented cognitive symptoms even when standard neuropsychological testing yields normal or borderline results.

The document instructs employers that common accommodations such as written instructions in lieu of verbal directives, extended deadlines, reduced meeting loads, flexible scheduling around symptom flares, quiet workspaces, and permission to use assistive software (including note-taking AI tools) must be considered reasonable absent specific evidence of undue hardship. Categorical denials based on job descriptions requiring "fast-paced" or "high-pressure" environments are flagged as likely violations.

Burden Shifting and Documentation

Significantly, the guidance addresses a recurring problem in long COVID disability litigation: the gap between patient-reported cognitive dysfunction and objective testing. Citing recent neuroimaging studies showing measurable changes in default mode network connectivity and cerebral hypometabolism in long COVID patients with normal MoCA scores, the EEOC states that employers and their medical reviewers may not require failed standardized cognitive tests as a precondition for accommodation. A treating physician's documentation of functional limitations is presumptively sufficient.

The guidance also clarifies that intermittent and episodic conditions — including the relapsing-remitting course typical of long COVID with post-exertional symptom exacerbation — qualify for accommodation even during periods of relative remission. Employers may not condition continued accommodation on continuous symptom presentation.

Industry Response

The Society for Human Resource Management issued a member alert the following week recommending that HR departments audit pending and denied long COVID accommodation requests from the past 24 months for potential exposure. Several employment defense firms predicted a wave of charge filings, noting that EEOC charge volume related to long COVID had already risen 41% year-over-year in 2025.

Disability rights advocates praised the guidance but cautioned that enforcement remains the central challenge. The National Employment Law Project estimated that fewer than 15% of workers with long COVID who needed accommodations had successfully obtained them under the previous case-by-case framework.

The guidance takes immediate effect for federal sector cases and applies to private-sector charges filed on or after April 1, 2026. It does not have the force of regulation but is entitled to substantial deference in federal court litigation.

Why this matters: By presumptively recognizing long COVID cognitive symptoms as ADA-covered impairments and rejecting test-score gatekeeping, the EEOC has materially lowered the barriers facing millions of workers attempting to remain employed while managing the condition's most stigmatized symptom cluster.

title: Nature Paper Identifies Persistent Spike Protein in Bone Marrow of Long COVID Patients 18 Months After Infection
excerpt: A collaborative study published in Nature in April 2026 detected intact SARS-CoV-2 spike protein and viral RNA in bone marrow biopsies from 71% of long COVID patients tested, with persistence documented up to 18 months after acute infection. The findings provide the strongest tissue-level evidence yet for the viral persistence hypothesis and identify a previously unrecognized reservoir.
body:
Researchers from the Karolinska Institute, Yale, and the University of Pennsylvania reported in Nature on April 9, 2026 that intact SARS-CoV-2 spike protein and viral subgenomic RNA were detected in the bone marrow of 71% of long COVID patients in a 94-person biopsy study, with the longest interval from acute infection being 18 months. None of the 28 matched recovered controls showed detectable viral material in bone marrow tissue.

The study used a combination of ultra-sensitive single-molecule array (Simoa) immunoassays, RNAscope in situ hybridization, and spatial transcriptomics on iliac crest biopsies. Spike protein was localized predominantly to CD14+ monocyte-lineage cells and a subset of stromal cells. Critically, the presence of subgenomic RNA — which is produced only during active viral replication and not stably preserved from initial infection — suggests ongoing, low-level viral activity rather than inert remnants.

Linking Reservoir to Symptoms

Spike protein burden in bone marrow correlated significantly with several clinical measures: serum interleukin-6 levels (r=0.58), D-dimer (r=0.44), and patient-reported fatigue and PEM scores. Patients with detectable bone marrow spike protein also showed altered hematopoietic progenitor populations, with an expansion of inflammatory monocyte precursors and reduced lymphoid output — a pattern that may help explain the persistent immune dysregulation and lymphopenia observed in many long COVID patients.

The bone marrow finding is particularly significant because it identifies a hematopoietic reservoir capable of continuously seeding circulating immune cells with viral antigen, providing a mechanistic explanation for systemic and fluctuating symptoms. Previous reservoir studies had focused primarily on gut tissue, lymph nodes, and the central nervous system.

Therapeutic Implications

The authors note that bone marrow is poorly penetrated by some antiviral agents, including nirmatrelvir, potentially explaining the disappointing results of extended Paxlovid trials. They argue the findings support prioritization of monoclonal antibody combinations, longer-acting protease inhibitors, and emerging nucleoside analogs with better tissue distribution. Two industry-sponsored trials — one of a long-acting spike-targeted monoclonal (AER-801) and one of obeldesivir in 12-week dosing — are now actively recruiting patients with biomarker evidence of persistence.

An accompanying editorial in Nature called the findings "the inflection point the field has needed," while urging caution that biopsy-based diagnosis is impractical for clinical use and that validated blood-based surrogate markers must be developed. The Polybio Research Foundation, which partially funded the work, announced a $14 million follow-on initiative to develop such assays.

Why this matters: By documenting an actively replicating viral reservoir in bone marrow more than a year after infection, the study reframes long COVID from a post-infectious syndrome into, for many patients, an ongoing infection — fundamentally reshaping the therapeutic agenda.

title: NIH RECOVER-VITAL Trial Finds 25-Day Paxlovid Course Fails to Resolve Long COVID Symptoms
excerpt: Final results from the NIH's RECOVER-VITAL trial, published in JAMA in April 2026, show that a 25-day course of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir produced no clinically meaningful improvement in long COVID symptoms compared with placebo.
body:
The long-awaited final results from the NIH's RECOVER-VITAL trial, published in JAMA in April 2026, show that a 25-day course of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid) produced no clinically meaningful improvement in long COVID symptoms compared with placebo. The findings dampen one of the more prominent hypotheses in the field — that viral persistence drives chronic symptoms and can be reversed with extended antiviral therapy.

The Phase 2 trial randomized 902 participants with moderate-to-severe long COVID across 24 U.S. sites between 2023 and 2025. Investigators measured changes in the PROMIS-29 symptom score, fatigue, cognitive function, and post-exertional malaise at 10 weeks. Participants in the Paxlovid arm reported a 1.3-point improvement on the primary symptom index — statistically indistinguishable from the 1.1-point improvement in the placebo group. Subgroup analyses by sex, time since infection, vaccination status, and baseline symptom severity all failed to identify a responder population.

What the Results Do and Don't Rule Out

Trial lead Dr. Kanecia Zimmerman of Duke cautioned that the negative result applies specifically to a 25-day oral antiviral regimen and does not disprove viral persistence as a mechanism. Tissue-reservoir studies, including the Patterson lab's spike protein detection work and the UCSF PET imaging program, continue to find SARS-CoV-2 antigens in gut and lymphoid tissue months after infection. Critics of RECOVER-VITAL note that nirmatrelvir penetrates such reservoirs poorly and that a 25-day course may be too brief to clear tissue-bound virus.

Several follow-on trials are already underway. The Polybio Research Foundation's PAX LC extension is testing a 15-day course combined with monoclonal antibodies, and a Stanford-led study is evaluating ensitrelvir, a Japanese-approved antiviral with better tissue penetration. Results from both are expected in late 2026.

Patient and Clinician Reaction

Patient advocacy groups expressed disappointment but not surprise. The Long COVID Alliance noted that many clinicians had already moved away from off-label extended Paxlovid prescribing after preliminary 2024 readouts suggested a weak effect. Some long COVID clinics, including those at Mount Sinai and UCLA, had quietly stopped offering the regimen last year.

The negative trial also intensifies pressure on RECOVER, which has spent over $1.6 billion since 2021 and has been criticized for slow enrollment and a focus on observational rather than interventional research. House appropriators have asked NIH to submit a revised research roadmap by July 2026, with explicit benchmarks for therapeutic trials in immunomodulation, microclot dissolution, and autonomic dysfunction.

Why this matters: With antivirals now off the table as a stand-alone fix, the search for effective long COVID treatments shifts decisively toward immune-modulating and reservoir-targeting strategies that have yet to reach late-phase trials.

title: Fourth Circuit Rules Long COVID Qualifies as ADA Disability Without Need for Individualized Severity Showing
excerpt: In a March 2026 en banc decision in Alvarez v. Sentara Healthcare, the Fourth Circuit held that long COVID categorically substantially limits major life activities under the ADA Amendments Act, removing a key evidentiary hurdle for workers seeking accommodations.
body:
In a March 2026 en banc decision in Alvarez v. Sentara Healthcare, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit held that long COVID categorically substantially limits one or more major life activities under the ADA Amendments Act, removing a key evidentiary hurdle for workers seeking workplace accommodations. The 10-5 ruling is the first appellate decision to adopt a per se approach to long COVID disability status.

The case involved Maria Alvarez, a surgical nurse in Norfolk, Virginia, who developed post-exertional malaise, tachycardia, and cognitive impairment after a 2022 infection. Sentara denied her request for a non-bedside reassignment, arguing she had not provided sufficient objective evidence that her impairments rose to the level of substantial limitation. A district court agreed. The Fourth Circuit, sitting en banc, reversed.

The Court's Reasoning

Writing for the majority, Judge Toby Heytens leaned heavily on the 2008 ADAAA's instruction that courts should construe disability "in favor of broad coverage" and on HHS and DOJ guidance from 2021 identifying long COVID as a potential disability. The court found that the diagnostic criteria for long COVID — which require functional impairment lasting at least three months — inherently establish substantial limitation as a matter of law. Plaintiffs must still prove they have the condition and that the requested accommodation is reasonable, but they need not separately litigate the severity prong.

The dissent, led by Judge Julius Richardson, warned the ruling effectively creates a new protected class by judicial fiat and will encourage litigation over contested diagnoses.

Practical Impact

The decision binds federal courts in Maryland, Virginia, West Virginia, North and South Carolina, but employment lawyers expect rapid influence elsewhere. The Second and Ninth Circuits are considering similar questions in pending cases. The EEOC, which filed an amicus brief supporting Alvarez, issued updated technical guidance the week after the ruling reaffirming that employers should presume long COVID is a covered disability when supported by a clinician's diagnosis.

Employer-side firms have advised clients to revise interactive-process procedures, focusing disputes on accommodation feasibility rather than disability status. The ruling also has implications outside employment: Title II and Title III claims involving school accommodations and public accommodations are expected to benefit from the same framework.

Disability rights groups, including the Center for Public Representation and Long COVID Justice, called the decision the most significant ADA development for the post-acute infection community since the pandemic began. They are now pushing the Social Security Administration to adopt parallel reasoning for SSDI determinations, where denial rates for long COVID claims remain above 70 percent.

Why this matters: Millions of workers with long COVID now have a clearer legal pathway to job accommodations in much of the country, shifting the burden of proof from patients to employers.

title: CMS Finalizes ICD-10 Long COVID Subtype Codes and New Outpatient Bundle Effective October 2026
excerpt: CMS published final rules in April 2026 creating five new ICD-10-CM subtype codes for long COVID and establishing a dedicated outpatient payment bundle for multidisciplinary post-COVID care, both effective October 1.
body:
The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services published final rules in April 2026 creating five new ICD-10-CM subtype codes for long COVID and establishing a dedicated outpatient payment bundle for multidisciplinary post-COVID care, both effective October 1, 2026. The changes mark the most substantial federal billing infrastructure ever built for the condition.

The New Codes

The existing U09.9 code, in use since 2021, has been criticized by clinicians and researchers as too vague to support either clinical care or epidemiological tracking. The new structure retains U09.9 as a parent code and adds subtypes for the predominant phenotype: U09.1 (cardiovascular/dysautonomia), U09.2 (cognitive/neurological), U09.3 (post-exertional malaise/ME-CFS-like), U09.4 (respiratory), and U09.5 (multisystem inflammatory). Clinicians may code more than one subtype, and CMS is permitting a transition year during which U09.9 alone remains acceptable.

The codes were developed in consultation with the CDC's National Center for Health Statistics, the American Academy of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation, and patient organizations including Body Politic and #MEAction. The subtype framework draws on the 2024 NASEM definition of long COVID and the RECOVER consortium's symptom-cluster analyses.

The Outpatient Bundle

More consequential for access to care, CMS finalized HCPCS code G2213, a monthly bundled payment of approximately $312 for outpatient long COVID management. The bundle covers care coordination, symptom monitoring, and integration of services across at least three specialties (commonly physiatry, cardiology, neurology, pulmonology, or rehabilitation therapy). It does not require an in-person visit each month, accommodating patients whose post-exertional malaise limits travel.

The bundle resolves a longstanding billing problem: long COVID care is inherently multidisciplinary, but fee-for-service codes poorly reimburse the cross-specialty coordination that defines effective post-COVID clinics. Several academic centers, including Mount Sinai, Stanford, and Johns Hopkins, had been operating their long COVID programs at a loss or relying on philanthropy. The bundled rate is expected to make these programs financially sustainable and encourage replication in community settings.

Reaction

The American Medical Association and the Society of Long COVID Physicians (formed in 2024) endorsed the changes. Some patient advocates raised concerns that the subtype system could lead to under-coding of patients with diffuse or shifting symptoms, and that the bundle's three-specialty threshold may exclude rural patients without local specialist access. CMS has committed to a review of utilization patterns in 2027.

Private insurers are expected to follow Medicare's lead, as is typical for new HCPCS codes. Several state Medicaid programs, including California and New York, have already indicated they will adopt parallel coverage.

Why this matters: For the first time, the U.S. health system has financial and diagnostic infrastructure built specifically for long COVID, making sustained specialty care viable for clinics and accessible to patients who have struggled to find it.
